title: Development of a Smart Warehouse Logistics Virtual Reality (VR) Simulator for Experiential Learning in Logistics Education
authors: Apinanchai Yooyativong, Taksakorn Sittiwong, Teeravisit Laohapensaeng, Thongchai Yooyativong
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: The present logistics and supply chain workforce needs staff members who can perform operational tasks while possessing digital technology skills. The current warehouse operation training methods encounter multiple barriers because they require expensive equipment and present safety hazards and insufficient adaptable training spaces. The authors present a Smart Warehouse Logistics Virtual Reality (VR) Simulator, which they developed to enhance practical learning experiences for warehouse operation training. The system duplicates complete logistics operations from receiving to shipping through sorting and error resolution in a physics-based 3D environment built with the Meta Quest 3 VR headset and Unity Engine and C# programming. The system enables learners to practice realistic warehouse operations through its combination of automated conveyor and sorting logic and forklift dynamics and error-handling mechanisms. The system architecture contains three separate layers (Input and Logic Core and Output) which allows for expansion and future connection to Industry 4.0 systems and digital twin platforms. The evaluation study employed a mixed-methods research design to evaluate system usability and student learning outcomes through a study with 32 undergraduate logistics students. The usability assessment through SUS reached 86.2, while participants demonstrated 48.7% better knowledge retention and their process errors decreased by 63.6%. The research participants gave feedback which showed their work engagement improved while they achieved better safety standards and operational performance.

title: SqueezeNet-Based Extraction of Compact Feature Vectors from Inspection Video Streams
authors: Oksana Karpinska, Lesia Dubchak, Anatoliy Sachenko, Oleg Zastavnyy, Bohdan Derysh, Ihor Romanets
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: Automated visual diagnostics of renewable energy assets require efficient transformation of inspection video streams into compact and informative representations. Raw inspection videos of wind turbine blades contain a large number of redundant frames, while surface defects such as cracks, erosion, and corrosion occur only locally. Direct pixel-level processing of such data is computationally inefficient for streaming applications. This paper presents a SqueezeNet-based software module for processing inspection video streams and extracting compact four-dimensional class-confidence vectors. The proposed approach performs frame sampling, standardized image preprocessing, and convolutional feature extraction, followed by a lightweight classification head that produces 4D probability-based feature representations. The focus is placed on feature compactness and temporal stability rather than classification accuracy alone. Experimental results obtained on real inspection video data demonstrate clear class separability in the representation space and high temporal consistency, with an average cosine similarity of 0.988 between consecutive frames. The proposed representation is well-suited for integration into intelligent diagnostic systems operating in real-time or near-real-time conditions.

title: RectLU: A Novel Activation Function Based on a Rectangle
authors: Thawatchai Chomsiri, Preecha Noiumkar
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This paper introduces RectLU (Rectangle Linear Unit), a novel activation function inspired by the geometry of rectangles. Its design begins by deriving a function representing the area of a rectangle with a constant diagonal slope (the ratio of its height to width). This area function is then multiplied by the input <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex> to form the core of RectLU's central segment. RectLU is structured into three distinct regions: a left segment returning 0, a right segment returning <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex>, and the aforementioned central function derived from the rectangle. We conducted experiments on two datasets: MNIST and CIFAR-100. For the MNIST experiments, a small Neural Network model with a limited number of hidden layers and nodes per layer was employed. Conversely, for the CIFAR-100 experiments, which involve a large number of classes, a larger and more complex model incorporating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) was utilized. Experimental results reveal that RectLUs with a smaller diagonal slope are suitable for compact Neural Network models, while those with a larger diagonal slope are advantageous for extensive models, datasets with many classes, and CNN architectures. We designed RectLU with a single adjustable parameter: the diagonal slope (m). Furthermore, we performed comparative experiments against other standard activation functions, including GELU, Swish, ELU, and ReLU. The experimental findings indicate that when the slope parameter m is appropriately tuned to the model, RectLU consistently outperforms these other activation functions.

title: Unraveling the Degradation Mechanisms of AlGaN-Based UVC-LEDs: A Comprehensive Analysis
authors: Pengyan Wen, Yu He, Meixin Feng, Jiahao Dong, Xinbo Zou, Jiaxiang Chen, Haolan Qu, Xiujian Sun, Qian Sun, Hui Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: In the fields of medical diagnostics, virus inactivation, and air/water purification, ultraviolet-C light-emitting diodes (UVC-LEDs) have emerged as pivotal light sources, attracting substantial attention in the post-COVID-19 era. Nevertheless, their long-term reliability remains a critical area requiring further optimization. This study focuses on the degradation mechanism of AlGaN-based UVC-LEDs. Experimental results demonstrate that the aged devices exhibit a notable increase in both turn-on voltage and ideality factor. Concurrently, their electroluminescence (EL) characteristics reveal a redshift in peak emission wavelength and a significant reduction in luminescence intensity. By integrating noise analysis and cathodoluminescence (CL) characterization, we conclude that defect-related nonradiative recombination centers were generated in the n-AlGaN layer beneath the quantum wells (QWs), and these centers facilitate defect-mediated carrier tunneling, contributing to the observed performance degradation. This research provides valuable insights to guide the subsequent design and development of high-reliability UVC-LEDs.
