title: Automated Human Activity Recognition Using Multiple Contactless Ultra-Wideband Radar Signals and Time–Frequency Representations
authors: Swarubini P J, Nagarajan Ganapathy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Radar signals-based human activity recognition (HAR) is a promising contactless approach for healthcare. However, these signals are highly nonstationary, exhibiting short-duration and dynamic variations that are difficult to discriminate. In recent studies, time–frequency representations (TFRs) are employed to capture both temporal and spatial information. Although TFRs are employed to capture the temporal-spatial dynamics of signals, TFR-based radar micro-Doppler signatures are less explored. In addition, TFR-based machine learning methods can provide improved feature learning. In this study, we attempt to analyze high-resolution TFRs namely, mel-spectrogram (Mel), smoothed pseudo Wigner–Ville distribution (SPWVD), short-time Fourier transform (STFT) and superlet transform (SLT) to explore the dynamics of radar signals for HAR. For this, a publicly available radar database (N=15 subjects) consisting of nine different activities are considered. The radar signals are preprocessed and segmented according to the nine activities. These radar segments are subjected to STFT, Mel, SPWVD and SLT. Ten features are extracted from each TFR and employed to various classification algorithms, namely support vector machine (SVM), k-nearest neighbours (kNN), decision tree (DT), random forest (RF), logistic regression (LR), XGBoost (XGB), AdaBoost (ADB), light gradient boosting machine (LGBM), CatBoost (CatB), and MobileNetV2. Extensive experiments are conducted for five-activity (stationary, translation, in-place movements, fall, standing up) and three-activity recognition tasks (mobility, in-place and fall) and evaluation metrics are obtained. The results show that SPWVD-based TFR performed best among all the TFRs. The combination of SPWVD-MobileNetV2 provides the highest overall effectiveness, obtaining an F1-macro (83.90%) and balanced accuracy (84.60%) for the three-class task and 69.09% and 70.15% for five-class task. The SPWVD–CatB combination achieved comparable performance to deep learning, with an F1-macro of 79.76% and a Matthews correlation coefficient of 66.53% for the three-class task. The findings demonstrate that SPWVD, combined with MobileNetV2, yields optimal performance across multiple activity classes suitable for deployment on mobile edge devices. These results indicate that the proposed approach could be extended to clinical and real-world monitoring.

title: QIMarker: Can Watermark Embedding Improve Image Quality?
authors: Chuan Qin, Zixiang Wei, Na Wang, Fang Cao, Ching-Chun Chang, Xinpeng Zhang, Chin-Chen Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Robust image watermarking plays a crucial role in copyright protection and digital content authentication. However, most existing methods inevitably introduce visual artifacts and structural distortions, leading to noticeable degradation in image quality. To address these limitations, this paper introduces QIMarker, an image enhancement–guided multi-task learning framework for robust image watermarking. Unlike existing methods that minimize distortion with respect to the original image, QIMarker redefines the optimization objective by aligning the watermarked image with its enhanced counterpart, thereby transforming watermark embedding from a quality-degrading process into a quality-promoting one. Specifically, it integrates a U-Net–based embedding network enhanced with attention mechanisms and a dedicated image enhancement branch for joint feature optimization. This design allows the watermark embedding to contribute positively to image reconstruction, yielding perceptually refined outcomes while maintaining high robustness. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of QIMarker in both visual quality and robustness. It improves structural similarity (SSIM) by up to 0.13 compared with existing methods, and achieves watermark extraction accuracy above 97% under common attacks such as noise addition, compression, scaling, and rotation.

title: Drift-Resilient Hybrid Feature Learning Framework for Accurate Mixed Industrial Gas Classification Using Chemical Sensor Arrays
authors: Ghazala Ansari, Rupali Singh, Sachin Kumar, Ravi Kumar
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The increased demand for reliable gas sensing in factory systems has prompted the development of smart sensory systems capable of overcoming errors and signal distortion caused by environmental changes. This paper introduces drift-resistant hybrid feature learning to classify mixed industrial gases, specifically the ethylene-carbon monoxide (CH-CO) and ethylene-methane (CH-CH) mixtures with the help of metal-oxide semiconductor (MOX) sensor arrays. The suggested framework combines both the statistical descriptors, which include the steady-state signal behavior, and the deep temporal-spatial embedding, which is trained by a one-dimensional CNN-GRU architecture. To generate these complimentary representations, a single feature space is produced, which is more interpretable and resilient. Optuna was used to conduct Bayesian search on the models hyperparameters and Extra Trees and LightGBM ensembles were used to conduct classification. The results of the experiments carried out on 180 time-series recordings with labels proved that the fused model attained average accuracies of more than 90% and best fold accuracies of 99% and surpassed a number of advanced machine learning and deep learning baselines. Synthetic baseline drift (0.8-1.4) was also used to test long-term reliability and the framework retained accuracies greater than 96% at all levels of drift. Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) also shed some light by determining kurtosis, skewness, and mid-frequency temporal response as the main discriminative sensor channels. The findings verify that the suggested framework provides a computationally efficient, interpretable and drift-resilient solution that can be used in real-time in an industrial context. In addition to gas analysis, the hybrid approach demonstrates a high potential of more general applications related to non-stationary multivariate sensor data, that is, environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics, and process control systems.

title: Quizzing: Exploring the comprehensive benefits of quizzing in college classrooms

title: Interactive Force-Impedance Control
authors: Fan Shao, Satoshi Endo, Sandra Hirche, Fanny Ficuciello
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Human collaboration with robots requires flexible role adaptation, enabling the robot to switch between an active leader and a passive follower. Effective role switching depends on accurately estimating human intentions, which is typically achieved through external force analysis, nominal robot dynamics, or data-driven approaches. However, these methods are primarily effective in contact-sparse environments. When robots under hybrid or unified force–impedance control physically interact with active humans or non-passive environments, the robotic system may lose passivity and thus compromise safety. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a unified Interactive Force-Impedance Control (IFIC) framework that adapts to interaction power flow, ensuring safe and effortless interaction in contact-rich environments. The proposed control architecture is formulated within a port-Hamiltonian framework, incorporating both interaction and task control ports, thereby guaranteeing autonomous system passivity. Experiments in both rigid and soft contact scenarios demonstrate that IFIC ensures stable collaboration under active human interaction, reduces contact impact forces and interaction force oscillations.

title: An Open-Source, Reproducible Tensegrity Robot that can Navigate Among Obstacles
authors: William R. Johnson, Patrick Meng, Nelson Chen, Luca Cimatti, Augustin Vercoutere, Mridul Aanjaneya, Rebecca Kramer-Bottiglio, Kostas E. Bekris
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Tensegrity robots, composed of rigid struts and elastic tendons, provide impact resistance, low mass, and adaptability to unstructured terrain. Their compliance and complex, coupled dynamics, however, present modeling and control challenges, hindering planning and obstacle avoidance. This paper presents a complete, open-source, and reproducible system that enables navigation for a 3-bar tensegrity robot. The system comprises: (i) an inexpensive, open-source hardware design, and (ii) an integrated, open-source software stack for physics-based modeling, system identification, state estimation, path planning, and control. All hardware and software are publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://tensegrity.yale.edu/</uri> tensegrity.yale.edu. The proposed system tracks the robot using a static overhead camera and executes collision-free paths to a goal among known obstacle locations. System robustness is demonstrated through experiments involving unmodeled environmental challenges, including a vertical drop, an incline, and granular media, culminating in an outdoor field demonstration. To validate reproducibility, experiments were conducted using robot instances at two different laboratories. This work provides the robotics community with a complete navigation system for a compliant, impact-resistant, and shape-morphing robot. This system is intended to serve as a springboard for advancing the navigation capabilities of other unconventional robotic platforms.

title: Model-predictive Vibration Reduction of a Suspended Cable-Driven Parallel Robot
authors: Sophie Rousseau, Nicolò Pedemonte, François Chaumette, Stéphane Caro
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: This letter focuses on the vibration reduction for Cable-Driven Parallel Robots. A Non-linear Model Predictive Controller (NMPC) is designed and implemented to adapt the control inputs sent to the robot actuators. This NMPC is based on an elasto-dynamic model of the robot, which is discretized and included in a constrained optimal control problem. Two experiments are conducted and the performance of the NMPC is compared with a Computed Torque Controller and a NMPC with an inelastic model. The first one is a trajectory following task and shows that the proposed controller significantly reduces the amplitude of the vibrations of the Moving-Platform along the trajectory and the second one focuses on a sudden braking situation and shows that the proposed control scheme manages to divide the amplitude of the post-braking oscillations by factors ranging from one and a half to twenty-two, demonstrating the interest of this controller for perturbation rejection.

title: A Kind of Flexible Forceps With High Clamping Force and Large Opening Angle for Endoscopic Surgery
authors: Yuan Xing, Fengchuan Liang, Mengjie Chen, Kang Kong, Tao Liang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: In endoscopic submucosal dissection, endoscopic forceps is subject to stringent requirements regarding flexibility, miniaturization, and mechanical performance. However, most existing endoscopic forceps suffer from insufficient clamping force and limited opening angle. In this letter, we analyze the influence of the jaw configuration on the clamping force and opening angle of flexible forceps, and develop an innovative composite-groove jaw configuration that achieves a high clamping force of 2.57 N and a large opening angle of 105°, while maintaining a compact outer diameter of 2.8 mm. Additionally, the forceps provides 360° independent distal rotation, allowing flexible orientation under bent configurations. The kinematic model of the forceps, established based on screw theory, defines a workspace that approximates a hemisphere with a radius of 15 mm. Its modular design facilitates rapid instrument replacement during surgery. The ex vivo experiment on a fresh porcine stomach confirms its ability to realize effective tissue traction and surgical field exposure during endoscopic submucosal dissection, demonstrating its potential to improve efficiency and safety in endoscopic surgery.

title: A Pneumatically Actuated Robotic Assistant for MRI-Guided Stereotactic Neurosurgery
authors: Gang Li, Atharva Paralikar, Pavel Yarmolenko, Ayush Nankani, Wolfgang Loew, Charles Dumoulin, Kevin Cleary, Chima Oluigbo, Reza Monfaredi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: This paper presents the development and cadaveric evaluation of a pneumatically actuated robotic assistant for MRI-guided stereotactic neurosurgery. The robot features two coaxial arcs that provide four degrees of freedom to position and orient a needle within an MRI scanner. It is driven by custom-designed MR-Safe pneumatic stepper motors and powered by in-room compressed medical air, enabling precise interventions under intraoperative MRI guidance. To improve anatomical visualization, two custom MR imaging coils were developed and integrated with the system. A dedicated clinical workflow was also proposed for robot-assisted stereotactic procedures under MRI-guidance. Targeting accuracy was first assessed in free space using an optical tracking system, yielding mean absolute errors of 0.97 ± 0.42 mm for tip position and 0.46 ± 0.23° for needle angle. System-level accuracy was then evaluated in MRI-guided phantom studies, showing errors of 1.38 ± 0.80 mm in tip position and 1.08 ± 0.67° in angle. Finally, a cadaver study validated the clinical workflow, with observed errors of 2.11 mm in position and 3.32° in angle. Quantitative imaging analysis confirmed minimal image degradation, with signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) variation below 12.26% and 2D geometric distortion under 0.20%.

title: A promising movement-based prosthesis control tested on valid and arm-amputated participants using first-person teleoperation of humanoid robot Reachy
authors: Vincent Leconte, Effie Segas, Bianca Lento, Emilie Doat, Etienne Guillaud, Matthieu Lapeyre, Pierre Rouanet, Gaëlle Lannuzel, Antoine Pirrone, Rémi Klotz, Daniel Cattaert, Aymar De Rugy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: We showed recently that a novel prosthesis control, based on natural arm movement reconstructed from an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) that receives movement goal and stump motion as inputs, enables close-to-natural reaching control in Virtual Reality (VR). Here, we applied and tested this control in the physical world using first-person teleoperation of Reachy2, an open-source humanoid robot made by Pollen Robotics that ranked 2nd at ANA Avatar XPRIZE. Direct teleoperation reproducing all arm joints of able-bodied participants (n=14) elicited a 100% success rate for grasping objects at various locations, with best possible usability scores and small workload scores. High success rates (>92%) were also obtained when all distal joints from the elbow onward were operated with the novel prosthesis control, using a movement goal identified through gaze-guided computer vision. While usability and workload scores were slightly degraded when able-bodied participants used the prosthesis control as compared to direct teleoperation, both were scored similarly well by a sample from our target population (n=8 participants with transhumeral limb loss). This platform and control schemes offer broad perspectives for prosthesis control, but also for teleoperation robotics and human-robot interactions.

title: Two-axis transparent force plate for droplet collision measurement using high speed line-scan camera and sampling moiré method
authors: Yukitake Nakahara, Toshihiro Shiratori, Choongyeop Lee, YunJung Heo, Hidetoshi Takahashi
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Force measurement and interface observation during droplet impact are essential for understanding droplet behavior in both scientific and industrial applications. However, achieving both backside optical transparency for interface observation and two-axis force measurement for quantifying shear-force effects has been challenging with conventional force-sensing methods using a single device. In this study, we propose a transparent two-axis force plate based on the sampling moiré (SM) method combined with a line-scan camera, which enables high-temporal-resolution force measurement while permitting optical observation beneath the impacted surface. The proposed device consists of a spring-supported, laser-cut glass plate with a periodic striped pattern and a prism-based optical system. A line-scan camera is employed to overcome the inherent trade-off between spatial resolution and frame rate associated with area-scan cameras. By duplicating the pattern image using a prism and analyzing the resulting displacements via the SM method, in-plane and out-of-plane forces are independently measured from one-dimensional images. Calibration experiments confirm linear force–displacement characteristics, with force resolutions of Fx = 0.39 mN and Fz =0.12 mN, respectively. Positional errors remained below 2% in-plane and 6% out-of-plane. The fabricated device exhibits a minimum resonant frequency of 656 Hz, which is sufficient to capture short-duration impact events. Finally, droplet impact experiments are conducted under both vertical and tilted conditions. Compared with the vertical configuration, the inclined condition demonstrates Fx values comparable in magnitude to Fz and a clear elongation of the droplet induced by shear forces. Overall, the proposed force plate provides a customizable platform for high-precision, high-speed force measurements in droplet dynamics.

title: A Generic Construction of q -ary Near-MDS Codes Supporting 2-Designs with Lengths Beyond q + 1
authors: Hengfeng Liu, Chunming Tang, Zhengchun Zhou, Dongchun Han, Hao Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: A linear code with parameters [n, k, n – k + 1] is called maximum distance separable (MDS), and one with parameters [n; k; n – k] is called almost MDS (AMDS). A code is near-MDS (NMDS) if both it and its dual are AMDS. NMDS codes supporting combinatorial t-designs have attracted growing interest, yet constructing such codes remains highly challenging. In 2020, Ding and Tang initiated the study of NMDS codes supporting 2-designs by constructing the first infinite family, followed by several other constructions for t > 2, all with length at most q + 1. Although NMDS codes can, in principle, exceed this length, known examples supporting 2-designs and having length greater than q + 1 are extremely rare and limited to a few sporadic binary and ternary cases. In this paper, we present the first generic construction of q-ary NMDS codes supporting 2-designs with lengths exceeding q+1. Our method leverages new connections between elliptic curve codes, finite abelian groups, subset sums, and combinatorial designs, resulting in an infinite family of such codes along with their weight distributions.

title: Physics-Guided Multistage Segmentation for Structural Characterization of TRISO Fuel Particles in X-ray CT Imaging
authors: Ping Yang, Songyuan Li, Ligen Shi, Jigang Duan, Genwei Ma, Xing Zhao, Xiangang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science
abstract: Accurate structural analysis of coated fuel particles is essential for assessing the performance and quality of advanced nuclear fuels. X-ray computed tomography (CT) provides a non-destructive means to visualize the multilayer structure of TRISO fuel particles. However, accurate layer segmentation remains challenging due to low interlayer contrast, imaging noise, and limited labeled data. This paper proposes a physics-guided multistage segmentation network (PMSNet) for automatic multilayer segmentation of TRISO fuel particle CT images. PMSNet integrates prior knowledge of the particle’s spherical geometry and coating hierarchy into a deep learning framework, and consists of three key components: axial-aware position encoding with layer-specific spatial priors, a multiscale perturbation strategy to enhance generalization, and a prior-guided conditional fusion mechanism to enforce interlayer consistency. Experiments on synthetic and real TRISO CT datasets demonstrate that PMSNet achieves mean Dice coefficients above 0.98 and mean IoU values exceeding 0.97 across multiple coating layers. Compared with state-of-the-art methods, PMSNet improves Dice scores by 3–8 percentage points on challenging layers and achieves boundary F1 scores close to 1.0 for key interfaces. While the primary focus of this study is TRISO fuel particle segmentation, additional evaluations are performed to examine the general applicability of the proposed framework. Results on medical CT datasets further confirm its robustness and adaptability to radiation-based imaging tasks. The proposed method contributes a physics-based AI segmentation framework, enables accurate and fully automatic multilayer segmentation of TRISO fuel particles, and provides a practical solution for more efficient and reliable non-destructive nuclear fuel inspection.

title: Descriptor: Solar and Kinetic Energy Harvesting Dataset for Human Activity Recognition (SKEH-HAR)
authors: Moid Sandhu, Sara Khalifa, Raja Jurdak, Marius Portmann, Brano Kusy
publish: IEEE Data Descriptions
abstract: This paper presents the multimodal Solar and Kinetic Energy Harvesting dataset for Human Activity Recognition (SKEH-HAR), collected to support research in self-powered activity recognition and wearable systems. The dataset was collected using custom-built wearable devices equipped with solar cells and kinetic energy harvesters, alongside accelerometer sensors. It includes data from 40 adult and healthy participants performing 5 common daily activities, including walking, running, sitting, standing, and going upstairs/downstairs, in both indoor and outdoor environments. The SKEH-HAR dataset captures energy harvesting signals across diverse light and motion conditions, enabling not only the analysis of energy availability but also supporting HAR based on energy harvested signals. This unique combination enables novel investigations into sustainable HAR systems and adaptive sensing strategies based on energy availability. The dataset is expected to serve as a valuable resource for research community working on activity recognition, low-power and batteryless sensing, and self-powered wearable technologies.

title: Signal Processing Techniques for Enhanced Performance of FMCW Radar Altimeter
authors: Reshma Sreekumar, Sreelal Sreedharan Pillai, Vani Devi Mani
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: In aerospace navigation, the Radar Altimeter plays an indispensable role in ensuring the navigation accuracy and safety of the aerial vehicle. In this work, we present signal processing techniques that can be implemented on an FMCW Radar Altimeter (RA) to offer better accuracy in altitude estimation. Accuracy and resolution play a crucial role in guaranteeing the safety of aerial vehicles, especially during the landing phase. Altitude in FMCW-RA is computed by estimating the frequency of the beat signal obtained by mixing the transmitted and received FMCW radar signal. A signal processing algorithm based on the Chirp Z transform (CZT) with interbin interpolation for improved accuracy of beat frequency estimation, along with a pre-processing stage to compensate for the performance degrading factors like random noise, is presented here. In this study, we validate the effectiveness of this algorithm using C-band RA and an altitude simulator ALT8000 and the performance is quantified in terms of Root Mean Square Error (RMSE).

title: Retraction Notice: Lifetime Enhancement Using Sleep Switch Over In Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: A. Karthikeyan, M. Aravinth, K.S. Hamshini, M. Indumathi
publish: 2019 5th International Conference on Advanced Computing & Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Expanding the endurance of the system and using the equity to its greatest utmost is the serious issue in Wireless Sensor Network (WSN). The remote sensor hubs in sensor establish are fueled utilizing battery-mechanized batteries. Nonetheless, giving vitality to hubs in the inaccessible condition is a noteworthy issue in WSN. Consequently WSN needs another vitality effective calculation to upgrade the system existence. In a sensor hub, the sending and accepting module expends more vitality when contrasted with different modules. In this paper, a Battery Restoration based Lifetime Endurance (BRLE) calculation is talked about, which considers battery voltage bend for planning the sending and receiving module of the sensor hubs.The Markov display assist in deciding the condition of the sensor hub as Cluster head and Cluster Member dependent on battery recuperation process. By booking the sending and receiving module dependent on the battery terminal voltage, recuperation factor and separation between the hubs, the lifetime of the system is upgraded.Trial execution demonstrate that the calculation beats the others by expanded lifetime and expanded throughput. The BRLE diminishes the difficulty and vitality gap issue, keeping away from misfortune in network with the sink.

title: Tag-Along Virtual Windows Increase Perceived Resistance and Task Load in Augmented Reality
authors: Motoki Kagami, Yuta Kataoka, Yutaro Hirao, Monica Perusquia-Hernandez, Satoshi Hashiguchi, Hideaki Uchiyama, Kiyoshi Kiyokawa, Shohei Mori
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Augmented Reality (AR) can enhance accessibility by anchoring virtual windows to the user's body. Among common approaches, head-following windows help maintain floating virtual windows within the user's field of view. Previous studies have actively explored this new design space to improve user experience and efficiency. In contrast, this study focuses on the perceived resistance of head-following windows in AR, despite their lack of physical mass. We conducted a within-subject experiment with 24 participants, manipulating Follow-Up Delay, Window Size, and UI Type. We measured subjective resistance ratings, NASA-TLX (Raw TLX Scores), and the gaze–head angular offset. The results showed that both a certain level of Follow-Up Delay and the Tag-Along elicited significantly stronger perceived resistance as well as task load. Although Window Size alone did not show a significant effect on resistance ratings, we observed an interaction between the size and UI Type. These findings extend existing pseudo-haptics research by revealing the previously unexplored domain of resistance in head-based interactions with head-following virtual windows. We further provide design implications for head-following windows in AR.

title: Use Case Matters: Comparing the User Experience and Task Performance across Tasks for Embodied Interaction in VR_supp1-3680577.pdf
authors: Marie Luisa Fiedler
publish: N/A

title: Selection of Suitable Battery Technologies for Electric Vehicles Using Entropy Weight and EDAS Methods
authors: Muhamad Rasydan Mokhtar
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: The transportation sector can be considered one of the leading contributors to global warming. Conventional vehicles that use an internal combustion engine (ICE) will produce carbon emissions through the tailpipe. Conversely, a modern vehicle called an electric vehicle (EV) has no tailpipe emissions. The battery is one of the most important components of an EV system because it stores energy. As a consequence, the battery choice is a crucial item for long-term consideration. Hence, this study proposes the integration of two Multi Criteria Decision Making (MCDM) methods, namely entropy weight and Evaluation based on Distance from Average Solution (EDAS), to choose suitable battery technologies among four different types of batteries. All these batteries (graphene, lithium metal, solid state, and aluminium - air) will be assessed according to the nine criteria, which are cost-effectiveness, performability, safety, life, weightlessness, recyclability, maintainability, manufacturability, and fast charging. The result reveals that solid-state batteries are the best energy storage technology, as they have very high performability and safety for sustainable transportation. The findings of this study will help manufacturers and researchers ascertain the effectiveness of MCDM in achieving the best battery technologies for EVs in the future.

title: Using ADDIE Model to Develop Digital Educational Game for Teaching Permutation Concept
authors: Natasha Dejdumrong, Phacharawat Eakgawatphokhin
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This study presents the development and evaluation of a digital educational game designed to enhance students' understanding of permutation concepts in mathematics. The game was developed using the ADDIE instructional design model to ensure systematic alignment between learning objectives and gameplay mechanics. It incorporates intrinsically integrated game-based learning principles to promote logical reasoning and active problem solving. A pretest-posttest experimental design was employed to examine the effectiveness of the game on students' conceptual understanding, engagement, and problem-solving performance. The results indicate that the proposed game-based learning approach leads to improved learning outcomes compared to traditional instructional methods. These findings support the use of educational games developed under the ADDIE framework as an effective approach for teaching abstract mathematical concepts.

title: Retraction Notice: Energy Optimized Node Level Trust Based Data Transaction in Wireless Sensor Network
authors: A. Menaka, R. Jagadish, M. Murali, R. Bharathwaj, G. Abinesh
publish: 2020 6th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN), is widespread used by various fields due to its advantages and application. The major issue focused in a network to increase its performance is energy consumption and secure data transaction without any loss. In our proposed work, secure data transaction is focused through analyzing node trust. The challenge-response model promotes a focus on assessing trust for oneself; And linking it to its sub-center using the proposed self-survey and self-affirmation calculations, respectively. Hence node level trust is evaluated through self scrutiny and self attestation. Self scrutiny is analyzing every nodes self integrity level and self attestation evaluates the trust level of nodes that are included in transaction. In addition to this energy optimization has been implemented, to obtain this Energy Efficient Sensor Routing (EESR) protocol is implemented. This algorithm considers minimum path selection, energy utilization and node trust. To increase the performance based on energy factor, sleep and a wake approach has been used. Compared to existing method our proposed work attains maximum performance in both data delivery and network lifetime parameters.

title: Retraction Notice: Implementation of Image Enhancement and Image Segmentation In Disease Diagnosis (Lung Cancer)
authors: P A Aisiri, B Ramya, D J Chaithanya, S Anitha
publish: 2021 7th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Recently, image processing structures have been routinely used in a few clinical areas for improving prior different validation and therapy thresholds, particularly in different mischief tumours, such as cell breakdown in the lungs, where the time factor is critical to identify the affliction in the patient as quickly as possible. Cell breakdown in the lungs has been attracting the thought of clinical and sciatic organizations in the latest years because of its high inescapability lined up with the inconvenient treatment. Estimations from 2008 show that cell breakdown in the lungs, all through world, is the one that attacks the best number of people. Early disclosure of cell breakdown in the lungs is critical for productive treatment. There are very few methods open to recognize harmful cells. Here procedure for division, for instance, watershed is used to recognize the infection cell and to find better approach out of them.

title: X-PDResNet: An Explainable Residual Neural Network for Discrimination of Partial Discharge Defects in High Voltage Apparatus
authors: Sounish Maiti, Biswarup Ganguly
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Localization and categorization of partial discharge (PD) defects are essential for understanding insulation degradation and preventing failures in high-voltage electrical apparatus. Although numerous machine learning and deep learning approaches have been successfully applied for PD diagnosis, most existing models operate as black-box frameworks, offering limited transparency regarding their classification decisions. To address this limitation, this paper proposes an explainable residual neural network (X-PDResNet) for multiclass PD defect diagnosis. A customized residual network has been proposed to discriminate PD defects by employing time–frequency (TF) representations for effective extraction of both temporal and spectral characteristics. Further, an optimized explainable architecture is developed to generate fine-grained TF representations by modulating internal parameters of PDResNet. The proposed approach depicts its interpretability in terms of intermediate feature maps and relevance maps across different residual layers of the customized network. Feature activations extracted from shallow and deep layers reveal the progressive evolution from low-level texture patterns to highly discriminative representations specific to PD, allowing visual insights into the model’s decision-making process. Extensive experiments and ablation studies demonstrate the superiority of the proposed X-PDResNet with 98.67% accuracy, 98.68 % precision and 99.33 % specificity. The proposed explainable framework also shows strong potential for non-invasive condition monitoring applications in electrical insulation systems.

title: Reading Between the Signals: A Comprehensive Tutorial on Harnessing Physical Side-Channels for Anomaly Detection
authors: Kurt A. Vedros, Hunter Squires, Constantinos Kolias, Domenic J. Forte, Daniel Barbará
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Side-channel analysis (SCA), which involves analyzing physical emissions from devices to infer sensitive information, was traditionally regarded as a technique for enabling cryptographic attacks. In recent years, SCA has been increasingly applied to defensive purposes, with anomaly detection emerging as a prominent application. This survey systematizes the expanding field of Side-Channel-based Anomaly Detection (SCAD), which utilizes physical emissions such as electromagnetic radiation, power consumption patterns, acoustic signals, and thermal signatures to enable real-time system profiling, authentication, attestation, and malware detection. In contrast to software-based methods, SCAD provides non-invasive monitoring of devices, including embedded systems deployed in critical infrastructures. As a result, SCAD approaches enhance system robustness against sophisticated threats, including code injection, hardware Trojans, and micro-architectural attacks. This survey reviews over 50 SCAD methodologies across multiple domains and device types. The complete detection workflow of SCAD processes, encompassing signal acquisition, reprocessing, feature extraction, and model evaluation, is examined in detail, and for each phase, dominant methodologies are compared, including statistical and machine learning approaches such as shallow learning and deep learning, with an emphasis on their respective strengths and limitations. Publicly available datasets are also discussed, along with experimental practices. Lastly, common pitfalls and emerging challenges are identified, including issues related to signal synchronization, the impact of environmental noise, robustness against adversarial attacks, and the need for scalable and explainable detection models.

title: Quality of Service Enhancement and Security Assurance in Internet of Things Networks: A Survey and Integrated Blockchain Sidechain Architecture
authors: Anurag Sharma, Gurpreet Singh Chhabra, Bhabendu Kumar Mohanta, Ali Ismail Awad, Muhammad Khurram Khan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) enables various applications, including healthcare and smart grids, many of which demand a high-quality of service (QoS) and are subject to external and internal attacks. While blockchain-based approaches provide built-in security, they generally incur a significant computation burden, negatively affecting QoS. This paper adopts a hybrid approach that integrates a comprehensive survey with the introduction of a novel blockchain sidechain architecture. The paper begins with an extensive review of QoS in IoT networks, sidechain mechanisms for improving QoS, blockchain for IoT security, and the deployment of blockchain in IoT via sidechains. This study then proposes a novel architecture for a blockchain sidechain that features a dynamic-truncation sidechain management model to combat the QoS and security tradeoff problem. The proposed architecture enhances network QoS through the sidechain while providing essential security via a blockchain mechanism. The study utilizes the Tendermint consensus mechanism, which is specifically designed to mitigate threats such as Sybil, denial-of-service (DoS), and double-spending attacks. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed architecture yields significant QoS improvements compared to non-sidechain methods, including a 22.54% reduction in communication delay, a 28.11% decrease in energy consumption, and a 35.64% increase in network throughput. The model also achieves a 10.47% improvement in packet delivery ratio and a 22.37% reduction in delay jitter, while consuming only 2.11 KB of storage, representing a 30.37% reduction in storage usage. Additionally, the suitability of the proposed approach for resource-constrained IoT environments is demonstrated by both its ability to deliver faster block generation than state-of-the-art methods and by the robustness of the Tendermint consensus mechanism, which is confirmed through security analysis. Coupled with the comprehensive review and insights into future research directions, this work serves as both a foundational reference and a pathway for advancing QoS and security in IoT networks.

title: DS²AF: An Iterative Detection to Spectral Similarity-Guided Augmentation Framework for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Shih-Yu Chen, Yu-Cheng Wu, Kai-Quan Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) has greatly benefited from deep learning; however, the high dimensionality of hyperspectral data and the costly acquisition of labeled samples result in persistent sample scarcity, especially in real-world applications. Few-shot learning (FSL) addresses this challenge by enabling classification with only a few labeled samples per class, yet existing methods often struggle with HSIs due to complex spectral-spatial characteristics and substantial intra-class variability. This paper proposes DS²AF (Detection-to-Spectral Similarity-Guided Augmentation Framework), an iterative FSL framework that incorporates a novel Hyperspectral Detection-to-Similarity Ratio (HDSR) algorithm. HDSR unifies three complementary perspectives—matched-filter, angular, and probabilistic—into a single ratio-based formulation, yielding a robust global similarity measure for reliable sample selection. Leveraging HDSR, a two-stage augmentation strategy is employed: an initial the regional augmentation leverages spatial locality to stabilize the initial model, preventing it from diverging when sample scarcity is extreme. This process establishes a strong foundation by enriching the local neighborhood of labeled samples. Once the model has developed preliminary discriminative capabilities, global augmentation then searches for more representative samples across the entire image domain, gradually expanding the training set without additional manual labeling. A multi-branch classification network is further developed, comprising a full-band 1D spectral branch, an attention-enhanced 2D spatial branch, and a hybrid 3D-2D spectral-spatial branch to extract complementary features. Moreover, an adaptive stopping criterion based on classification consistency is introduced to ensure stability and prevent overfitting. Experiments on the Indian Pines, University of Pavia, and Salinas datasets under the 5-shot setting achieve overall accuracies of 83.47%, 93.01%, and 96.16%, respectively, substantially outperforming recent FSL approaches and confirming the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework.

title: Geographic and Statistical Analysis of EESS-Passive Satellite Overpasses for Spectrum Coexistence
authors: Nicholas Brendle, Jonathan Chamberlain, Joel T. Johnson, David Starobinski
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The passive Earth Exploration-Satellite Service (EESS) provides critical environmental measurements to support meteorological forecasting and climate monitoring. As demand for bandwidth increases among both active and passive users, spectrum sharing is becoming essential. Due to the high sensitivity of EESS-passive radiometers to anthropogenic interference, effective sharing strategies must account for satellite overpasses to limit measurement exposure to unwanted noise. This paper presents a statistical characterization of EESS-passive satellite activity by analyzing interarrival times and overpass durations across multiple geographic locations. Using observational datasets from 15 microwave radiometers, we compute complementary cumulative distribution functions (CCDFs) for both metrics across 15 cities and assess how latitude and longitude affect their statistical properties. While overpass durations are largely location-invariant, interarrival times show strong dependence on latitude, consistent with the polar-orbiting trajectories of the satellites. A key contribution of this work is the introduction of generalized models for the mean and standard deviation of both interarrival times and overpass durations for variable spatial cutoff distances (30–100 km), defined as the maximum horizontal separation between a fixed location and satellite measurements retained for analysis. These models are validated using regression analysis and support location- and scale-aware characterization of radiometer activity. Subsequently, we apply this framework to an economic spectrum-sharing model, demonstrating that minor changes in radiometer utilization —particularly at high latitudes — can lead to significant variation in provider profitability. These results provide valuable insights for policymakers and commercial stakeholders evaluating spectrum coexistence strategies involving EESS-passive systems.

title: Graph-Based Representation Learning with Beta Uncertainty for Enhanced Multimodal Emotion Recognition_supp1-3678321.pdf
authors: Yeon-kug Moon
publish: N/A
abstract: The performance of multimodal emotion recognition (MER) systems critically depends on their ability to effectively integrate information from heterogeneous sources. However, existing fusion methods including attention-based mechanisms, auxiliary regression, and sampling-based approaches often lack efficient strategies for managing uncertainty in the presence of noisy or conflicting inputs across modalities. To address this limitation, we propose a novel framework grounded in an Uncertainty-Aware Fusion Mechanism. Our system employs a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with specialized Transformer encoders to learn modality-specific representations. Within the fusion module, each modality expert predicts the parameters of a Beta distribution, yielding a bounded reliability score within the [0, 1] interval. In contrast to computationally intensive sampling techniques or generic Gaussian-based models, our method enables efficient, single-pass confidence estimation. These Beta-derived reliability scores dynamically modulate the contribution of each modality, attenuating the influence of less reliable sources. The resulting uncertainty-weighted representation is subsequently refined by a Dynamic Graph Convolutional Neural Network (DGCNN), which captures high-order, sample-specific relationships among modalities. We evaluate our approach on three diverse benchmarks: the DEAP dataset for physiological, the MELD and IEMOCAP datasets for conversational emotion recognition. Experimental results demonstrate significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting the effectiveness of the Beta-distribution-based fusion strategy for robust emotion recognition.

title: Evolutionary Hyper-Transformation for Multi-AAV Path Planning to Visit Moving Targets_supp1-3678144.pdf
authors: Bin Xin
publish: N/A
abstract: This article addresses a novel path planning problem for multiple fixed-wing autonomous aerial vehicles (AAVs) to visit a set of moving targets, originating from AAV cooperative missions such as emergency communication and target surveillance. This problem can be formulated as a multiple Dubins traveling salesman problem with moving targets (mDTSPMT). The key challenge lies in the strong cross-level coupling between target assignment, encounter sequences, and motion-constrained paths for multiple AAVs in the presence of moving targets. To solve mDTSPMT efficiently, we develop an efficient transformation method by sampling the access location and heading of each AAV to visit moving targets, constructing the mDTSPMT roadmap, and transferring it into an asymmetric multiple traveling salesman problem (AMTSP). This transformation allows the use of mature AMTSP solvers while preserving the essential motion and timing constraints of the original problem. However, the performance of the transformation method heavily depends on the quality of the samples. To improve the quality of samples, a hyper-transformation (HT) framework is proposed, which adaptively optimizes AAV sampling, guiding the search toward more promising configurations and enhancing both the solution quality and computational efficiency of the transformation method. Experiments with extensive instances show that the proposed method outperforms four competitive algorithms in generating coordinated and time-efficient Dubins paths for multiple AAVs encountering multiple targets.

title: Feature Optimization Approach Arcface-Based Surveillance Face Recognition
authors: Manaschai Aonon, Phasit Charoenkwan, Phudinun Singkhamfu, Jirapat Ruathaitong
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Security cameras are crucial for smart cities and identification, but face recognition faces challenges including varying illumination, pose variations, occlusions, and inconsistent resolution. This research proposes a feature optimization approach to enhance deep learning-based face recognition under challenging environments. Multiple optimization strategies including PCA, Grey Wolf Optimizer, Chi-squared, ANOVA, and Mutual Information were evaluated on ArcFace and FaceNet architectures. A two-stage verification system combining similarity thresholding with Rank-1 verification improves recognition accuracy. Experimental results on surveillance data from Chiang Mai, Thailand demonstrate that ArcFace with PCA retaining 95% variance achieved optimal F1 score of 0.714, substantially outperforming FaceNet with ANOVA_k100 (F1 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.500)$</tex>, while reducing dimensionality from 512 to 180 dimensions. Surveillance conditions require substantially lower similarity thresholds <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.30-0.45)$</tex> than controlled environments due to cumulative image quality degradation. Comparative evaluation confirms ArcFace's superior base representation for surveillance applications. F1 score proves essential for evaluation due to severe class imbalance in surveillance scenarios.

title: Applying the ADDIE Model to Develop a Phygital Pretend Play Learning Kit for Enhancing Wellbeing in Early Childhood Education Through Playful Learning
authors: Devanny Gumulya, Vanessa Elaine
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Playful learning through pretend play can significantly enhance children's well-being in early childhood education (ECD) by fostering imagination, creativity, and joyful exploration. In recent years, the integration of physical and digital tools has gained traction to enhance children's pretend play experiences. This study explores the research and development of a phygital (physical + digital) pretend play learning kit with the theme of Pilot Occupation, using the ADDIE framework, a five-step instructional design model (Analyze, Design, Develop, Implement, Evaluate) commonly applied to create effective learning materials. Following a research-through-design approach, the study develops prototypes as outcomes of the ADDIE model while also identifying key indicators for an effective phygital learning kit. For the physical aspect, integrating diverse materials is crucial to stimulating children's imagination and fostering creative play. In the digital component, self-paced learning features should allow children to explore at their own time and pace, promoting autonomy and self-regulation. Additionally, personalization is essential to adapt the experience to individual preferences, needs, and learning styles. Incorporating gamification elements, such as challenges, rewards, and levels, enhances motivation and engagement. To ensure a seamless blend between physical and digital tools, a multi-sensory approach is necessary, where tangible materials provide tactile engagement while digital components offer auditory and visual stimuli, creating a holistic learning environment. This research contributes to the field well-being of ECD by providing insights into the design and development of phygital learning kits that support early childhood learning and establishing clear indicators for their successful implementation.

title: Application of Convolutional Neural Networks for Enhancing Gemstone Classification Performance
authors: Lukkana Poempool, Phornsin Pumpakar, Chiabwoot Ratanavilisagul, San Ratanasanya
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Gemstones have different values and characteristics, such as color, shape, and texture. These differences make manual classification and counting difficult and time-consuming, especially when gemstones have similar appearances. In practice, gemstone classification is still mainly performed by humans, which can lead to errors caused by fatigue or repeated counting. This study applies image processing and deep learning techniques to improve the accuracy and efficiency of gemstone classification and counting under controlled imaging conditions. The OpenCV library is used to adjust brightness, reduce noise, and detect object edges, while a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is used to learn gemstone features such as color, brightness, and texture. Moreover, the HSV color model is applied to reduce the effect of lighting and shadows that can affect classification accuracy. Experimental results show that the proposed method provides better classification and counting performance than the compared methods, achieving an accuracy of 99.70%. The results indicate that the proposed system can effectively reduce human-related errors and processing time, and it demonstrates potential applicability as a laboratory-scale, proof-of-concept solution for automated gemstone inspection.

title: Classifying Burmese (Myanmar) Medical Reviews Using Synthetic Data Approach
authors: Hein Minn Thu, Pyae Bhone Moe, Nyein Chan Ko Ko, Aye Hninn Khine
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Burmese (also known as the Myanmar language) remains a low-resource language for Natural Language Processing, particularly in the healthcare domain, where labeled corpora are limited due to privacy and ethical concerns. This study investigates the feasibility of using synthetic data for medical review sentiment classification in Burmese. We construct synthetic medical review corpora by translating English medical review datasets into Burmese using both large language models and machine translation models. The resulting datasets are evaluated on downstream sentiment classification tasks using Logistic Regression and Support Vector Machine models with both syllable-level and word-level features. Experimental results show that synthetic reviews translated by Google Translate achieved the highest F1-score of 0.71 on the Medication Review Dataset, while Gemini-2.5-pro yielded an F1-score of 0.45 on the Medical Condition Dataset. These findings demonstrate that, despite occasional translation errors and hallucinations, synthetic data can serve as a foundation resource for Burmese healthcare NLP. This work provides the first benchmark for Burmese medical review classification and highlights the importance of combining synthetic data generation with expert validation for reliable low-resource digital health application.

title: TCSET 2026 Cover Page

title: Reliability in Industry-Academia Assessment: A Framework for Validating Competencies in IT Education
authors: Prasara Jakkaew, Pruet Putjorn, Nacha Chondamrongkul
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: High-stakes competency assessments involving mixed-expert panels face inherent reliability challenges. This study investigates an assessment paradox in a hackathon-style evaluation where a psychometrically sound rubric yielded unreliable outcomes. A post hoc analysis of a national IT talent program in Thailand revealed that while the assessment instrument demonstrated excellent internal consistency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha=0.912$</tex>), interrater reliability for absolute agreement was poor (ICC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=$</tex> 0.276). The discrepancy was diagnosed as systemic Scale Disagreement, where experts applied divergent severity standards. To address this, a six-step protocol using Zscore standardization was applied to normalize scores. The rectified results were perceived as fair by the learners. The findings suggest that while pre-assessment rater calibration is a necessary preventive measure, a post hoc statistical rectification protocol serves as a critical safety net to ensure the validity and fairness of certification.

title: Fidelity as Forgetting: Rethinking Reconstruction in Neural Volumetric Media
authors: Yin Bing, Shuyi Wang, Angela Chulei Tang, Siyun Wang, Yuan Zhang

title: Automated UAV Controller Synthesis via LLM-Generated Control Logic and Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Christopher Carr, Miguel Martínez-García, Benjamin James Marshall, Matthew Coombes, Eve Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Large language models guided evolutionary frameworks for program synthesis have demonstrated strong results across combinatorial benchmarks; however, such frameworks have not been widely explored for robotic control systems. This article presents an automated framework for synthesizing control logic and optimizing numerical coefficients, enabling the deployment of generated controllers onto robotic platforms. To produce deployable controllers, the framework separates control logic synthesis from numerical parameter optimization, with controller coefficients optimized via particle swarm optimization using a Markov decision process reward signal. The resulting controllers are evaluated in a custom uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAV) simulation environment, validated using PX4 software-in-the-loop, and subsequently deployed on a physical UAV. Controller performance is evaluated on lemniscate and lissajous trajectory-tracking tasks and compared against proportional–integral–derivative with disturbance observer and linear quadratic regulator baselines. Across both trajectories, the framework-generated control law achieves improved tracking accuracy relative to the baseline controllers, with a minimum reduction in mean squared error of approximately 38% in real-world experiments. These results demonstrate the feasibility of deploying automatically synthesized control logic on a physical UAV, bridging automated program synthesis and real-world control deployment.

title: Cap4Bridge: Caption-Guided Cross-Modal Contextualization with Stochastic Augmentation for Text-Video Retrieval
authors: Minju Jeon, Hyungee Kim, Si-Woo Kim, Youngtaek Oh, Soeun Lee, Dong-Jin Kim
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: A key challenge in text-video retrieval is bridging the semantic gap between information-rich videos and concise text queries. Existing methods often address this by incorporating auxiliary captions from Large Language Models (LLMs) or employing stochastic modeling. However, these approaches face critical challenges: captions can lack domain-specific relevance, while stochastic methods that directly model text embeddings risk distorting the original query’s intent. To overcome these issues, we propose Cap4Bridge, a framework that leverages semantic anchors searched from a domain-specific caption anchor bank. Our framework introduces two key components: (1) Caption-Guided Cross-Modality Contextualization, which uses a shared co-attention mechanism to enrich both video and text representations with these anchors, and (2) Similarity-Aware Stochastic Augmentation, which applies Gaussian noise scaled by relevance to the searched semantic anchors rather than the query itself. This integrated strategy bridges the fundamental information imbalance by providing complementary context to both modalities and robustly expanding the semantic representation while preserving the original query’s intent. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across most benchmarks, demonstrating its high efficacy and generalizability, particularly in challenging cross-domain settings.

title: Long-Tailed Skeleton-Based Action Recognition via Realistic Mixing and Class-Aware Sampling
authors: Jinwoo Kim, Wonhee Kim, Mi-Seon Kang, Jong Taek Lee
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Skeleton-based action recognition has gained increasing attention due to its robustness to background variations and privacy-preserving properties. However, real-world skeleton datasets often exhibit severe class imbalance, leading to long-tailed data distributions that significantly degrade recognition performance, especially for tail classes. Existing mix-based skeleton augmentation methods partially alleviate this issue by increasing data diversity, but they often generate physically implausible skeletons and fail to explicitly account for class imbalance during augmentation. In this paper, we propose a long-tailed augmentation framework with two key components: (i) realistic skeleton mixing in the relative bone vector space and (ii) class-aware sampling that increases effective augmentation frequency of underrepresented classes. Specifically, we perform mixing in the relative bone vector space rather than joint coordinate space to preserve kinematic consistency and further enforce bone-wise length alignment to prevent unrealistic limb deformations. To mitigate class imbalance, we introduce a class-aware weighting mechanism that biases sampling toward tail classes. Extensive experiments on the long-tailed NTU RGB+D and NTU RGB+D 120 benchmarks show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing approaches across various imbalance factors, delivering substantial improvements on the tail classes while remaining robust across different backbone architectures.

title: S$^{3}$aDPWo: Spatial-, Semantic-, and Shape-Aware Diffusion Policy Towards Autonomous Wound Repair
authors: Wenda Xu, Haozhe Fang, Zexin Cao, Zhihang Tan, Gongcheng Wang, Han Wang, Weidong Wang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Imitation learning (IL) offers a promising pathway for enabling surgical robots to perform autonomous wound repair. However, existing methods often neglect spatial semantics and wound-shape information, leading to poor generalization and low success rates. This paper presents the Spatial-, Semantic-, and Shape-aware Diffusion Policy towards autonomous Wound Repair (S$^{3}$aDPWo), a framework integrating two visual perception modules tailored for wound repair: the Spatial Semantic Perception Module (SSPM) and the Wound Shape Perception Module (WSPM). These modules supply the action predictor with semantically enriched point clouds and keypoint-based wound geometric descriptors, enabling S$^{3}$aDPWo to jointly perceive spatial-semantic and wound-shape information. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms in wound segmentation and keypoint prediction, and further validate the overall framework on wound approximation—a key contact-rich sub-task of wound repair essential for facilitating subsequent suturing and promoting healing. Notably, S$^{3}$aDPWo achieves success rates of 90% and 80% on seen and unseen wound instances, respectively, while maintaining mean errors below 3 mm across inter-edge distance, edge-height difference, and edge consistency. This substantially outperforms SOTA IL baselines in both generalization and performance.

title: Marking Underwater Infrastructure: A Crab-Like Robot for Traversing Pipes
authors: Jiyuan Jiang, John Grezmak, Kathryn A Daltorio
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Underwater infrastructure projects are increasingly seeking robots to inspect, repair, maintain, and salvage in the ocean with minimal human diver time. To perform tasks that would otherwise require human workers, most remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) are designed to operate either while resting on bottom surfaces (which provides stability) or while suspended neutrally buoyant in the water column (which expands workspace, but requires careful thrust control for large forces or precise motion). Here, our hypothesis is that by using the legs to locomote on and grasp infrastructure substrates, stable toolpaths are generated while walking, without relying on localization or thrust actuators. To our knowledge, the robot presented here is the first legged, underwater robot capable of walking along pipes. To show repeatable, consistent toolpaths, we attach a marker that leaves continuous paths on the cylindrical surface of the pipe. Specifically, we demonstrate that our half meter-wide hexapod walks horizontally or climbs vertically on both 25.4 cm and 45.7 cm diameter pipes. Full (360°) circumferences on both pipes are completed in 75 s, and a 3.5 cm × 2.5 cm square is drawn in 3 s. Individually, these demonstrations parallel future application-specific tasks such as cutting a piling into sections for salvage, cleaning patches of pipeline for ultrasonic testing, or dislodging invasive species. Taken together, our work suggests that bio-inspired legs can provide robust robotic solutions to support ambitious undersea construction projects. Future work is expected to demonstrate value of combining closed-loop model-based control; adaptive behaviors for addressing biofouling or other pipe irregularities; underwater thrusters for initial positioning.

title: Flapping Hydrofoil Propulsion for Unmanned Surface Vehicles: Design and Experimental Evaluation
authors: Luca Romanello, Felix Koch, Elias Zorgati, Daniel Gebhart, Pham Huy Nguyen, Mirko Kovac, Sophie F. Armanini
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: This study presents a novel hydrofoil-based propulsion system for water surface-operating robots, inspired by the paddling locomotion of aquatic species. Several hydrofoil designs were developed and evaluated using a custom Hydroflapper setup to assess their hydrodynamic performance. The proposed framework incorporates a camber-modulating mechanism to investigate whether dynamic camber variation can enhance thrust and propulsion efficiency. Initial simulations indicated that camber modulation increases horizontal thrust compared to symmetric hydrofoils, highlighting its potential benefits. A physical prototype was then constructed, featuring independent control of heave and pitch motions. Indoor and outdoor experiments were conducted to measure thrust generation and efficiency under various hydrofoil geometries and operating conditions, benchmarking results against conventional propeller-based systems. Experimental findings show that symmetric hydrofoils exhibit higher efficiency than propeller-driven systems in starting from rest conditions, while camber-changing hydrofoils demonstrated reduced performance at the tested scale-likely due to mechanical limitations in the actuation mechanism. Complementary CFX simulations supported these results, showing minor efficiency gains for the cambered foils and suggesting that observed inefficiencies originate from mechanical rather than hydrodynamic constraints.

title: IDC-Integrated SRR-based Printed Differential Microwave Sensor on Biodegradable Substrate for High Sensitivity Material Sensing
authors: S. M. Ishraqul Huq, Gaozhi Xiao, Sharmistha Bhadra
publish: IEEE Journal of Radio Frequency Identification
abstract: This paper presents a printed differential microwave sensor fabricated on a biodegradable cellulose acetate (CA) substrate for dielectric sensing of solid and liquid materials. The proposed sensor employs a splitter/combiner-based feeding network with differential ring resonator architecture to compensate for environmental effects. The sensitivity is enhanced by integrating an interdigital capacitor (IDC) within the sensitive split region of the resonators. The sensor is experimentally validated for sensing thin solid materials with variable thicknesses, and different concentrations of ethanol and methanol solutions. In solid sensing, the maximum differential resonant frequency shift is 236 MHz for a 0.5 mm thick CA sample. In liquid sensing, a total differential resonant frequency shift of 270 MHz for ethanol and 106 MHz for methanol is achieved over the full 0 to 100% concentration range. A thermal stability study shows that the differential sensing scheme exhibits strong immunity to environmental temperature variations, maintaining a constant differential resonant frequency of 246.25 MHz in response to temperature change. In addition, a controlled biodegradability study of the CA substrate shows maximum weight losses of 17.8% and 40.1% for thick and thin samples, respectively. The proposed sensor with the fully additive fabrication technique using printed silver conductors enables a low-cost, robust, and environmentally sustainable sensing platform with high sensitivity.

title: Successful Diversity Is Not What You Think: How to Build a Great Start-Up Team
authors: Anouk Hubrechsen, Claire Migliaccio
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine

title: A Review of Neural Networks in Precipitation Prediction
authors: Yugong Zeng, Jiayuan Wang, Jonathan Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Precipitation prediction has undergone a profound transformation. A notable limitation of traditional NWP is the need for extensive statistical post-processing. To address this challenge, neural network–based approaches were developed. These approaches offer a framework that directly learns the mapping from atmospheric predictors to precipitation targets. Based on the technological development, this article first reviews the traditional precipitation forecasting methods and summarizes the development trends of precipitation forecasting based on neural networks. We then outline the training process, loss functions, and some datasets for precipitation prediction. In the main body of the article, we detail the basic artificial neural networks (ANNs), spatial feature extraction models, time feature extraction models, generative models, Transformer models, graph neural networks (GNNs), and emerging hybrid models. Finally, in the appendix, we supplement the commonly used evaluation metrics. This paper focuses on the advantages and disadvantages of various neural network models in precipitation forecasting applications, and also pays attention to the latest progress of neural network-based methods. Overall, neural networks have significantly improved the accuracy of short-term and medium-term precipitation forecasting, but still face challenges in representing extreme rainfall, handling imbalanced data, and ensuring physical consistency. The latest progress shows that future prediction systems will increasingly rely on the integration of multiple sources of data and hybrid physical-data-driven models to enhance their robustness and applicability. By compositing research covering multiple eras and paradigms, we not only depict the history of neural networks in precipitation prediction but also outline future directions in next generation forecasting systems.

title: Synthetic AI-Based Ultrasound Images of Breast Cancer on Small Datasets
authors: K. Onsasipat, U. Chaumrattanakul, W. Lohitvisate, S.S. Makhanov
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Ultrasound (US) remains one of the most reliable and inexpensive tests. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have emerged as a promising tool for augmenting medical image datasets used by AI solutions. However, GANs trained on small datasets (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$300-500$</tex> images) frequently encounter mode collapse, overfitting, and instability. Many GAN images look unrealistic. Enhanced Deep Convolutional GAN (EDCGAN) is introduced to generate quality synthetic images of breast ultrasound (BUS). The contribution consists of several modifications to the standard architecture and postprocessing based on the visual evaluation by radiologists. The Inception Score, the Structural Similarity Index, and the Mean Squared Error comply with the results obtained in the preceding works. The proposed idea to merge Deep Learning(DL) and Machine Learning(ML) is based on the experience of the radiologists, which considerably improves the outcome. The experiments demonstrate that EDCGAN outperforms selected state-of-the-art GANs on small datasets.

title: A Pilot Study on the Feasibility and Preliminary Efficacy of a Virtual Reality System for Stress Reduction
authors: Pakakrong Panthanan, Banphot Nobaew
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Workplace and academic stress remain significant impediments to well-being, yet many Virtual Reality (VR) interventions face practical barriers due to specialized monitoring requirements and limited user agency. This pilot study examines the feasibility and preliminary efficacy of an accessible, design-driven VR stress management system integrating Apple Watch-based heart rate variability (HRV) monitoring with a customizable VR framework that allows users to actively design and configure their own virtual environments. 24 participants (14 employees and 10 students) completed a single 15-minute immersive session. Outcomes were assessed using HRV (rMSSD) and the SPST-20 perceived stress scale. The intervention demonstrated high procedural feasibility, with no reports of adverse effects or motion sickness. Following the VR experience, perceived stress decreased consistently, with the largest reductions observed in affective dimensions such as anxiety and frustration. At the physiological level, HRV increased at the group level, indicating enhanced autonomic recovery, while individual variability was evident, particularly among participants with higher baseline physiological arousal. The convergence of subjective and objective outcomes suggests that a user-centric VR framework integrated with wearable technology represents a feasible and promising approach to stress regulation. These findings provide a foundation for scaling personalized digital interventions within the field of Digital Arts and Media for Health.

title: To be Healed or Hacked? - User-Centered Ethical Design for Embodied AI in Mental Health Care_supp1-3680608.mp4

title: Retraction Notice: Automatic Breast Tumor Cancer Diagnosis Based on a Hybrid Densenet
authors: G. Jayandhi, J.S. Leena Jasmine, R. Seetharaman, S. Mary Joans, R. Priscilla Joy
publish: 2022 8th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: The breast cancer based image classification and division is proposed by utilizing a Deep learning (DL) technique. A few DL models is used to classify Mammographic information to forecast and analyze breast cancer. In this work, the mixture of two DL is named as Hybrid Auto encoder and DenseNet CNN (HAE-DNN). The Auto-encoder (AE) is likewise a NN that is utilized for a compacted portrayal of crude information. This AE has both encoder and a decoder for pressure and extraction process. Therefore DL of DenseNet is a sort of CNN that has thick associations among layers, through Dense Blocks. This organization can associate each layer with relating feature-map measures straightforwardly with each other. Therefore the proposed strategy is very well based on foreseeing performance in conditions of accuracy, Recall, F1 score, RMSE and MAE individually. The proposed technique is contrasted and the earlier DL strategy and accomplished a preferred outcome over existing.

title: Title Page
authors: N/A
