title: Retraction Notice: Comprehensive solution of Scheduling and Balancing Load in Cloud - A Review
authors: N. Manikandan, A. Pavin
publish: 2019 Third International conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)
abstract: Cloud computing provides powerful methods for digital resources to share computing tasks. Congruent scheduling solutions for task and workflow are necessary in order to develop profitable executions and obtain availability, load balancing and consistency in cloud computing, The problem of planning, an NP-hard issue, is solved by the use of multiple metaheuristic algorithms. This paper elaborates on the scheduling schemes for task and workflow for the cloud environment. Cloud computing is instrumental in sharing data and offering a myriad of resources to users, wherein users pay only for the resources used by them. Cloud computing sanctions storage of data and allocated resources in an open environment, thus enabling an increase in the quantity of data storage. Several types of load balancing issues such as memory, CPU and network load are inevitable in cloud environment. To solve these balancing problems by locating the overloaded nodes and diverting the load to another node in the network, load balancing employed. This helps in delegating the incoming workload to various nodes in the system to assure that none of the nodes are overloaded.

title: Retraction Notice: Prediction of Juvenile Delinquencies in Correlation with Education
authors: R. Akila, J. BrindhaMerin, R.K. Vishal, Vishal Krishnan S.H.
publish: 2020 6th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Juvenile Crime is not a healthy trait for any developing country and certainly not for a widely cultured and populous country like India. India has been suffering from overpopulation and low literacy for many years now and it affects the growth of the nation. Education is the key to improve the state of the country and more juvenile crime, children receive lesser quality education and it keeps pulling down the nation. Not only that, it also poisons the next generation and is not healthy for the future of the nation. Even though education is provided for the convicted children, not everyone who is released continues to study which leads to nothing but more uneducated adults who are less productive in this competitive world. This project analyses the juvenile delinquency rate over the course of 30 Years and predicts the possible rate of juvenile crime in India in the forthcoming years, taking into account the various factors that would affect said juvenile crime rate, which include but not limited to: overpopulation, education especially between the ages 16 to 18, financial state of family, cultural differences, regional differences and past criminal history within the convicted juvenile's family. Since factoring in all the aforementioned features into account is near impossible, various machine learning (Linear Regression, Polynomial Regression regression, Back Propagation Network and Adam Optimization Algoritms) and deep learning models are created to primarily focus on the admission rates at different education levels in India. The results of said models provide us with insight on how important education is and how would the future generations shape up when the current trend continues, and also which of the selected learning models is the most accurate.

title: Hybrid Dynamic-Statistical Algorithm for Optimizing CMOS-Type System Configuration
authors: Iryna Kremer, Olha Shymchyshyn, Mariia Ivakh, Ruslan Tykhovetskyi
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: This paper investigates the impact of technological variability on configurable CMOS GreenPAK systems and the limitations of conventional analysis techniques. Using empirical data from the SLG47011V chip, correlation links between key functional block parameters were identified and incorporated into refined statistical models. A hybrid dynamic–statistical algorithm is introduced, combining step-wise digital state-machine simulation with analytical variability estimation via a modified Delta method. Compared with Monte Carlo analysis, the proposed approach reproduces statistical parameters with an error below 2% while reducing computational cost by over two orders of magnitude. For a PWM generator, agreement with experimental measurements better than 0.2% was achieved. The method enables fast and accurate reliability evaluation and is well suited for early-stage design of configurable mixed-signal microsystems.

title: Neural Network Image Quality Enhancement Using Multiscaling, Attention, and Residuals
authors: Arkadiusz Talun, Pawel Drozda, Yuriy Romanyshyn, Sergei Yelmanov, Markiian Durkot, Oles Tehlivets
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: The problem of image quality enhancement based on a neural network using multiscale, attention, and residuals is considered. The MIRNet neural network and its modifications were used as the basic neural network. The structures and functional features of individual MIRNet network blocks are presented including scaling blocks and channel attention block. The TID2013 database was selected as the basis for test images to study the characteristics of neural network training and functioning. Two options for enhancing images from the TID2013 database were considered: 1) training the network on images from the TID2013 database and enhancing the quality of images from this database, and 2) training the neural network on the LoL dataset and enhancing the quality of images from the TID2013 database using the neural network trained in this way.

title: IEEE Standard for Wearable, Cuffless Blood Pressure Measuring Devices
authors: N/A
publish: N/A
abstract: A normative definition of wearable cuffless blood pressure (BP) measuring devices and the objective performance evaluation of this kind of device are established in this standard. This standard is independent of the form of the device or the vehicle to which the device is attached or in which it is embedded. All types of wearable BP measurement devices, including epidermal and unobtrusive devices with different modes of operation (e.g., short‑term, long‑term, snapshot, continuous, beat‑to‑beat BP, or BP‑variability measurement), are covered by this standard. This standard is, however, limited to the evaluation of devices in which a cuff is not used during measurement, and the evaluation of all sphygmomanometers that are used with an occluding or inflatable cuff for the indirect determination of BP on the upper arm or wrist is not covered. Guidelines are provided in this standard for manufacturers to qualify and validate their products, potential purchasers or users to evaluate and select prospective products, and health care professionals to understand the manufacturing practices of wearable BP devices.

title: Retraction Notice: Vegetable Disease Detection Using K-Means Clustering And Svm
authors: U. Rahamathunnisa, M.K. Nallakaruppan, A. Anith, Sendhil Kumar K.S.
publish: 2020 6th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: India is the cultivating country and our country is the biggest maker in agricultural products. So, we have to classify and exchange our agricultural products. Manual arranging is tedious and it requires works. The automatic grading system requires less time for grading of the agricultural products. Image processing technique is helpful in examination and evaluating the products. In this paper we proposed a vegetable disease detection system for recognizing diseased vegetables. Here we utilize the Image processing system for reviewing the vegetables. Vegetables are recognized dependent on their features. The features are color, shape, size, texture. We extract these features utilizing algorithms to distinguish the vegetables. We develop a recognition system for 2D input images. The main aim of this work is detecting infected vegetable based on features with K-means clustering algorithm. Algorithm includes three main steps namely enhancement, segmentation and classification. Vegetable samples are collected as images from high resolution camera and data acquisition is carried out for database preparation. The image segmentation process is based on pixel of the image and is applied to get the segmented and infected vegetables using K-Means Clustering algorithm. The image classification is based on Support Vector Machine (SVM) which perform supervised leaning for classification.

title: Retraction Notice: IOT Based Health Tracking Shoe For Elderly People Using Gait Monitoring System
authors: K Jose Reena, R. Parameswari
publish: 2021 7th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Modern Technology increases the economic needs of each person in their machine life. This Machine life reduces the time to take care of their elderly parents in the real world. This paper proposed techniques to monitor and track their elderly parents from their shoe embedded with sensors. Proposed Techniques that track their gait structure and blood pressure, heart rate, and information passed to the responsible person of the elderly parents through SMS. This Paper consists of strategies to implement the gait monitoring system through the wireless sensor with a mobile application using the GSM Sim module.

title: CDSSL: A Self-Supervised Learning Framework for Cross-Domain Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
authors: Zixin Zhang, Huan Ni, Yufu Zang, Jinsheng Chen, Qiucan Huang, Qianqian Zhang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Cross-domain remote sensing semantic segmentation often experiences performance decline due to spectral distribution inconsistencies caused by variations in satellite sensor parameters. To address this issue, this paper proposes a collaborative domain adaptation framework, termed CDSSL, which integrates style transfer and self-supervised learning. The method employs an optimal transport-based style transfer approach to align visual features across datasets. An entropy-weighted self-supervised strategy is applied to guide pseudo-label selection using prediction uncertainty, thereby improving semantic consistency throughout the adaptation process. Experiments on LoveDA and GID datasets demonstrate that our framework consistently enhances segmentation performance across diverse land-cover categories in both domain transfer directions.

title: A PPO-GAN-Enabled Moving Target Defense for Cyber-Resilient and Cost-Aware Power System Scheduling
authors: Ali Peivand, Seyyed Mostafa Nosratabadi, Jamshid Aghaei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy resources and the escalating sophistication of cyberthreats, particularly false data injection attacks, impose severe operational and security challenges on modern power grids. This article develops a reinforcement-learning-based moving target defense (MTD) framework whose core novelty is a proximal policy optimization (PPO) agent that learns an adaptive perturbation policy, jointly selecting reactance perturbation magnitudes and line locations to maximize the attack detection rate while keeping the operational cost increment within predefined limits. To enrich the threat model, a generative adversarial network (GAN) acts as an adversary that synthesizes stealthy attack vectors, forcing the PPO-driven MTD policy to adapt under uncertainty. Battery energy storage (BES) is studied as a flexibility resource that mitigates the economic burden of MTD actions by providing compensation, enabling stronger yet cost-feasible perturbations. The following three configurations are evaluated: PPO-MTD, PPO-MTD with BES, and PPO-MTD with BES- and GAN-based adversarial training. The framework is validated on the IEEE 118-bus test system across multiple detection-threshold scenarios. Results show that BES improves detection by about 125% while maintaining cost increments below 0.02%, and GAN-driven adversarial dynamics further enhance robustness, sustaining satisfactory security under stealth attack rates up to 17%. The findings indicate that PPO-driven adaptive MTD enables a secure, adaptive, and economically sustainable paradigm for next-generation cyber-physical power systems.

title: A Swin Transformer-Based Hybrid U-Shape Network for Nonlinear Hyperspectral Unmixing
authors: Jiangyi Chen, Mengying Jiang, Yuanchao Su, Ruoqing Xu, Xu Sun, Jianjian Gao, Xiaofei Yang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Hyperspectral unmixing remains challenging, as most existing deep learning methods often neglect multi-scale relationships and rely on fixed receptive fields, failing to adequately capture the hierarchical characteristics of nonlinear mixing phenomena. Inadequately addressing these issues hinders the precise characterization of intricate nonlinear physical interactions and leads to the loss of fine-grained spatial-spectral details, degrading the accuracy of abundance estimation. To overcome above limitations, this paper introduces the Swin Transformer-based Hybrid U-Shape Network (STHUN) that incorporates the multilinear mixing model (MLMM) to better represent nonlinear mixing. STHUN employs a hybrid architecture synergizing convolutional layers for local feature extraction with Swin Transformer blocks to capture long-range dependencies. A novel multi-attention feature enhancement module (MAFM) is further designed to facilitate robust multi-scale feature fusion through hierarchical attention mechanisms. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets validate that STHUN achieves superior performance in endmember extraction and abundance estimation compared to state-of-the-art approaches, demonstrating its effectiveness in advancing hyperspectral unmixing research.

title: Comparative Anylysis of Radiation Characteristics of a Single Impedance Body
authors: Mykhaylo Andriychuk, Borys Yevstyhneiev
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: The radiation properties of the small single impedance body determined using the asymptotic approach are compared with one received by classical methods of Debye potentials. In both the cases the solution to scattering problem is got in the semi-analytic form that makes the possible to carry out the numerical computations in a wide range of physical parameters of problem. The conducted calculations allow to evaluate the area of applicability of asymptotic approach because the Debye potential methods provides with the exact solution in the analytical form.

title: Decentralization Model for the Replica State Discovery Protocol
authors: Maksym Kotov, Serhii Toliupa, Oleksandr Buchyk
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: The advent of intercommunication technologies has led to a surging demand for public computational services alongside requirements for their security, reliability, and operational stability in the face of ever-growing traffic volumes. Modern services incorporate distributed topologies to meet said demands through independent scalability and redundancy mechanisms. With myriads of interconnected microservices and processes encompassed within a single system, coordination solutions are often used to distribute tasks and orchestrate efficient execution. Alongside the growing computational demand, recent advances in decentralization technologies primarily based on blockchain have pushed the tendency towards open public systems independent of any controlling entity. The Replica State Discovery Protocol is a general-purpose coordination protocol allowing the implementation of custom pluggable modules responsible for cluster state management. Through its interfaces, a group of independent workers could be organized into a coherent cluster without any need for a dedicated controller server. Existing iterations of the protocol depend on centralized communication solutions to propagate messages as well as authentication. The purpose of this article is to develop a decentralized model of RSDP.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Data Augmentation Strategies for Brain Tumor Segmentation on Small MRI Datasets
authors: Serhii Misochenko, Karina Selivanova
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: Accurate segmentation of brain tumors in MRI remains challenging in settings where only small annotated datasets are available, making data augmentation essential for improving model generalization. Inspired by prior work highlighting the potential of elastic deformation and brightness adjustment for tumor segmentation. This study presents a comparative evaluation of different data augmentation strategies for brain tumor segmentation on a small MRI dataset. We focus on analyzing the impact of geometric and photometric augmentations on segmentation accuracy and tumor boundary preservation. The YOLOv8s architecture is used as the base segmentation model due to its robustness when trained on limited data.

title: Retraction Notice: WSN in Defence Field: A Security Overview
authors: Rama Moorthy H., Varsha Bangera, Zeba Amrin, N.J. Avinash, Krishnaraj Rao N.S.
publish: 2020 Fourth International Conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)
abstract: The communication in the military is generally significant. It is a must to have a safe channel through which data is traded progressively and for the protection of data. The wireless sensor network can be utilized in a military application for observing aggressor exercises Iil trespassers, keeping track of enemies, and power assurance. Wireless sensor networks have an enormous arrangement of disseminated sensor nodes. These sensor nodes are low controlled, minimal effort, little in measure, and can do constrained measure of calculation. Here in this paper, it is possible to check with the different types of security issues of WSN. Also, a different existing security algorithm can be studied and deployed in the WSN’ defense application.

title: Effective Scriptwriting Principles for Public Presentation During the Covid-19 Pandemic
authors: Suparada Prapawong
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This paper examines how scriptwriting contributed to public communication during the COVID-19 pandemic, drawing on observations from the Covid-19 Script Project at the College of Arts, Media and Technology, Chiang Mai University. Rather than focusing solely on the accuracy of health information, the study considers how digital storytelling practices-especially the way stories were structured and voiced-helped audiences make sense of an overwhelming and rapidly changing situation. Using qualitative textual analysis, ten widely viewed YouTube videos were reviewed for their narrative flow, tone, and use of visual explanation. The analysis is guided by key concepts in Digital Storytelling [1], Participatory Communication [2], Transformative Learning [3], and Health Communication in Crisis Contexts [4]. Across the selected clips, three recurring scriptwriting principles emerged: contextual empathy, where messages reflect personal or shared experiences; narrative simplicity, which favors clear and approachable language; and participatory authenticity, expressed through real voices, testimonies, or community-based collaboration. These principles contributed to making scientific information feel easier to follow and, in many cases, more credible to general audiences. The findings offer a qualitative framework that may also support future AI-driven or NLPbased content generation systems for public communication.

title: Retraction Notice: A Novel Design of Autonomous Cars using IoT and Visual Features
authors: B Padmaja, P V Narasimha Rao, M Madhu Bala, E Krishna Rao Patro
publish: 2018 2nd International Conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC), 2018 2nd International Conference on
abstract: Autonomous car is a ground vehicle that is capable of driving without user interference. Traffic congestion and number of collisions are major issues in road traffic control due to rapid increase day-by-day. Autonomous cars provide a solution to this problem in an efficient and economical way. The proposed system utilizes mathematical models like neural networks and image processing techniques to sense the environment. This is implemented as three major components: curved road detection (steering), road sign and signal detection and obstacle detection (collision avoidance). Back Propagation is used for steering control with detection of curved roads; Haar features are used for road signal, sign detection and a distance sensor for collision avoidance. Data collected from the sensors is sent to a server for processing. Based on the result, a command is sent to the car. A GPS module attached to the car identifies the location of the car and with the help of a 3rd party location service, route to destination is identified and directions are sent to the car. Wireless networks are used to transmit data between sensors and the server. Python scripts are used to control and integrate all the units together. The designed system can attain high accuracy with real – time constraints.

title: Retraction Notice: A Trend Analysis of Diffusion Energy Effective Clustering Techniques in Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: S. Manikandan, M. Jeyakarthic
publish: 2019 5th International Conference on Advanced Computing & Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Evolutions and recent trends in communication technologies have the development of low charge, low power, tiny sized and multidimensional purpose sensor node for wireless sensor networks (WSNs). But energy constrained nature of WSNs demands that their planning and interactive protocols to be designed in an energy aware manner. This paper proposed to make a trend analysis reports on the major perspectives of fuzzy based distributed energy efficient clustering techniques. The most representative fuzzy based clustering techniques describes, deliberated and qualitatively analyzed. In particular the proceeds of different distributed clustering are analyzed with respect to their implication performance and application circumstances. This analysis aims to deliver suitable guidance for system architects to evaluate and select appropriate fuzzy based distributed clustering techniques for their specific application.

title: Retraction Notice: Recent Study on Image Denoising using Deep CNN Techniques
authors: Ashly Roy, P Anju, Linnet Tomy, M. Rajeswari
publish: 2021 7th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Image denoising is one of the important subjects in the examination field. On the presentation of profound CNN in picture denoising, the picture is by all accounts more exact from commotion when contrasted with the non-cnn picture denoising strategies. Thusly, this paper is an audit on some CNN models for denoising boisterous picture including Deep CNN utilizing leftover learning with and without group standardization, Deep shrinkage CNN, Mixed commotion decrease CNN, Residual learning of profound CNN with Symmetry-Rectifier Linear Unit (SyReLU), Dilated Residual Networks with Symmetric Skip Connection (DSNet), Fast and adaptable denoising CNN (FFDNet), Batch-renormalization denoising network (BRDNet) and Attention-guided denoising convolutional neural organization (ADNet).The denoising achievements of the above models are analyzed on different gray and colour.

title: Point Cloud Video Modeling With Progressive Prior Knowledge Guidance and Adaptive Neighboring Aggregation
authors: Jingkun Yan, Hongwei Ge, Mingze Cui, Chunguo Wu, Mingde Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Point cloud video modeling not only has to address the natural irregularity of point clouds, but also the challenge of capturing spatial and temporal representation simultaneously. Current methods attempt to approximate the temporal dimension using several 3-D point cloud frame sequences but struggle in sparser conditions. Accurate point trajectory tracking is crucial for effectively capturing temporal dynamics, as point positions across different frames are often inconsistent, especially during rapid motion or at low frame rates. Conventional point tube operations aggregate motion features over fixed time windows but fail to capture rapidly changing scenes. Implicit tracking techniques are limited by quadratic time complexity, which restricts their practical use. In this article, we propose a native 4-D framework (N4DF) that guides the network to learn spatio-temporal dynamics from a native 4-D perspective. Furthermore, we devise a dynamic point spatio-temporal (DPST) convolution to adaptively select the optimal point-tracking strategy, which constructs local plane regions in anchor frames and propagates them to neighboring frames to evaluate point cross-frame movement distances. To further enhance the global modeling power of N4DF, we develop a dynamic self-tracking re-encoding (DSTR) module that employs point-wise self-attention to search for relevant points across the entire video. Compared with the recent 4-D modeling methods, N4DF demonstrates superior performance on MSR-Action3D and NTU RGB+D for action recognition (+0.7% and +1.2% accuracy, respectively), on HOI4D for action segmentation (+1% accuracy), and on Synthia 4-D and nuScenes-lidarseg for semantic segmentation (+0.49% and +1.7% mIoU, respectively). Our N4DF shows greater robustness at low frame-rate settings due to native 4-D modeling and adaptive tracking, making it suitable for tracking fast-moving objects in future real-time scenarios.

title: An improved driving power supply method for a portable electromagnetic impact source with a short acceleration distance
authors: Wei Wang, Xian Liao, Xinglin Lu, Jingjie He, Zhihong Fu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Portable electromagnetic impact seismic sources, which use reluctance coil emission technology, have been partially applied in seismic engineering and geophysical exploration. This technology offers significant advantages in equipment stability and the simplicity of the electromechanical structure. However, the short acceleration distance inherent to their portable design limits the hammer's ability to achieve high ejection kinetic energy. In this study, a new driving power supply for the acceleration coil was enhanced and redesigned based on the previously established architecture. By leveraging the complementary advantages of commonly used driving power supplies (capacitor and battery), a method combining both for a time-sharing drive was proposed. The results of comparing and analyzing the output kinetic energy of the seismic source under five different driving power supply methods indicate that the ‘battery driven first, capacitor driven later’ outperforms ‘battery driven alone’, ‘capacitor driven first, battery driven later’ and ‘capacitor driven alone’, increasing the hammer's kinetic energy by 20.49%, 48.96%, and 109.23%, respectively. Based on an analysis of the reasons for these improvements, a scheme named ‘battery driven first, two capacitors driven later sequentially’ was proposed. Compared to ‘battery driven alone’, this scheme enhances the hammer's kinetic energy by 41.57%. The improved driving power supply significantly enhances the performance of the electromagnetic impact seismic sources, achieving an output kinetic energy of 1910.35 J.

title: Invisible Bullet-Magnetism Aim Assistance in VR Dart Throwing: Effects on User Experience and Performance_supp1-3680707.mp4
authors: Ren Boz
publish: N/A
abstract: Aim assistance has been shown to be effective in traditional video games; however, its application in VR contexts remains relatively underexplored. We investigate the effects of invisible bullet-magnetism aim assistance applied at the time of throw on performance and user experience in a VR dart-throwing task. We corrected the trajectory by adjusting the initial throwing direction to reduce the landing position deviation from the target center by 50%. We conducted a within-subjects experiment with 29 participants, comparing the aim-assist condition with a control condition without assistance. Aim assistance with invisible bullet magnetism significantly improved objective and perceived performance, accuracy, and precision. Participants in the aim-assist condition reported significantly higher perceived competence and control, while perceived suspense and pressure were scored significantly lower. Enjoyment, internal attribution of achievement, and workload remained comparable between the two conditions. Participants attributed their success to themselves rather than external factors in both conditions. The invisible bullet-magnetism aim assistance in our study remained largely unnoticed, and participants favored the aim-assist condition.

title: PP-wave reflection coefficient approximation for seismic amplitude inversion in biaxial stress-induced anisotropic media considering microcrack closure
authors: Liangyi Deng, Xingyao Yin, Zhengqian Ma, Kun Li, Yaming Yang, Bing Wang, Guanjie Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Horizontal biaxial stress, a prevalent stress state of Earth’s crust, can cause preferential closure of microcracks within rocks, thereby triggering elastic anisotropy. However, the nature effect of biaxial stress on seismic reflection response has been devoted scant attention. Addressing this issue, an approximate PP-wave reflection coefficient in biaxial stress-induced anisotropic media incorporating microcrack closure is developed for seismic amplitude inversion. Based on the dual-porosity model, we treat the initial unstressed rock as a stress-insensitive background matrix embedded with randomly oriented microcracks. Under small biaxial stress, microcracks preferentially close, expressed as normal and tangential compliances decreasing exponentially as a function of the normal stress acting on the microcrack surface. A corresponding biaxial stress-induced anisotropy model is derived, and then experimentally validated using existing rock samples. We then use it to construct the effective stiffness tensor of the biaxial stress-induced anisotropic media through the effective medium theory and the weak anisotropy assumption. Two sets of stress-dependent anisotropy parameters (SDAPs), defined as the combination of elastic modulus, stress and normal/tangential compliance, are introduced to quantify the magnitude of stress-induced anisotropy. Furthermore, considering the scattering theory, a linearized PP-wave equation is derived in terms of P- and S-wave modulus, density and SDAPs. We exhaustively investigate the effect of biaxial stress on seismic response characteristics. Finally, within the Bayesian framework, the SDAPs are inverted using the azimuthal amplitude difference inversion method incorporating Cauchy prior information and low-frequency regularization. The inverted SDAPs then serve as inputs to predict the remaining isotropic parameters. The feasibility of the inversion approach is illustrated with synthetic seismic examples and a real data application. We determine that our study contributes to understanding stress-induced anisotropy and can produce potential stress indicators.

title: Theoretical and Empirical Study of Spatial Power Focusing Effect for Sparse Arrays At Terahertz Band
authors: Yongchao He, Taihao Zhang, Cunhua Pan, Hong Ren, Xianzhe Chen, Tian Qiu, Bingchang Hua, Jiangzhou Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This work investigates the spatial power focusing effect for large-scale sparse arrays at terahertz (THz) band, combining theoretical analysis with experimental validation. Specifically, based on a Green's function channel model, we analyze the power distribution along the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$z$</tex-math></inline-formula>-axis, deriving a closed-form expression to characterize the focusing effect. Furthermore, the factors influencing the focusing effect, including phase noise and positional deviations, are theoretically analyzed and numerically simulated. Our analysis theoretically quantifies the impact of practical non-idealities, revealing that while the focusing effect remains robust against phase noise, it is highly sensitive to positional deviations, where even wavelength-level positional deviations lead to a significant reduction in received power. Finally, a 300 GHz measurement platform based on a vector network analyzer (VNA) is constructed for experimental validation. The measurement results demonstrate close consistency with theoretical simulation results, confirming the spatial power focusing effect for sparse arrays.

title: Descriptor: The WiFi-Based Human Activity Recognition Under Everyday Life Changes Dataset (WiFi-Wild)
authors: Amany Elkelany, Robert Ross, Susan Mckeever
publish: IEEE Data Descriptions
abstract: WiFi-based Human Activity Recognition (HAR) provides a non-invasive alternative to camera- and wearable-based sensing, but model performance often degrades under environmental and subject-specific variations. This work introduces the WiFi-Wild dataset, a publicly available collection of WiFi Channel State Information (CSI) signals designed to capture everyday life changes such as furniture rearrangement, transmitter/receiver placement, device interference, and subject diversity. The WiFi-Wild dataset was collected in a residential environment using ESP32 devices, with a single participant performing a range of daily activities under multiple spatial and environmental scenarios, and additional participant appearances were included to evaluate subject variability. To demonstrate dataset utility, a hybrid deep learning model combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with an attention-based Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) network was trained for activity classification and evaluated across different configurations. Results show that environmental and subject-related variations substantially affect model performance, with furniture rearrangements and TX/RX repositioning reducing F1-scores by up to 21%, and signal interference or clothing changes causing decreases of 7–12%. The WiFi-Wild dataset enables systematic, reproducible evaluation of WiFi-based HAR models under realistic conditions and supports the development of more robust systems.

title: Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Night-time Light in Sudan during the Ongoing Conflict
authors: Yuanxi Ru, Yingce Wei, Xubing Zhang, Xi Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The armed conflict in Sudan, spanning from April 2023 to the present, has escalated into a severe humanitarian disaster. Night-time light data can serve as a valuable proxy for monitoring the humanitarian conditions in conflict-affected area where ground-based data collection is severely constrained. This study utilized the Black Marble monthly night-time light product (VNP46A3) to analyze the spatiotemporal dynamics of night-time light in Sudan during the conflict period, with particular focus on major conflict hotspots such as Khartoum State and the Darfur region. Additionally, we examined refugee movements from Sudan to Chad by analyzing night-time light changes along the Sudan-Chad border. Furthermore, we employed a two-way fixed effects (TWFE) model to quantify the relationship between night time light change (NLC), conflict frequency, and internally displaced persons (IDPs). The results indicate that night-time light in Sudan exhibited a sharp and persistent decline following the onset of the conflict, with nearly half of the states experiencing a light reduction exceeding 50%. Along the Sudan Chad border, night-time light decreased on the Sudanese side but increased on the Chadian side, suggesting an influx of Sudanese refugees into Chad. The TWFE model demonstrated that NLC has a negative correlation with both conflict frequency and the net outflow of IDPs. These findings can provide the international community with a deeper understanding of the evolving humanitarian situation in Sudan, and highlight the value of night-time light remote sensing in effectively capturing conflict induced socioeconomic disruption and population displacement.

title: On the Effect of Domain-Adversarial Supervision Placement for Cross-Sensor 6D Pose Estimation
authors: Tobias Niedermaier, Sarah Weiß, Mahmoud Salem, Christopher Bonenberger, Maik Knof, Stefan Elser, Markus Reischl
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Domain-adversarial training is a common approach to mitigate domain shift in deep learning, yet architectural design choices such as the placement of adversarial supervision are often treated heuristically. We present a systematic empirical assessment of gradient reversal-based domain-adversarial training applied to cross-sensor 6D pose estimation, focusing on the placement of domain classification heads within a multi-modal red, green, and blue(RGB)–point cloud fusion network. Using a simplified bidirectional RGB–point cloud fusion network, we evaluate domain head placement at multiple depths, including modality-specific encoders and intermediate fusion stages, under a strict multi-source training protocol with no access to target-domain data. Experiments are conducted on controlled synthetic datasets with multiple runs per configuration and complemented by real-world cross-sensor RGB-D evaluations. Across all settings, performance differences between domain head placements are dominated by run-to-run variability, with no statistically significant advantage for any particular placement. Real-world experiments exhibit similarly small differences and qualitatively align with synthetic results. These findings indicate that, for the studied architecture, gradient reversal-based domain-adversarial training is largely insensitive to the precise placement of the domain head, suggesting that both early and late integration constitute viable design choices. This robustness provides practical flexibility in network design, supports reproducible evaluation across placements, and motivates futurework on complementary adaptation mechanisms that can be combined with adversarial supervision.

title: Resilient networked control systems subject to cyber threats: a data encryption and cloud computing based model predictive control approach
authors: Giuseppe Franzè, Vicenç Puig, Antonello Venturino, Francesco Tedesco
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This work presents a resilient model predictive control framework designed for constrained cloud-based networked control systems, addressing false data injection attacks that affect both the controller-to-actuator and sensor-to-controller communication channels. By taking advantage of the robustness of the receding horizon control philosophy and the confidentiality property of homomorphic cryptosystems, an innovative strategy based on their join action is conceived to secure data packets, i.e., ensuring a promptly detection in response to different malicious external actions, designing an array of attack countermeasures for the on-line operations. Finally, the recursive feasibility of the resulting scheme is formally proved in terms of sufficient conditions derived by resorting to a joint use of set-theoretic arguments and integrity properties of the shared data. A case study, taken from a part of the Barcelona drinking water network, is used to validate the whole architecture in simulation.

title: Micromobility Vehicle Classification for Smart City Infrastructure Using Magnetic Field Sensor Array
authors: Eidenis Kasperavičius, Dangirutis Navikas, Vytautas Markevičius, Algimantas Valinevičius, Mindaugas Žilys, Mindaugas Cepenas, Jožef Ritonja, Dardan Klimenta, Nikolay Hinov, Darius Andriukaitis
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Urban mobility worldwide is seeing an increasing use of micromobility vehicles such as bicycles and e-scooters. However, accurate methods for measuring their traffic parameters remain limited, which could result in outdated infrastructure planning practices. Conventional sensing technologies such as pneumatic tubes, computer vision, and inductive loops struggle with the small dimensions, low metal content, and high maneuverability of micromobility vehicles. This work presents an experimental classification method based on magnetic signatures, which is designed for micromobility traffic monitoring in smart city applications. A sensor array consisting of 11 magnetic field sensors installed across a bicycle path was used to collect 236 real-world vehicle signatures. Fourteen temporal, spatial, and frequency-domain features were extracted from each signature and evaluated using multiple machine learning models. Gradient Boosting model achieved the highest performance with a cross-validation accuracy of 82%, demonstrating that magnetic field signatures contain sufficient information to differentiate bicycles, e-scooters, and heavy micromobility vehicles. Even with a limited dataset, results demonstrate the feasibility of micromobility traffic monitoring based on magnetic signatures. Expanding the dataset and implementing additional signal processing algorithms is expected to further enhance classification accuracy and support safe and accessible mobility in smart cities.

title: Respiration Rate Estimation Using ECG and PPG Data: Open Source Implementations Focused on Wearables
authors: Chryssalenia Koumpouzi, Matthew Pediaditis, Emmanouil Spanakis, Vangelis Sakkalis
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Respiratory rate (RR) is an indicator of various psychological and pathological conditions and needs to be monitored easily and reliably. Emerging trends and recent advancements indicate that wearables are the future of continuous noninvasive vital measurement for healthcare. Numerous recent studies have focused on estimating RR from the electrocardiogram (ECG) or photoplethysmogram (PPG), which can be acquired non-invasively with minimal hardware and without obstructing spontaneous breathing patterns. While easy and flexible in acquisition, such signals may suffer from poor signal quality, which in turn results in unreliable estimation of RR, an issue that can be addressed by evaluating the quality of the obtained estimates. In this work, we propose a novel method for RR estimation based on the cross-correlation function that demonstrates improved estimation properties compared to most existing open-source methods achieving 0-bias and limits of agreement (LOAs) from -5.37 to 5.44 bpm. We propose the use of variance as an estimate quality metric and demonstrate how this approach results in higher accuracy. Two types of fusion are designed and evaluated, stressing the importance of quality evaluation and estimate combination in improving accuracy, reaching LOAs between -1.44 and 0.9 bpm. Finally, we stress the importance of signal duration in estimation accuracy, and demonstrate how longer segments result in lower error, validating this finding on multiple open-source methods. Our results are evaluated on three publicly available datasets, including CapnoBase, a benchmark database on respiration.

title: Advertising & Sales
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine

title: Sonar Mapping and Obstacle Avoidance for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles in Unknown Marine Environments
authors: Guoshun Liu, Shanmin Zhou, Huarong Zheng, Shuo Liu, Wen Xu
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: This paper proposes a safe navigation framework for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) that integrates sonar mapping and motion planning operating in unknown environments. The challenge is that sparse sonar data causes incomplete obstacle boundaries, posing risks to the vehicle's safe navigation. Furthermore, the AUV's nonlinear dynamics and constrained onboard computing capacity complicate real-time planning. To address these issues, Bayesian kernel inference method is first adopted to estimate occupancy in unobserved regions. Its accuracy is enhanced by an entropy-based adaptive approach that adjust the training set and kernel length. Then, a multilayer motion planner is designed, integrating global path planning with local trajectory generation. Specifically, a novel 3D path planner capable of handling AUV pitch and yaw constraints for efficient search is proposed, followed by path refinement and spatiotemporal optimization within the sonar's perception range to obtain locally optimal trajectories. The sonar mapping and obstacle avoidance motion planning are evaluated through simulations and pool experiments using a real AUV. Results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed underwater mapping and planning framework.

title: Integrating Digital Twin and Swarm-Based UAV Systems for Autonomous Wildfire Prevention and Detection
authors: Enrico Gomes, Nuno Rodrigues, Alexandrino Gonçalves, Paulo Costa, Carlos Costa, Roberto Ribeiro, António Pereira
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The increasing number of wildfires throughout the world has resulted in a global loss of forest areas, which are important for sustaining life. To detect and prevent these disasters, a system comprised of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles, their Digital Twins and a Boids-based autonomous patrolling algorithm is proposed. By replicating an Unmanned Aerial Vehicle and its camera specifications, or by use of a real drone, it is possible to simulate wildfire detection and prevention missions in a virtual map. These vehicles can be controlled manually or set to move autonomously as a swarm by following an algorithm based on the Boids program, where they operate with a bird flock-like movement and avoid colliding with other neighbouring vehicles. Additionally, the use of real-time detection of objects of interest through Computer Vision is also an option. A first solution built upon the proposed system was implemented as a web application for access through a considerable number of devices and validation for the system achieved promising results.

title: Phase-Only Synthesis for Reconfigurable Arrays With Antenna Selection and DRR Constraints
authors: Wen Fan, Xuhui Fan, Tao Wang, H. C. So, Chunze Li
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a novel approach for sparse reconfigurable arrays that combines phase-only control, sparse design, and DRR constraints to minimize peak sidelobe level (PSL) while reducing system cost and size. The proposed approach decouples the optimization problem involving coupled excitation weights (with common amplitudes and DRR constraints) and antenna selection vector via an alternating minimization framework, and then applies successive convex approximation to handle nonconvex constraints, i.e., common excitation amplitude, DRR constraints, Boolean selection, and mainlobe upper bounds. Furthermore, to enhance the convergence of the proposed algorithm and mitigate potential infeasibility caused by initial points in convex approximation problems, we incorporate slack variables whose quantity increases gradually over the iteration. Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach in decreasing the number of elements and DRR while synthesizing multiple patterns that satisfy radiation performance.

title: Fabrication and Test of a Curved-Canted-Cosine-Theta Magnet Used for a Laser-driven Proton Accelerator System
authors: Jinshui Sun, Yaohui Wang, Shunzhong Chen, Wanshuo Sun, Yong Chen, Kedong Wang, Xinning Hu, Qiuliang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Proton therapy (PT) is a precise and efficient form of radiotherapy that can accurately target lesions to destroy tumor cells while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues. Peking University has initiated a project to develop a laser-driven proton accelerator system. This paper reports a curved Canted-Cosine-Theta (CCT) magnet, which is a critical component in the proton accelerator system. The CCT superconducting magnet was designed with a curved dipole magnet and two quadrupole CCT magnets nested symmetrically at the dipole magnet ends. The curved CCT magnet has a bending radius of 1000 mm, with tilt angles of 30° and 45° for the dipole and quadrupole magnets, respectively. The dipole magnet was used to generate a 2.5T transverse magnetic field in a 63-mm warm bore, and the quadrupole magnets were used to generate a 20T/m gradient magnetic field at both ends of the magnet. The whole magnet was wound with NbTi superconducting wire, with an operating current of 380A. In this paper, the magnet manufacturing process and testing are presented.

title: Automatic OCT scanning of moving soft tissue using continuum endoscopic robots
authors: Guiqiu Liao, Sujit Kumar, Phillippe Zanne, Benoit Rosa, Florent Nageotte, Michel De Mathelin, Diego Dall’Alba, Michalina J. Gora
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) offers real-time, high-resolution imaging of tissue internal structures, presenting a potential enhancement over conventional endoscopic examination of the digestive system surface. However, miniaturized OCT probes typically present challenges with a limited field of view and require precise positioning to optimize tissue visualization. This research addresses the challenge by leveraging robotizing a surgical endoscope and a steerable OCT probe to provide closed-loop scanning of larger areas in the presence of tissue motion. We propose a robotic multi-pullback technique, supported by real-time, tactile feedback from OCT images. Deep learning algorithms are employed for OCT image stabilization and segmentation, enabling autonomous robotic volumetric OCT scanning. Compared to previous works, our closed-loop control approach considers, in addition to the probe flexibility, also the movements and deformations of the tissue during the robotic scanning. For experimental validation, we designed a moving soft tissue phantom with force sensing and compared the proposed method with fixed-angle approaches. Our method consistently maintained minimal contact forces (around 50 mN) and clear tissue visibility for tissues with different stiffness and motion patterns. Resulting imaging quality was further validated using a multi-layer optical phantom under dynamic conditions.

title: Physics-Informed Reinforcement Learning for Utility-Aware Pilot Selection in LEO Channel Estimation
authors: Da Wan, Zhongliang Deng, Wenliang Lin, Sheng Wu, Chunxiao Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Data-assisted channel estimation (DA-CE) faces unique challenges in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) scenarios with large Doppler, where phase distortion and pilot sparsity degrade the utility of data symbols for refinement. Moreover, myopic, context-agnostic reliability metrics often fail to identify data symbols that are truly useful for improving estimation accuracy. To resolve these challenges, we innovatively reformulate the data selection as a pixel-level utility masking problem, similar to semantic segmentation tasks, where each resource element (RE) is evaluated for its utility in channel refinement. We introduce an integrated framework rooted in physics-informed reinforcement learning (PIRL), addressed by two symbiotic components. First, a Physics-informed Subspace Basis Expansion Models-LMMSE (PiSBEM-LMMSE) algorithm acts as the perception layer of our framework, which yields a high-fidelity, physics-consistent state representation by expressing the dominant Doppler-induced dynamics via a low-rank complex-exponential basis and decoupling them from residual stochastic fading. Second, a lightweight U-Net-based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent, acting as the cognitive decision core, learns an optimal, context-aware masking policy upon this structured representation. The U-Net architecture, with its encoder-decoder structure and skip connections, is specifically chosen to process the multi-channel, image-like state representation, capturing both global channel dynamics and local perturbations. Extensive simulations demonstrate that our framework achieves significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods, exhibiting remarkable robustness in LEO scenarios where conventional approaches fail.

title: History of Wireless Education in the Last Century and the Role of the University of Calcutta [Historically Speaking]
authors: Prasanta Kumar Basu, Susanta Sen, Debatosh Guha, Trevor S. Bird
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine

title: Improving Local Feature Matching by Entropy-inspired Scale Adaptability and Flow-endowed Local Consistency
authors: Ke Jin, Jiming Chen, Qi Ye
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Recent semi-dense image matching methods have achieved remarkable success, but two long-standing issues still impair their performance. At the coarse stage, the over-exclusion issue of their mutual nearest neighbor (MNN) matching layer makes them struggle to handle cases with scale difference between images. To this end, we comprehensively revisit the matching mechanism and make a key observation that the hint concealed in the score matrix can be exploited to indicate the scale ratio. Based on this, we propose a scale-aware matching module which is exceptionally effective but introduces negligible overhead. At the fine stage, we point out that existing methods neglect the local consistency of final matches, which undermines their robustness. To this end, rather than independently predicting the correspondence for each source pixel, we reformulate the fine stage as a cascaded flow refinement problem and introduce a novel gradient loss to encourage local consistency of the flow field. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our novel matching pipeline, with these proposed modifications, achieves robust and accurate matching performance on downstream tasks.

title: Decentralized Model Predictive Control for Platooning: Enhancing Human–Driver Collaboration
authors: Justin M. Kennedy, Lixin Yang, Daniel E. Quevedo, Falko Dressler
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Recent advances in cooperative adaptive cruise control have demonstrated the potential for vehicle platooning to revolutionize road transportation through enhanced safety, reduced congestion, and improved energy efficiency. While autonomous vehicle technology continues to evolve rapidly, current regulatory frameworks and safety considerations necessitate human–driver supervision. This creates a unique challenge in developing control systems that can effectively balance autonomous operation with human intervention. To enhance the human–driver collaboration with autonomous vehicle platooning, in this article, we present a novel decentralized model predictive control framework that explicitly incorporates human–driver interaction while maintaining desired intervehicle distances and velocities in platoon formations. This framework employs a distributed architecture where each vehicle operates independently and exchanges local measurements through vehicle-to-vehicle communication. To overcome the inherent unreliability of wireless communications in real-world scenarios, we develop a robust distributed state estimation strategy. This approach enables each vehicle to combine local sensor measurements with received data to construct accurate estimates of the full platoon state. Based on these estimates, vehicles compute optimal control actions locally while achieving performance comparable to an ideal centralized controller with perfect communication. Through extensive Plexe simulations, we demonstrate that the proposed decentralized model predictive controller achieves comparable performance to the ideal centralized case, even under partial state information and communication constraints.

title: FuzzyCam: A Class Activation Image Generation Method Based on Fuzzy Impulse Detection
authors: Xiao Zhicheng, Liu Ming, Wang Junjie, Yu Shengze, Chen Siwei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: This paper proposes FuzzyCAM 1, a novel Class Activation Mapping (CAM) method that enhances deep neural network interpretability by exploiting impulse features in gra dient maps. Within this framework, we propose constructing CAMs based on specific features and establish the relationship between impulse noise and the CAM method through two theorems: Theorem 1 proves the existence of gradient impulses during backpropagation, and Theorem 2 demonstrates gradient impulses origin in misclassified samples and intrinsic link to model learning. FuzzyCAM detects these impulses via a fuzzy impulse detection algorithm, converts them into adaptive weights for feature map fusion, and generates targeted class activation maps. To address the shortcomings of existing CAM evaluation metrics, a dynamic threshold segmentation method based on the σ principle and SRNA have been proposed. Visualizations con firm its superiority in highlighting semantically critical regions, advancing reliable model interpretation.

title: LMG: Efficient Streaming of Layered Mesh-Gaussian 3D Scenes
authors: Yuan-Chun Sun, Guodong Chen, Sam Ziaie Kondori, Mallesham Dasari, Cheng-Hsin Hsu
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Multimedia Systems Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: A Pose-only Geometric Constraint for Multi-Camera Pose Adjustment
authors: Shunkun Liang, Banglei Guan, Bin Li, Qifeng Yu, Yang Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multi-camera systems offer rich observation capabilities for visual navigation and 3D scene reconstruction; however, the resulting feature redundancy often compromises computational efficiency. This challenge is particularly pronounced during bundle adjustment, where the non-linear optimization of both system poses and scene points incurs substantial computational overhead. To address this challenge, this paper introduces a pose-only geometric constraint for multi-camera systems and proposes a corresponding pose adjustment algorithm. Specifically, we use generalized camera model to establish a unified representation of the multi-camera system. Building upon this model, we formulate the multi-camera pose-only constraint, which implicitly represents a 3D scene point using two base observations and their associated poses, thereby achieving a pose-only representation of the projection geometry. Subsequently, we introduce a multi-camera pose adjustment algorithm that eliminates 3D points from the parameter space, thereby achieving efficient and focused pose optimization. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms baseline bundle adjustment methods in computational efficiency, while maintaining or even improving pose estimation accuracy

title: Asymptotic Analysis of Max-Min SINR in Downlink MISO System with Multi-Weighted Power Constraints
authors: Shasha Liu, Abla Kammoun, Hayssam Dahrouj, Mohamed-Slim Alouini
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: This work addresses the fundamental problem related to the characterization of the parameters of optimal precoders at the asymptotic regime, a topic of increasing interest given the upsurge in massively connected networks and their emerging applications. The paper, particularly, focuses on the downlink of a single-cell multi-user system, where a base station (BS) equipped with M antennas communicates with K single-antenna users. We then propose a low-complexity design for the optimal linear precoder (OLP) that maximizes the minimum signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) while ensuring multi-weighted power constraints. To achieve this, we consider the asymptotic regime where M and K grow large while maintaining a fixed ratio. Utilizing tools from random matrix theory (RMT), we derive explicit deterministic approximations for the parameters of the OLP and the allocated powers for users. One of the paper highlights is that the approximations provide deeper insight into the structure of the optimal precoders, predict the active weighted power constraints, and significantly reduce the implementation complexity in large-scale networks. Another paper highlight is how to analyze best scenarios where the power weights for some users are exactly zero, a case not addressed in [1]. We revisit the original OLP, presenting an implementation that accommodates zero-power weights and propose its equivalent low-complexity asymptotic design. Numerical results are used to validate the asymptotic analysis is accurate in the finite system regime and investigate the impact of weighted power constraints on the system energy efficiency (EE).

title: OFERA: Blendshape-driven 3D Gaussian Control for Occluded Facial Expression to Realistic Avatars in VR
authors: Seokhwan Yang, Boram Yoon, Seoyoung Kang, Hail Song, Woontack Woo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: We propose OFERA, a novel framework for real-time expression control of photorealistic Gaussian head avatars for VR headset users. Existing approaches attempt to recover occluded facial expressions using additional sensors or internal cameras, but sensor-based methods increase device weight and discomfort, while camera-based methods raise privacy concerns and suffer from limited access to raw data. To overcome these limitations, we leverage the blendshape signals provided by commercial VR headsets as expression inputs. Our framework consists of three key components: (1) Blendshape Distribution Alignment (BDA), which applies linear regression to align the headset-provided blendshape distribution to a canonical input space; (2) an Expression Parameter Mapper (EPM) that maps the aligned blendshape signals into an expression parameter space for controlling Gaussian head avatars; and (3) a Mapper-integrated Avatar (MiA) that incorporates EPM into the avatar learning process to ensure distributional consistency. Furthermore, OFERA establishes an end-to-end pipeline that senses and maps expressions, updates Gaussian avatars, and renders them in real-time within VR environments. We show that EPM outperforms existing mapping methods on quantitative metrics, and we demonstrate through a user study that the full OFERA framework enhances expression fidelity while preserving avatar realism. By enabling real-time and photorealistic avatar expression control, OFERA significantly improves telepresence in VR communication. A project page is available at https://ysshwan147.github.io/projects/ofera/.

title: Interaction Under Whole-Body User Rotations in VR Space
authors: Filip Škola, Fotis Liarokapis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) enables interactions beyond real-world limitations, yet many such capabilities remain underexplored due to concerns about user comfort and increased disorientation and cybersickness. This study examined how altering the user's virtual pitch orientation influences interaction performance and subjective ratings. In a within-subject design, 30 seated participants experienced 12 virtual tilts from moderate to extreme (±180°). We assessed comfort, simulator sickness, perceptual responses, and performance. Our results revealed no significant increases in nausea, disorientation, or decreased comfort, with mild tilts performing equivalently to baseline. Notably, even extreme tilts maintained low nausea levels. Performance effects were mixed: forward tilts yielded similar or slightly improved performance, whereas backward tilts modestly impaired performance, though not significantly. These findings suggest that VR experiences featuring virtual body orientations distinct from the user's physical posture are feasible without compromising comfort or performance, enabling novel opportunities in simulation, interface design, visualization, creative content creation, and VR gaming.

title: A Mixed-Methods Comparative Study of Remote and Traditional SDR Laboratories Via Bloom's Taxonomy, Surveys, and Focus Group
authors: Rania Hussein, Zhiyun Zhang, Marcos Inoñan, Pablo Orduña
publish: IEEE Transactions on Learning Technologies
abstract: Hands-on experimentation with Software-Defined Radios (SDRs) is critical for teaching radio frequency (RF) and wireless communication concepts in Electrical and Computer Engineering (ECE) and Computer Science (CS) curricula. While traditional radios require costly hardware and have fixed configurations, SDRs offer a flexible, software-programmable alternative. Remote SDR laboratories further enhance accessibility by enabling students to conduct experiments anytime, anywhere—reducing institutional costs and offering flexibility. While SDR remote laboratories offer significant benefits, current research lacks systematic comparisons of their effectiveness in developing cognitive skills versus traditional labs and short-term skill persistence. This study evaluates the RHL-RELIA (Remote Engineering Lab for Inclusive Access), a novel SDR remote laboratory integrating popular hardware and software to support remote experimentation. A controlled split-group experiment was conducted with 36 students (primarily 4th-year undergraduates and graduate students with minimal communication theory background) in a capstone course at a public university in the United States. Lab 0 introduced foundational SDR skills, while Lab 1 compared learning outcomes between students using RHL-RELIA and traditional SDR lab kits. In Lab 2, all students used RHL-RELIA to assess short-term persistence. Student reports were evaluated using a Bloom's taxonomy rubric spanning the Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate levels. Results revealed that the RHL-RELIA group outperformed the in-person cohort in Lab 1 across four cognitive domains (Remember, Understand, Analyze, and Evaluate). Longitudinal analysis of Lab 2 showed sustained gains: students who initially used RHL-RELIA retained higher performance in Remember, Understand, Apply, and Evaluate compared to the control group, suggesting long-term cognitive benefits. Pre- and post-lab online surveys were administered to collect structured student feedback, complemented by a focus group study to gather qualitative insights into their learning experiences. Thematic analysis revealed positive perceptions of RHL-RELIA, with students emphasizing its usability and flexibility. These findings suggest that RHL-RELIA has advantages over traditional in-person SDR labs in fostering both immediate learning outcomes and short-term knowledge retention.

title: Gradient Embedding and Modal Complementarity Networks for Audio-Video Depression Level Prediction
authors: Mingyue Niu, Zhongping Zhang, Chang Gao, Yan Zeng, Ning Fan, Shiqing Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing
abstract: Physiological studies show distinct audio-video behaviors correlate with depression levels, leading some researchers to use Transformer architecture to predict depression levels. The positional embedding in Transformer assigns a positional marker to each element, but the multi-head attention mechanism operates at the token level and is difficult to examine the temporal changes of elements. This brings limitations in capturing dynamic differences reflecting depression. Besides, previous works adopt element-wise operation, fully connected layers or attention mechanisms for modal complementarity. However, they don't specify what the supplemental representation is, and don't explore to what extent the supplemental representation is accepted. This hinders the model to characterize depression cues. Therefore, we propose the Gradient Embedding (GE) and the Modal Complementarity (MC) modules. The GE module embeds the gradient into sequences to perceive the dynamics of elements, thereby helping to capture depression-related temporal patterns. Besides, we stipulate the supplementary representation as the difference between two modal representations. In this way, the MC module quantifies to what extent the supplemental representation is accepted by a weighted combination of unimodal representation and supplemental representation, thereby achieving effective modality complementarity. Experimental results (RMSE = 6.42/6.23, MSE = 5.06/5.00) on AVEC 2013 and AVEC 2014 datasets illustrate the progressiveness of our method.

title: All-Optical Microwave Frequency Division by Harmonic Frequency Locking
authors: Mohammad AlMulla
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: A microwave frequency divider based on an intensity-modulated, optically injected semiconductor laser is demonstrated. The division mechanism arises from the interaction between the externally applied modulation frequency and the period-one (P1) oscillation frequency induced by optical injection, allowing for fractional division of the input microwave signal. The achievable frequency division factors are determined by the numerators of the harmonic frequency-locked ratios, which follow a Farey-sequence relationship between the modulation frequency and the locked P1 oscillation frequency. This enables controlled generation of microwave outputs at rational subharmonics of the input frequency. Experimental results demonstrate division factors of 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7 by tuning the optical injection conditions. The divided microwave signals retain the spectral purity and stability of the injected modulation while providing access to lower, application-relevant frequencies. The proposed approach offers a simple, compact, and fully optical method for microwave frequency division and synthesis, eliminating the need for high-frequency electronic dividers or complex optoelectronic feedback loops.

title: NTL Detection: Multi-Modal Architecture for Smart Meter Fraud Detection in a Realistic Deployment
authors: Emiliano Acevedo, Martín Schmidt, Sara Silva, Camilo Mariño, Álvaro Gomez, Alicia Fernández, Alexander Martins, Gonzalo Caudullo, Mauricio Berois
publish: 2025 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies Conference - Latin America (ISGT LA)
abstract: Non-Technical Losses (NTLs) in power distribution pose persistent operational and financial challenges for utilities worldwide. This paper presents DeepDAICE, a multimodal deep learning architecture designed to detect NTLs by jointly modeling heterogeneous smart meter data, including low- and high-resolution consumption, alarm sequences, and contextual customer attributes. We compare DeepDAICE with a system based on a classical learning model currently deployed by Uruguay’s state-owned power utility (UTE), using two large-scale real-world datasets. We observe consistent improvements across both global and operational metrics. Additionally, results from field inspections validate the practical value of DeepDAICE, showcasing strong and complementary detection capabilities relative to the existing model. Its modular architecture and robust generalization make DeepDAICE a promising candidate for deployment and integration in real-world utility operations.

title: Explainable One-Class Machine Learning for Anomaly Detection in Hydro-Ecological Monitoring
authors: Maksym Palka, Nataliia Dziubanovska, Leonid Bytsyura, Anatoliy Sachenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: Water monitoring data are often irregular, incomplete, and lack reliable quality labels, which limits supervised methods. This paper proposes a station-specific, label-free one-class pipeline for multivariate anomaly detection in water-quality monitoring. Isolation Forest is trained separately for each station after completeness-based feature filtering, median imputation, and robust scaling. Detected events are explained using robust deviations, reporting the top contributing indicators for each anomaly. Experiments on 2023–2024 data from river reaches, reservoirs, and an estuary demonstrate data-driven anomaly counts across stations and interpretable indicator-level profiles suitable for expert review and operational validation.

title: Enhancing Chinese Listening Proficiency Through an Interactive Learning Approach with Edpuzzle
authors: Mingjuan Wang, Kannika Daungcharone, Krittawaya Thongkoo
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This study examines the effects of Edpuzzle, an interactive digital learning tool, on Chinese listening comprehension among Thai junior high school students. A quasi-experimental design with pre-test and post-test measures was employed to compare changes in listening proficiency between an experimental group that received Edpuzzle-based listening instruction and a control group that received traditional listening instruction. Listening proficiency was assessed using test tasks that involved both short and long dialogues, as well as comprehensive listening activities, with a focus on students' understanding of main ideas, details, and inferential meaning. The pre-test results indicated no significant difference between the two groups, suggesting comparable listening proficiency prior to the intervention. In contrast, the post-test results showed that the experimental group significantly outperformed the control group, indicating that the Edpuzzle-based intervention was associated with improved listening comprehension. These findings suggest that integrating interactive digital tools into conventional instruction may enhance student engagement and learning outcomes in Chinese listening. Future research is recommended to explore the longterm effects of such digital interventions and their applicability to other language skills.

title: Retraction Notice: Artificial Intelligence based Anxiety Detection in Patients
authors: Likhita Chowdary Koneru, Routhu Ramya Dedeepya, Angadi Lakshmi, G Kranthi Kumar
publish: 2022 Sixth International Conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)
abstract: Wellbeing and health refer to a condition of complete mental, social and physical prosperity that go beyond the absence of sickness or deficiency. When distanced from genuine prosperity, individuals will generally ignore material and mental concerns and illnesses. Depending on the depth of experiences and accidental complications faced by the individual, these ailments have a wide range of effects. Ideally, it makes it impossible for individuals to live normal, merry lives, and without doubt, and eventually leads to deterioration. It can similarly provoke steady mental degradation, obstacle, control, or self-hurt in specific conditions. This research study intends to design and develop an AI-based profound prosperity assist application with a straightforward UI, which can be used to explore and survey mental prosperity characteristics, for instance, pressure levels, following sensations of uneasiness and required treatment using data assessment procedures. Natural Language Processing (NLP) approaches are being used for serving an arrangement of infections, addictions, and various issues. These NLP ways of managing plan are utilized to train an AI-based application, which consolidates a Chatbot with the relational information index to attract clients dynamically and give them the best, speedy, and convincing responses for their inquiries.

title: Design and Implementation of a Hybrid-Powered Automatic Agricultural Produce Oven System
authors: Suwannee Panyot, Wirot Ponglangka, Niwat Nualkan, Kamonlak Chaidee
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This research aimed to develop a Hybrid Automatic Agricultural Dryer using combination of solar and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) energy to enhance drying efficiency and reduce energy costs. The developed system measures <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$192 \times 236 \times 100 ~\text{cm}$</tex> and equipped with a 2,300 kilocalorie (kcal) infrared gas burner and four drying trays sized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$90 \times 90 ~\text{cm}$</tex>. Experimental results from drying 20 kg of lemongrass showed that the hybrid system required only 8 hours with a final moisture content of 16.3 %, using 2 kg of LPG, compared with 10 hours and 4.1 kg of LPG for gas-only drying. The hybrid dryer reduced the drying time by 11.1 % and LPG consumption by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 1. 2 \%}$</tex>, demonstrating its effectiveness and suitability for community-level agricultural processing.

title: Air Quality Forecasting Using a Long Short-Term Memory Neural Network
authors: Ivan Rudavskyi, Halyna Klym
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: Accurate air quality forecasting is critical for environmental regulation and safeguarding public health. This study presents an analytical framework which uses a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network, enhanced with data imputation via the Expectation–Maximisation (EM) algorithm, as well as standardised pre-processing. The EM procedure reconstructs incomplete monitoring records to ensure a consistent and reliable dataset, while standardisation strengthens model robustness and facilitates efficient training. The LSTM architecture is used to identify temporal patterns in pollutant time series, and its forecasting ability is compared with that of several alternative models. Experimental findings suggest that combining imputation, normalization and deep learning techniques significantly enhances the accuracy of air quality forecasts and improves the overall reliability of the model.

title: The Principle of Matching Patch Elements in Broadband Antenna Array
authors: Yaroslav Gritsev, Olga Shcherbyna
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
