title: Characterizing Shale Brittleness and Organic Enrichment through Matrix Modulus Using Rock Physics-Based Elastic Impedance Inversion
authors: Xiaoyu Lv, Zhentao Sun, Huafeng Hu, Zhaoyun Zong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep shale gas, as a crucial unconventional energy resource, is assuming an increasingly significant role in securing energy supply and advancing the global shift toward low-carbon development. Nevertheless, deep shale reservoirs are typically characterized by pronounced heterogeneity, complex mineralogical composition, and significant organic enrichment, which make the simultaneous seismic-scale characterization of brittleness and organic matter particularly challenging. To overcome these difficulties, we present a rock physics–driven inversion framework that incorporates two diagnostic parameters: the matrix bulk modulus (Km) and the disturbance bulk modulus (ΔK). Km characterizes the inherent stiffness of the mineral matrix and demonstrates strong sensitivity to brittle mineral abundance, whereas ΔK captures the softening impact of organic matter on rock rigidity, thereby offering improved sensitivity to organic enrichment. By combining these two parameters, the respective influences of minerals and organic matter can be effectively disentangled at the seismic scale. Building upon this concept, we derive a new reflection coefficient expression formulated in terms of the matrix modulus and establish an elastic impedance inversion scheme. This framework enables the concurrent prediction of shale brittleness and organic enrichment from seismic observations. Rock physics analysis confirms that Km is highly sensitive to brittle mineral content, while ΔK exhibits a relative increase of up to 300% with rising organic matter fraction, significantly exceeding the response of conventional density (~20%) and P-wave impedance (~40%). Synthetic tests show over 95% consistency between the proposed reflectivity and the exact Zoeppritz solution within 30° incidence. Well-log validation yields correlation coefficients of 0.77 between Km and mineral brittleness index, and 0.81 between ΔK and organic matter content, both outperforming conventional elastic indicators. Field applications demonstrate that the method achieves higher prediction accuracy, better vertical resolution, and improved lateral continuity compared to existing techniques. Beyond shale gas, this methodology offers a broadly applicable framework for characterizing unconventional reservoirs, including shale oil, oil shale, tight hydrocarbons, and gas hydrates, highlighting its relevance for future energy exploration and development.

title: Retraction Notice: Comparative Analysis of Epilepsy Prediction using Machine Learning and Deep Learning
authors: Y. Sai Chandu, Shaik. Fathimabi
publish: 2022 Sixth International Conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)
abstract: One of the most common neurological illnesses is epilepsy. The life of an epileptic patient is impacted by the seizures. There are multiple challenges to identify the symptoms of various categories of epilepsy, and a number of researchers have developed solutions. This work focuses on the comparative analysis of epilepsy prediction using Machine Learning and Deep Learning. An experimental analysis is performed with eight machine learning algorithms and deep learning algorithms. In addition to that, the outcome shows which algorithm leads to good results, unlike AI calculations. LSTM deep learning algorithm has achieved more accurate results than the eight machine learning models.

title: Temporal Foveated Fluid Animation in Virtual Reality
authors: Yue Wang, Yan Zhang, Zhi Wang, Ziqi Liu, Xubo Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Simulating realistic fluids in virtual reality (VR) is computationally demanding, often limiting the scale and complexity of immersive environments. Existing foveated fluid simulation approaches primarily focus on spatial adaptivity. In this paper, we introduce a gaze-contingent fluid simulation system from a temporal perspective. We conduct a perceptual study to quantify the relationship between gaze eccentricity, fluid density deviation, and the perceptual threshold for simulation timesteps. Based on these findings, we fit a perceptual model that predicts the timestep requirements for maintaining perceptual realism in VR fluid animation. To exploit this model, we propose an asynchronous position-based fluids (PBF) algorithm that assigns fluid particles different local timesteps according to their visual importance and density deviation, ensuring both physical stability and perceptual validity. Our solver performs high-frequency updates in perceptually critical regions while progressively reducing updates elsewhere. A validation user study shows that our method remains perceptually indistinguishable from a high-fidelity, uniform-timestep PBF simulation. Objective evaluations further confirm that our approach improves efficiency while maintaining perceptual quality. Runtime experiments demonstrate speed-ups of up to 1.52× across diverse fluid scenarios, enabling more complex and larger-scale fluid phenomena in real-time VR. Our findings extend the paradigm of foveated fluid animation into the temporal domain, providing a perceptually grounded framework for fluid simulation in VR.

title: Cryogenic concept of a Low-Noise Amplifier using IHP130 nm BiCMOS technology
authors: Bao Ton, Damien Prêle, Manuel Gonzalez, Jean Mesquida, Didier Charrier, Si Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Cryogenic low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) are critical components for the readout of cryogenic sensors. Placing readout circuitry in close proximity to the detector at cryogenic temperatures minimizes the length of interconnections prior to the first amplification stage. Low-temperature operation can also significantly enhance LNA performance. However, microelectronics foundries do not typically provide device models for cryogenic operation. Therefore, characterization and validation of the technology at these temperatures are required before designing a full integrated circuit. This paper proposes a conceptual design for a cryogenic LNA optimized for the readout of a Superconducting Quantum Interference Device (SQUID). To manage power dissipation at cryogenic temperatures, the LNA is also designed for operation at intermediate temperatures. Consequently, the target operating temperature range is from 77K to 300K. The design focuses on a fully differential voltage amplifier to achieve high common-mode noise rejection. The LNA's first stage is based on a differential pair of SiGe heterojunction bipolar transistors (HBTs), selected for their low-noise and high-speed characteristics. Operating HBTs at cryogenic temperatures enhances both transconductance and current gain. The design is implemented in IHP's 130nm SiGe BiCMOS technology. Key cryogenic measurements, including current gain (β) and transconductance efficiency (gm/Ic), are presented and discussed.

title: Secure Difference Contraction Watermarking for Static Deep Neural Networks
authors: Shanxiang Lyu, Junren Qin, Fan Yang, Rongke Liu, Zhihua Xia, Xiaochun Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Static deep neural network (DNN) watermarking techniques typically employ irreversible methods to embed watermarks into the DNN model weights. However, this approach causes permanent damage to the watermarked model and fails to meet the requirements for integrity authentication. Reversible data hiding (RDH) methods offer a potential solution, but existing approaches suffer from limitations in usability, capacity, and fidelity, hindering their practical adoption. In this paper, we propose a secure static DNN watermarking scheme called Secure Difference Contraction (SDC). Our scheme utilizes a one-dimensional quantizer for watermark embedding and employs dithering to ensure key-dependent security, i.e., the watermark cannot be correctly extracted without the secret key used during embedding. Additionally, we design two schemes to address the challenges of integrity protection and legitimate authentication for DNNs. Simulation results on training loss and classification accuracy demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of our proposed methods, highlighting their advantages in capacity and fidelity over existing techniques.

title: Load Balancing via Deep Contour Detection in Edge-Driven Industrial Automation Systems
authors: Zhendong Chen, Chun Ouyang, Maolin Pan, Yang Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: In modern industrial production lines, efficient load balancing with real-time performance is crucial, but traditional methods struggle with dynamic workloads and complex system states. This paper proposes a deep learning-based online load balancing method for industrial motor control, integrating real-time sensor data analysis and dynamic load optimization at the edge device level. Our pipeline combines pressure sensor array data processing, intelligent pressure boundary extraction using an enhanced BASNet, and a real-time load balancing algorithm. By fine-tuning deep learning models on a custom industrial sensor dataset, the method achieves high-precision pressure pattern recognition and dynamic load optimization with low edge-device latency. We further propose an edge-based management architecture with RTOS integration and a high-priority message center for efficient real-time coordination. Experiments on a labscale production line show a 30% improvement over no-balancing systems and 7.8% over adaptive algorithms. The system’s low-latency processing meets industrial control requirements, with deep learning decision operations completing in approximately 2 ms and core manufacturing tasks processing within hundreds of nanoseconds. This research also contributes a practical solution for industrial load balancing that effectively combines deep learning capabilities with edge computing, enabling enhanced autonomous operation while ensuring system stability and efficiency.

title: Truthful Double Auction Mechanisms for Delay-Aware DNN Inference Offloading in Collaborative Edge Computing
authors: Dongkuo Wu, Xingwei Wang, Xueyi Wang, Qiang He, Siyu Yan, Min Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cloud Computing
abstract: Nowadays, the development of Collaborative Edge Computing (CEC) has greatly accelerated deep neural network (DNN) inference by enabling edge service providers (ESPs) in the JointCloud federation to deploy computational resources and well-trained DNN models at the edge of the network for collaborative inference with mobile devices (MDs), thereby promoting the rapid advancement of intelligent applications. This raises the need for an effective DNN inference offloading mechanism between MDs and ESPs. However, existing schemes often lack market efficiency and fail to ensure desirable economic properties. To this end, we propose a truthful double auction mechanism for delay-aware DNN inference offloading (TDAD), which integrates a dynamic programming approach with an adaptive resource allocation and pricing strategy to maximize social welfare while ensuring truthfulness, budget balance, and individual rationality. Specifically, TDAD first employs a binary search-based delay-aware partitioning and offloading method to determine the minimum feasible resource profile for each MD, and then applies a dynamic programming-based double auction to match MDs' inference demands with ESPs' combinatorial resources, and compute the corresponding payments and rewards. Theoretical analysis proves that TDAD satisfies the desired economic properties, while experiments in realistic CEC environments validate its effectiveness and efficiency.

title: Retraction Notice: QOS-Aware Task Scheduling in An Edge Environment
authors: R Subha, S Ananthi, B Gomathi, Suchithra, E Saranya, M. Abinaya
publish: 2023 9th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: In Edge frameworks, Virtual Machines (VM) are used for the asset usage as per the planning by the users. The most common example for this is accessible RAM, disregarding about the long haul usage. The planning and situation processes are computationally of high cost and the influence of execution is conveyed. In this paper, we propose a Edge VM planning calculation that considers previously running VM asset usage over the long run by the dissecting past VM usage levels to plan the current VMs. This is done by advancing the execution in the utilization of K-Means Nearest Neighbor (KNN) and Naive Bayes characterization procedure. The Euclidean distance of KNN is estimated and then the virtual machine is booked on the actual machine. The Edge in the board processes the VM situation & influences the previously sent frameworks so the point is to limit such execution corruption. In addition to this, over-burden of VMs will quite often take the assets from the adjoining VMs, so that the work boosts VMs genuine CPU usage. The outcome of our experimental setup shows the refinement in the conventional instant-based actual machine determination which learns the framework conduct as well as it adjusts over the long run. It also improvises the counts of the actual machines which gets diminished by the utilization of K-NN and NB classifier rather than traditional usage of Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier.

title: Retraction Notice: A Survey of Breast Cancer Detection Using Medical Imaging Techniques
authors: C. Ganesh, T. Jeevitha, A. Jayalakshmi, M. Shanthini, N. Senthil Kumar, P. Malini
publish: 2023 9th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: In this study, we examine the various medical imaging methods that were used to identify breast cancer. We analyse the efficacy, benefits, and drawbacks of each technique for detecting breast cancer in its early stages. Because MRI, ultrasound, and X-ray mammography are the main imaging modalities used in clinics to diagnose breast cancer, these are the areas on which we focus the most.

title: Retraction Notice: A Comprehensive Review of Air Quality Prediction Techniques: Methods, Performance and Challenges
authors: Suseela Sellamuthu, Niranjan, Ayush, Vaidik Manori, Yash Chaurasia, Prakarsh Mohan
publish: 2024 8th International Conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)
abstract: Air quality is what makes the places we live in habitable and affects the well-being and productivity of the people. Not paying attention to air quality can be dangerous, which has raised alarm among scientists, researchers, and the general public because it has been getting worse over the past decades. This paper will look at various methods and techniques for monitoring and predicting Air Quality Indexes (AQI) and how complicated it is to predict air quality exactly. Diverse machine learning models have been developed as well as data-driven approaches to improve the accuracy and credibility of air pollution forecasts. The dynamic nature of environmental conditions may explain why despite advances, predicting air quality remains a difficult task due to many interfering variables. This paper also proposes an approach for the creation of an efficient alert system. By using current data that offers predictive models that should help with time-sensitive real-time alerts about poor air quality to minimize any negative effects from such occurrences, these predictions should provide up-to-date results on air pollution indices. Such an alert system could be integrated with existing operational techniques, which would improve air quality management and as a result, the safety of life and productivity of individuals.

title: Hardware Means for Immediate Assessment of Radiation Exposure of the Civilian Population in the Radiation Accident Zone
authors: Sergii Ubizskii, Denis Afanassyev, Oleksandr Poshyvak, Vasyl Rabyk, Oksana Nebesniuk, Yaroslav Zhydachevskyy
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: This work is devoted to describing the development and features of an emergency dosimetry system designed to solve the problem of urgent assessment of the degree of radiation exposure of big number of civilians who are not equipped with personal dosimeters but find themselves in a radiation-affected area. The proposed concept assumes that the individual dose is assessed based on the time and place of the person's stay in the accident zone, and the distribution of absorbed doses in the accident zone is determined by readout the absorbed doses with passive dosimeters, pre-placed at specific local points of a grid covering the most likely locations of radiation accidents of man-made or terrorist origin – near nuclear facilities and in crowded places. The developed emergency dosimetry system is a key element in the implementation of this approach. Unlike known approaches of the retrospective dosimetry for reconstructing the individual doses of irradiated persons, the proposed concept allows for the triage of victims in the radiation accident zone within the first hours after its occurrence. This, in turn, not only allows for the provision of adequate medical care, but also serves to quickly assess the scale of the radiation accident and make timely management decisions regarding response and mitigation of consequences. The developed system also allows, in the event of a terrorist attack using radioactive materials that are unknown in advance, to identify the radioisotopes used while assessing the absorbed doses.

title: Combined Method Development the BLDC Electric Drive Model of Drone
authors: Bohdan Kopchak, Vira Oksentyuk, Andrii Mandiuk, Dmytro Kolesnik, Andrii Kushnir, Ihor Babii
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: A design approach for developing a new BLDC motor for a drone has been created and tested in Ansys Maxwell RMxprt based on several predefined parameters, including rated power, voltage, speed, torque, and current, followed by the required design calculations. To verify and assess the adequacy of the obtained results, a prototype motor was used for comparison. The distinguishing feature of the proposed approach is that a motor with similar key parameters is selected as a prototype; corrections at the initial stages are applied to the main dimensions, while at the final stages the geometry of the air gap is adjusted. The number of design iterations depends on the accuracy of the target motor parameters, which in this work was set and achieved with an error not exceeding 10%. A drone electric drive model based on the designed BLDC motor was developed in MATLAB Simulink using the results of the Ansys Maxwell RMxprt design calculations. The BLDC-based drone drive model was investigated in MATLAB Simulink under various operating conditions. The proposed approach demonstrated its effectiveness and potential for creating new motors and electric drive systems, particularly for drones, based on BLDC technology and for their further analysis. The conducted studies constitute a basis for developing an accurate model of a drone propeller electric drive. The propeller model is complex and highly sensitive to rotational speed. The studies presented in the article allow further transition to comprehensive investigations of drone thrust behavior.

title: Predicting Super-Class Drug Mechanisms from Large-Scale IC50 Cell-Line Sensitivity Profiles
authors: Tauhidur Rahman Sakib, Mostofa Adib Shakib, Saad Ibn Munir, Saiful Islam Badhon, Md. Sadman Yeasir Sakif, Mim Deb Roy
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: Predicting mechanisms of action of drug compounds at the super-class level is indispensable for early drug discovery and functional assessment of compounds. The aim of this study is to perform Super-Class–level mechanism-of-action prediction from phenotypic IC50 cell-line sensitivity data under strict leakage control. In this work, we present a leakage-safe machine learning pipeline for Super-Class Mechanism-of-Action (Super-Class MoA) prediction using drug–cell line sensitivity profiles prepared at large scale from the GDSC dataset. The standardized features derived from IC50 were encoded into 32-dimensional latent embeddings using an autoencoder, while SMOTE was applied solely to the training embeddings of each outer fold to address class imbalance. The resulting embeddings were used to train a stacked neural-boosted ensemble consisting of a neural network and XGBoost, with validation embeddings kept completely untouched to ensure unbiased evaluation. The framework achieved a mean accuracy of 0.9182, a mean macro-F1 score of 0.9118, and a mean macro-AUROC of 0.9568 across five outer folds, demonstrating consistent performance across the three Super-Class MoA categories. These results indicate that latent representations derived from phenotypic drug-response profiles capture biologically meaningful variation, supporting robust Super-Class MoA prediction in early-stage screening settings.

title: Embedded Machine Vision: Machine Learning and Performance Metrics
authors: Anton Holosha, Serhii Kovbasa, Sergey Edward Lyshevski
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: We design, evaluate and validate embedded machine vision modules for aerial systems and mobile robots. The developed methodology, learning algorithms, and end-to-end convolutional neural networks support robust learning. The compilers convert synthesized models into the neural network operator set format, and then into the executable optimized machine codes. We investigate resource-constrained system-of-systems sensing, communication and processing hardware. The models are executed by the neural processing units with AI accelerators. The developed performance metrics are evaluated to assess explainable, interpretable and achievable learning, performance and capabilities. The conducted fundamental, applied and experimental research focuses on development and evaluation of minimal-complexity algorithms to locate and recognize multiple objects, assess governance, ensure indexing and support dynamic tracking. Using the developed balanced datasets, the learning models are trained and implemented by single-forward-pass networks, such as the You Only Look Once and Detection Transformers. In realistic laboratory settings our findings are tested and verified. Performance metrics are evaluated for optically stabilized low quality cameras in harsh, denial and nefarious environments. The developed tools, middleware and hardware are aimed to advance processing, data management, information exploitation and intelligence sharing across multiple modalities and domains. For aerial and land platforms in complex dynamic environments, using prerecorded and live videos, capabilities and performance in multi-object recognition, indexing, tracking and governance are evaluated. Unique identifiers are assigned to differentiate detected objects within specific classes.

title: Contextual Multi Arm Bandit Framework for Adaptive Video Streaming
authors: Yuriy Zanichkovskyy, Larysa Hlinenko, Volodymyr Fast
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: Adaptive video streaming over heterogeneous networks remains challenging due to rapid and unpredictable variations in network conditions, especially when multiple delivery paths are available. Existing approaches typically optimize bitrate adaptation and path scheduling independently, leading to suboptimal Quality of Experience (QoE) in highly dynamic environments. This paper proposes a QoE-aware adaptive video streaming framework based on a Contextual Multi-Armed Bandit (CMAB) model that jointly selects video bitrate and delivery path in real time. The proposed approach incorporates rich application-level and network-level context, including playback buffer occupancy, available throughput, latency, throughput variance, packet loss, jitter, and historical decisions. By learning directly from observed QoE feedback, the system dynamically adapts to rapidly changing conditions across multiple network interfaces, such as Wi-Fi and cellular. Experimental evaluation under controlled network emulation demonstrates that the proposed CMAB-based joint decision framework consistently outperforms baseline schemes that decouple bitrate adaptation from multipath scheduling. The results show higher sustained video quality, fewer rebuffering events, improved bitrate stability, and more efficient utilization of heterogeneous network paths. These findings confirm that learning-driven joint optimization is an effective and practical solution for next-generation multipath adaptive video streaming.

title: Optimization of Acousto-Optic Interaction in ВTGS Crystals
authors: Pavlo Solomenchuk, Nataliya Demyanyshyn, Marian Andrushchak, Oleh Buryy, Oleksandr Lishchuk, Bohdan Mytsyk
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: The anisotropy of the acousto-optic figure-of-merit М<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> of the Ba<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>TaGa<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>Si<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">14</inf> (BTGS) crystal, which is promising for acousto-optic applications, is studied. The surfaces of the spatial distribution of the acousto-optic figure-of-merit for nine conditions of acousto-optic interaction and their stereographic projections are constructed. The maximum value of the acousto-optic figure-of-merit and the geometry of its observation for this crystal have been determined numerically. It has been shown that the value of М<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> for BTGS crystals is 30% higher than the best value of М<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> for other crystals of the langasite group, namely CTGS and LGS, and is three times higher than М<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> of quartz crystal. The most effective geometry of the acousto-optic interaction and an example of a sample cut from this crystal for an optimized acousto-optic cell are given.

title: Impact of Sampling Rate on the Performance of Power System Oscillation Detection Algorithms
authors: Cheolhun Im, Yoon-Seong Choi, Dongho Lee, Seongmin Pyo, Haechul Choi
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This study analyzes the effect of sampling rate on the oscillation detection performance of power systems using the MPM(Matrix Pencil Method) and HTLS(Hankel Total Least Squares) algorithms. Real PMU(Phasor Measurement Unit) data and PSS/E simulation data were used to compare the oscillation detection capabilities under various sampling rate conditions. The results showed that higher sampling rates generally improved detection accuracy, although the relationship was not strictly proportional. This study highlights the trade-off between data resolution and detection reliability.

title: Retraction Notice: Border Surveillance System using Computer Vision
authors: Ahamed Yasar Z., Dinesh Kumar R., S. Aadarsh, Hari Kumar G.
publish: 2020 6th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: The surveillance of border areas or any other secured places using security guards at each and every moments is difficult. It is made possible using digital cameras, a device which is used widely now a days for surveillance purposes. In such a case, the following paper defines the border surveillance system. This is done by computer vision. The requirements includes a camera, Raspberry-pi, Arduino-UNO and Buzzer. The digital camera is used to capture the live movements. The attained information/records are sent as an input to the raspberry pi which uses a computer vision (open CV) software to detect the objects and faces as positive and negative. The corresponding coding is done using python. If any mismatched objects or face (negative) is detected, automatically a signal is sent from the pi module. The desired signal is sent to the authority or monitoring room using transmitter. At one end, the Raspberry-pi module is connected with a monitor, transmitter and at the other end, a receiver with Arduino and buzzer. When any signal is received by a receiver, it is passed to the Arduino, which in turn triggers the buzzer connected to the Arduino and gives an alert signal or sound as it was programmed. In addition to this, the live streaming video can be seen in the monitor/display connected to the raspberry-pi. This surveillance system using computer vision can also be used at various places which are being under surveillances and are secured.

title: Collision-Free Cooperative Control for Heterogeneous Multi-Vehicle Systems With Connectivity Preservation: A Path-Guided Solution
authors: Xiaohui Yue, Huaguang Zhang, Juan Zhang, Zeyi Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Safe coordination of heterogeneous multi-vehicle systems in uncertain, cluttered, and communication-constrained environments poses a significant challenge in intelligent transportation and mission-critical applications. This paper addresses the collision-free formation-containment control problem with connectivity preservation under a unified path-following framework. A hierarchical two-layer control architecture is developed to address the heterogeneous dynamics of aerial and ground vehicles. In the leader layer, a three-dimensional path-following controller decouples spatial-temporal behaviors, guiding multiple aerial quadrotors to form a desired formation along a time-independent implicit path. In the follower layer, a distributed containment controller based on neighbor interactions ensures that ground vehicles converge within the convex hull spanned by the quadrotors. To handle system nonlinearities and unknown disturbances, a predictor-based neural observer (PNO) is proposed in both layers, using prediction errors to adaptively update the norm of neural weights, thereby enhancing transient estimation while reducing online computational burden. Artificial potential fields are also incorporated to guarantee collision avoidance and connectivity maintenance. The stability of the closed-loop system is proved by Lyapunov analysis. Simulation results substantiate that the proposed method ensures safe formation-containment, precise uncertainty estimation, and reliable collision avoidance under obstacle conditions, while comparative studies highlight the superiority of the proposed PNO in mitigating control chattering and reducing computational overhead.

title: A General Solution for Scaling Existing LCC-S WPT Systems to Higher Output Voltages
authors: Tabish Imtiaz, Saad Mekhilef, Marizan Mubin, Tey Kok Soon, Ahmed Elsanabary
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Enhancing gain in Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) systems traditionally requires redesigning key components such as the coil, compensation network, control strategies, or the inclusion of power converters and switched reactive networks. This paper presents a modular, interoperable and cost-effective solution for LCC-S compensated WPT systems that achieves voltage gain enhancement without the need for complex control mechanisms, offering a simplified and efficient alternative to existing methods.. The proposed passive network is inherently interoperable with any LCC-S WPT architecture, maintaining a consistent gain at the operating frequency regardless of coil parameters or loading conditions, while maintaining Zero Phase Angle (ZPA) operation to ensure high efficiency and stable power transfer. A 1 kW laboratory prototype demonstrates a fixed voltage gain of 2 at approximately 92% DC-DC efficiency, validating the network’s tuning-free performance across different output voltages and under x- and y-axis coil misalignments. The results confirm that the proposed boost-π network provides a compact, interoperable and modular practical solution for scalable high-power WPT applications.

title: Correntropy-Based Robust Blind Seismic Deconvolution
authors: Tales Câmara, Kenji Nose-Filho, Pedro O. A. Santos, Sérgio Luiz E. F. Da Silva, Bruno César Zanardo Honório, Jorge Luiz Matias, Samuel Xavier-de-Souza, Tiago Barros, Luiz F. Silveira
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Blind deconvolution is a well-established technique in seismic data processing, used to estimate subsurface reflectivity from recorded signals. However, traditional methods often rely on the ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-norm and assume Gaussian noise, which can limit their performance in real seismic environments, where impulsive and non-Gaussian noise may occur. In order to address this limitation, this work proposes a robust blind deconvolution approach based on a correntropy cost function. By replacing the traditional ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-norm with a correntropy criterion, the method accounts for higher-order statistical information and becomes robust to outliers and non-Gaussian noise. The algorithm alternates between estimating the reflectivity signal and the source wavelet, based on the Alternating Minimization Sparse Multichannel Blind Deconvolution (AM-SMBD) framework. The method is evaluated on synthetic data under noiseless, Gaussian, and impulsive noise conditions, as well as on real seismic records. Results show that the proposed approach performs similarly to the ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-based method under Gaussian noise and outperforms it in environments with α-stable non-Gaussian noise. It also was able to better preserve reflectors and attenuate noise in real seismic data.

title: Optical, Thermal and Mechanical Characterization of Standard and Thin-Clad Germanosilicate Single-Mode Fibers for Sensitivity-Enhanced Force Sensing With Fiber Bragg Gratings
authors: Fabian Buchfellner, Alexander Roehrl, Silas Heissel, Constanze Eulenkamp, Alexander W. Koch, Johannes Roths
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: We present a comprehensive optical, thermal, and mechanical characterization of two commercially available thin-clad germanosilicate single-mode fibers named “SM1500(7.8/80)P” and “SM1500(4.2/50)P” whose smaller cladding diameters are advantageous for fiber-based force sensing in comparison to a standard SMF28e+ fiber. The effective refractive indices were determined using a precise fiber Bragg grating (FBG) inscription-based method with precision in the order of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-5}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Moreover, a numerical approach reveals GeO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> doping levels of 3.42(5) mol% for SMF28e+, 5.92(8) mol% for SM1500(7.8/80)P, and 20.09(28) mol% for SM1500(4.2/50)P, which were validated via energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The increased GeO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> content in the thin-clad fibers correlates with their enhanced photosensitivity. The temperature and strain sensitivities of the fibers were quantified, showing only minor changes in temperature response and nearly constant strain coefficients across all samples. As expected, the force sensitivity increased significantly with decreasing cladding diameter, achieving a 2.4- fold and 6.1- fold enhancement for the 80 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mu }$</tex-math></inline-formula>m and 50 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mu }$</tex-math></inline-formula>m fibers, respectively, compared to SMF28e+. These enhancements, achieved without post-processing such as tapering or etching, preserve the mechanical and optical integrity of the fibers while improving their force-to-temperature sensitivity ratio. The findings demonstrate that thin-clad germanosilicate fibers are promising candidates for high-performance, temperature-compensated force sensing platforms compatible with standard telecom equipment and ultra-violet FBG fabrication technologies.

title: Surrogate Models in Power System Scheduling Problems: Learning Active Constraints via Graph Neural Networks
authors: Lingkang Jin, Laurens Bliek, Nikolaos G. Paterakis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid
abstract: Power system scheduling problems are solved repeatedly and require fast computation for real time deployment of asset setpoints. Bilevel optimization is often employed due to its ability to capture the hierarchical structure between network operators and end users. However, the inherent complexity of bilevel models makes repeated solutions computationally expensive. To address this, we propose a surrogate modeling approach that predicts the set of active constraints in the full bilevel formulation, thereby significantly reducing computational effort. Rather than relying on extensive sampling over uncertain parameters, we exploit the bipartite graph structure of optimization problems and apply Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to learn constraint activity directly by leveraging the intrinsic geometric dependencies of the optimization models. We evaluate the end to end approach by comparing the surrogate objective values and computational speedup against the original problem, benchmarking against Random Forest (RF) and Feed-foward Neural Network (FNN) classifiers across a range of increasingly complex test cases, from linear programming problems to mixed integer linear programming and bilevel problems. Results show that GNN based models, which encode mathematical properties like complementary slackness and binary activations to reduce model complexity, achieve up to 10-20x speedup with an objective deviation under 4% and a 99% optimality ratio. This represents superior performance to RFs and FNN, highlighting the suitability of GNNs for directly encoding the mathematical structure of optimization models, thanks to its inherent topology awareness to solve complex constraint activity prediction tasks in power system scheduling.

title: BeamStellar: Beaming Display with Low-Latency, 6-DoF Glasses Tracking via Spatio-Temporal LED Encoding
authors: Jonas Weigand, Christian Eichhorn, Yuichi Hiroi, Yuta Itoh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Conventional Augmented Reality (AR) head-mounted displays (HMDs) integrate all essential components within the wearable unit, imposing limitations on weight, heat dissipation, and battery life. Beaming Displays (BDs) address these constraints by externalizing computation and projection; however, achieving reliable low-latency six-degrees-of-freedom (6-DoF) tracking remains a challenge. We present BeamStellar, a BD system that enables low-latency 6-DoF head tracking by combining a position-sensitive detector (PSD) with a spatio-temporally modulated infrared LED array mounted on passive glasses. The system integrates tracking and projection via a co-aligned optical path and performs real-time signal processing on an FPGA to minimize tracking latency. We describe the system architecture, pose reconstruction pipeline, and evaluate the pose-tracking latency of approximately 430 μs. BeamStellar provides a replicable, lightweight AR tracking platform, establishing a new paradigm for low-latency environment-based AR systems.

title: Efficacy of High-Fidelity VR Threat-and-Error Simulation for Competency-based Pilot Training_supp1-3679896.mp4
authors: EngTat Khoo
publish: N/A
abstract: The aviation industry faces increasing pilot training demands, and reliance on conventional Full Flight Simulators (FFS) limits training capacity. Virtual Reality (VR) offers scalable, remote training opportunities, but its role as a complement to FFS requires empirical validation. In collaboration with Singapore Airlines (SIA) instructor pilots, we developed a VR training prototype for a visual approach into Gimhae International Airport, emphasizing Competency-Based Training and Assessment (CBTA)-based Threat and Error Management (TEM). An empirical study with 39 SIA Boeing 737-MAX 8 type-rated first officers evaluated VR against FFS. An equivalence analysis showed that VR achieved performance outcomes comparable to FFS in 13 of the 16 Observable Behaviors (OBs) and across 4 Competencies. In addition, a comparative analysis indicated measurable performance improvements when VR was used to supplement FFS training. Our results suggest VR can meaningfully complement FFS in targeted competency areas, with future work required to assess its broader integration across additional scenarios and performance metrics.

title: Transformer Based Multi-Target Bernoulli Tracking for Maritime Radar
authors: Caden Sweeney, Du Yong Kim, Branko Ristic, Brian Cheung
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Multi-target tracking in the maritime domain is a challenging problem due to the non-Gaussian and fluctuating characteristics of sea clutter. This article investigates the use of machine learning (ML) to detect and track weak targets in the maritime domain. The proposed method uses a transformer to extract point measurements from range-azimuth maps, before clustering and tracking using the Labelled multi-Bernoulli (LMB) filter. A birth density design using the transformer attention maps and prediction scores is also developed. The error performance of the transformer based approach is presented and compared with a constant false alarm rate (CFAR) detection technique. The LMB filter is run in two scenarios, an ideal birth approach, and the measurement driven birth approach. Experiments indicate that the transformer based method has superior performance to the CFAR approach for all target scenarios discussed.

title: Collaboration Under Competition Empirical Impacts on Motivation and Teamwork in a Multiplayer Programming Game
authors: Ratchanon Nobnop, Jetmanus Ratisaksoontorn, Pakakrong Panthanan, Yootthapong Tongpaeng
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Undergraduate programming courses that rely on lectures and individual assignments often struggle to keep students motivated and to foster real teamwork. This study introduces a multiplayer programming game that combines within-team collaboration with between-team competition through randomized, time-limited programming tasks. In a within-subjects pre–post study, with twenty-four students played in asymmetric roles: Debuggers solved tasks to fill a completion Bar, while Buggers solved their own tasks to unlock disruption abilities that slowed the opposing team. Outcomes were measured using a usability survey, perceived usefulness and behavioral intention of Technology Acceptance Measurement, Interest and Enjoyment of Intrinsic Motivation Inventory, and team cohesion. Paired t-tests showed significant improvements with large effect sizes on all measures, alongside reduced variability after gameplay. Overall, robust pre–post gains across usability, acceptance, motivation, and team cohesion together with reduced post-session variability indicate that the collaborative–competitive, role-asymmetric design is impactful and classroom-feasible. Replication at scale with coding-performance, retention, and ablation analyses is warranted.

title: Digital Twin Frameworks for Museum Curation and Adaptive Visitor Experiences
authors: Wai Yie Leong
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Digital Twin (DT) technologies are emerging as transformative enablers for next-generation cultural heritage management, offering real-time synchronization between physical museum assets and their virtual counterparts to enhance curation, conservation, and visitor engagement. This paper presents a comprehensive Digital Twin Framework for Museum Curation and Adaptive Visitor Experiences (DTMCAVE), integrating multimodal sensing, semantic asset modelling, AI-driven behavioural analytics, and adaptive experience orchestration. The proposed framework addresses limitations in traditional museum operations, including static exhibition layouts, non-personalized visitor pathways, and fragmented conservation records. Using a hybrid architecture combining Building Information Modelling (BIM), Internet of Things (IoT) sensors, and edge-cloud orchestration, the DTMCAVE model enables dynamic environmental monitoring, predictive conservation, and real-time visitor flow simulation. Machine learning models further support adaptive storytelling and personalized navigation based on cognitive engagement, mobility profiles, and interaction patterns. A case-study implementation conducted in a mid-scale heritage museum demonstrates improvements in curatorial decision-making, artefact risk detection, and visitor satisfaction scores. Quantitative results reveal reductions in conservation-related anomalies by 32 % and a 27 % increase in adaptive engagement metrics. This study advances DT research within the cultural heritage domain and provides a scalable pathway for smart, inclusive, and data-driven museum experiences.

title: UAV Based Cellular Network Coverage Optimization
authors: Kliment N. Angelov, Marin V. Nedelchev
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This article addresses the issue of using unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) as base stations for implementing new or supplementing the capabilities of existing terrestrial mobile cellular networks. In particular, the specifics of providing radio coverage are considered, considering parameters such as the radiation pattern and gain of the UAV antennas to achieve optimal coverage levels. Through such optimization of the antennas, better quality of service, lower levels of interference, more reliable handover, etc. can be achieved.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Deep Learning Neural Networks for Human Activity Recognition Using Wrist-Worn Inertial Sensor
authors: Sakorn Mekruksavanich, Wikanda Phaphan, Anuchit Jitpattanakul
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: The recognition of human actions from data captured by wearable inertial sensors has become a key component of modern healthcare applications, activity-monitoring systems, and assistive living solutions. This study presents a comparative evaluation of deep learning-based neural network models for categorizing routine daily activities using motion signals collected from wrist-worn sensors. Experiments are conducted on the publicly available WHARF dataset, which contains accelerometer and gyroscope recordings acquired during everyday human movements. The evaluated approaches include convolution-based models, sequential learning architectures, and hybrid designs that combine spatial feature extraction with temporal sequence modeling. Model effectiveness is assessed using classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-measure, and computational overhead, with particular attention to the trade-off between architectural complexity and recognition performance in resource-constrained wearable systems. *Corresponding author: Anuchit Jitpattanakul (anuchit.j@sci.kmutnb.ac.th)

title: Conformal Geometric Algebra Graph Learning Theory for 3D Human Skeleton Action
authors: Cao Wenming, Wang Bo, Hu Jing
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This study addresses the challenge of insufficient semantic expression in 3D human action skeleton structures, where Euclidean coordinate-based graph representations struggle to capture the complex semantic relationships between joints. To tackle this issue, a geometric algebrabased graph representation is first constructed based on geometric algebra theory. The effectiveness of a geometric algebra-based graph neighborhood aggregation method is subsequently designed and validated. Furthermore, a geometric algebra representation space for 3D human skeleton action data is established using geometric algebra graphs. Based on this, a multi-scale graph representation model for human skeletons is developed, enabling robust expression of complex semantics and effective feature extraction for 3D human skeleton actions. Finally, extensive experiments are conducted to validate and analyze the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: The Importance of Hospital Accreditation System in Thailand: Examining Its Role in Enhancing Service Quality, Patient Trust, and Outcomes for Wellness Tourism
authors: Sharmarke Farah, Santichai Wicha
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Hospital accreditation systems play a critical role in strengthening healthcare quality, patient trust, and service outcome factors that are particularly vital in wellness tourism destinations such as Thailand. This study explores the relationship between hospital accreditation and patient satisfaction by integrating open datasets from the Thailand Healthcare Accreditation Institute (HAI) and national patient experience surveys. Using merged data from accredited hospitals and patient feedback records, the study applies correlation and regression analyses to evaluate how accreditation status influences satisfaction across dimensions such as service quality, safety, staff professionalism, and treatment appropriateness. The findings reveal that accreditation has a statistically significant positive effect on patient satisfaction, even after controlling for hospital type, demographic characteristics, and healthcare schemes. Accredited hospitals consistently record higher mean satisfaction scores and demonstrate stronger performance across service and safety indicators. These results emphasize accreditation's dual function as a framework for quality assurance and as a strategic factor enhancing Thailand's global competitiveness in wellness tourism. While the study's primary focus remains on the relationship between accreditation and wellness tourism outcomes, it also outlines a potential future direction: the development of a web-based Hospital Accreditation Decision Support System (HADSS). This proposed system would support periodic performance monitoring tracking of accreditation performance and patient feedback analytics, promoting data-driven improvement, transparency, and long-term sustainability across Thailand's healthcare and wellness tourism ecosystem.

authors: S. Rajes Kannan, S Amutha
publish: 2020 6th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Recently, Wireless Mobile Networks (WMNs) square measure significantly created owing to the expansion of ease, wide offered remote cell phones. With the expanding kind of portable applications, similar to online life systems, GPS, cry, and so on, individuals' life has been indissoluble from cell phones that may get to the web at whenever and anyplace. title: Behavior and Sublinear Algorithm for Opinion Disagreement on Noisy Social Networks
authors: Wanyue Xu, Yubo Sun, Mingzhe Zhu, Zuobai Zhang, Zhongzhi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: The phenomenon of opinion disagreement has been empirically observed and reported in the literature, which is affected by various factors, such as the structure of social networks. An important discovery in network science is that most real-life networks, including social networks, are scale-free and sparse. In this paper, we study noisy opinion dynamics in sparse scale-free social networks to uncover the influence of power-law topology on opinion disagreement. We adopt the popular discrete-time DeGroot model for opinion dynamics in a graph, where nodes' opinions are subject to white noise. We first study opinion disagreement in many realistic and model networks with a scale-free topology, which approaches a constant, indicating that a scale-free structure is resistant to noise in the opinion dynamics. Moreover, existing algorithms for estimating opinion disagreement are computationally impractical for large-scale networks due to their high computational complexity. To solve this challenge, we introduce a sublinear-time algorithm to approximate this quantity with a theoretically guaranteed error. This algorithm efficiently simulates truncated random walks starting from a subset of nodes while preserving accurate estimation. Extensive experiments demonstrate its efficiency, accuracy, and scalability.

title: Safe Policy Optimization with Cost Practical Stability: A DQ-learning Method
authors: Chenyu Wang, Linkai Liu, Quan Quan
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Safety is a critical requirement in robotics, leading to extensive research in Safe Reinforcement Learning. While most existing methods utilize soft constraints to limit cumulative violations, robotic control scenarios demand that instantaneous safety costs also converge rapidly following a safety excursion. Inspired by practical stability from safety control, we formulate cost practical stability as a novel constraint. We introduce DQ-learning, an algorithm derived from D-learning, a differential predictive learning method ensuring system stability, to satisfy this constraint. DQ-learning merges the safety stability of D-learning with the reward optimality of Q-learning, incorporating this constraint within a policy optimization framework. Evaluations on multiple robotic safety benchmarks and a sim-to-real drone target-catching task demonstrate that DQ-learning significantly outperforms baseline methods in both constraint satisfaction and sample efficiency.

title: A Fast-Converging Hybrid ZQ Calibration Architecture with Charge Injection for Multi-Die High-Density Memory Interfaces
authors: Dong-Ho Shin, Jun-Ha Lee, Joon-Young Kim, Kang-Yoon Lee
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a fast-converging hybrid ZQ calibration architecture for multi-die high-density memory interfaces. In multi-die package systems, ZQ calibration latency increases because multiple dies share a single external reference resistor, making fast per-die calibration essential. To address this system-level challenge, the proposed architecture combines pre-driver voltage control for pull-up calibration with strength-code-based pull-down calibration, thereby reducing loop count and routing complexity while minimizing calibration time and chip area. In addition, shared reference loading increases the effective capacitance at the ZQ node, degrading voltage transition speed and limiting the operating frequency of the ZQ loop. To mitigate this physical limitation, a ZQ charge injection equalizer accelerates the node transition, achieving up to 95.6% improvement in the maximum operating frequency of the ZQ loop under 32-load conditions based on post-layout simulations. Furthermore, a reference-voltage-switching-based offset cancellation scheme removes comparator offset within 2 ZQ clock cycles, thereby minimizing calibration time overhead while enhancing calibration accuracy and statistical consistency across chips. Fabricated in a 28-nm CMOS process, the circuit operates at 1.0 V/1.8 V (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CC</sub>Q/V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CC</sub>), occupies 0.0303 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, and achieves 160 ns calibration time at 100 MHz with a maximum error of 3.9 mV across 20 measured chips.

title: Time-Varying Inductance Emulating Control of DFIG for Fast Demagnetization During Grid Faults
authors: Donghai Zhu, Lingwei Ren, Jiabing Hu, Haipeng Zhu, Yu Shen, Yong Kang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: During grid faults, fast demagnetization is crucial for the safe operation and reactive power support of doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG). However, existing demagnetization methods employing fixed control parameters fail to unlock the potential for fast demagnetization. To cope with it, this letter proposes a time-varying inductance emulating control that makes the rotor side of DFIG emulate an inductance with a time-varying value. The proposed method significantly accelerates the decay of stator flux while ensuring safe demagnetization within current and voltage constraints. Finally, experimental results validate the proposed method.

title: STaRQ-Agent - Investigating Generalization in Knowledge Graph Question Answering via A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
authors: Longquan Jiang, Debayan Banerjee, Ricardo Usbeck
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Generalization to different Knowledge Graphs (KGs) is a core challenge of Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA). Current models struggle to adapt to unseen KGs due to their underlying heterogeneity. Most approaches depend on data-hungry methods, such as supervised fine-tuning and KG-specific few-shot demonstrations. These systems are limited in cross-KG generalization, and may exhibit hallucination problems, especially in low-resource or domain-specific scenarios. This paper presents STaRQ-Agent, an LLM-based multi-agent collaborative framework for the KGQA generalization problem. It comprises a core agent for SPARQL query generation, accompanied by three supportive agents for schema selection, template generation, and query refinement. This collaborative framework helps mitigate hallucination problems inherent in LLMs. STaRQ-Agent was evaluated on LC-QuAD 1.0, QALD-9, MetaQA, and MatKGQA datasets in few-shot and zero-shot settings. Experimental results demonstrate strong overall generalization across diverse KGs, without requiring high-quality annotated data, re-training, or fine-tuning. Specially, on LC-QuAD 1.0, QALD-9, and MetaQA, few-shot STaRQ-Agent outperforms the strongest baseline by 5.1%, 4.3%, and 0.7%, while zero-shot STaRQ-Agent shows only modest F1 drops of 8.7%, 12.3%, and 10.8%. Moreover, the state-of-the-art results on MatKGQA show its robust out-of-distribution generalization to novel KGs in low-resource specific domains.

title: STENet: Superpixel Token Enhancing Network for RGB-D Salient Object Detection
authors: Jianlin Chen, Gongyang Li, Zhijiang Zhang, Liang Chang, Dan Zeng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Transformer-based methods for RGB-D Salient Object Detection (SOD) have gained significant interest, owing to the transformer's exceptional capacity to capture long-range pixel dependencies. Nevertheless, current RGB-D SOD methods face challenges, such as the quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism and the limited local detail extraction. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel Superpixel Token Enhancing Network (STENet), which introduces super pixels into cross-modal interaction. STENet follows the two stream encoder-decoder structure. Its cores are two tailored superpixel-driven cross-modal interaction modules, responsible for global and local feature enhancement. Specifically, we update the superpixel generation method by expanding the neighborhood range of each superpixel, allowing for flexible transformation between pixels and superpixels. With the updated superpixel generation method, we first propose the Superpixel Attention Global Enhancing Module to model the global pixel-to-superpixel relationship rather than the traditional global pixel-to-pixel relationship, which can capture region-level information and reduce computational complexity. We also propose the Superpixel Attention Local Refining Module, which leverages pixel similarity within superpixels to filter out a subset of pixels (i.e., local pixels) and then performs feature enhancement on these local pixels, thereby capturing concerned local details. Furthermore, we fuse the globally and locally enhanced features along with the cross scale features to achieve comprehensive feature representation. Experiments on seven RGB-D SOD datasets reveal that our STENet achieves competitive performance compared to state of-the-art methods.

title: A Fast-Charging Inductive-Capacitive Dual-Mode Orthogonal Orientation-Independent Switched-Mode Wireless Power Transfer System for Battery-Less Implantable Medical Devices in 65nm CMOS
authors: Gourab Barik, Sukriti Shaw, Baibhab Chatterjee, Shreyas Sen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems
abstract: Batteryless implantable medical devices (IMDs) require tens of μW to mW-level power while operating under stringent size constraints, uncertain post-implant orientation, and high body-channel attenuation that forces high-ratio voltage multiplication and slow energy accumulation. This paper presents a Inductive-Capacitive dual mode wireless power transfer (WPT) system that improves charging latency and link robustness by combining three techniques: (i) a split rectifier (REC) architecture with temporal energy combining to mitigate stage-leakage and accelerate energy accumulation, (ii) an orthogonal coil-fed cuboid receiver that provides spatially neutral (orientation-independent) operation, and (iii) dual-mode inductive–capacitive powering by reusing the same conductors as both inductive coils and capacitive electrodes. A load-isolating switch (LIS) further suppresses leakage during startup, reducing the average load-leakage. Fabricated in 65-nm CMOS, the 0.19 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> prototype achieves ~3.4× faster charging compared to the TEG/solar based prior-art design. In addition, under identical input conditions of –12 dBm at 70 MHz, the proposed 4 × 60-stage split-rectifier architecture demonstrates approximately 4× reduction in charging time compared to a conventional 240-stage implementation. The WPT system achieves a minimum input power sensitivity of –26 dBm (2.5 μW) and operates with input amplitudes down to ~30 mV, enabling compact, faststarting, and spatially robust wireless powering for IMDs.

title: Positional Prompts-Enhanced Brain-Heart-Gut Interactions for Mild Cognitive Impairment Diagnosis
authors: Fan Li, Shilun Zhao, Shuwei Bai, Dengqiang Jia, Fang Xie, Jiangtao Liang, Han Zhang, Ya Zhang, Zhongxiang Ding, Yin Xu, Kaicong Sun, Dinggang Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging
abstract: Mild cognitive impairment (MCI) is the prodromal stage of dementia involving complex interactions between the brain and peripheral organs. Emerging evidence indicates that heart dysfunction and gut microbiota dysbiosis can contribute to MCI pathogenesis. Yet, these discoveries of cross-organ interactions have not been applied to assist MCI diagnosis. In this work, we propose a novel diagnostic framework that exploits the interactions of brain, heart, and gut using whole-body PET images to guide MCI diagnosis for scenarios when only brain MRI, PET, or PET&MRI are available. Specifically, we collected a multi-cohort, multi-modal dataset comprising 1,545 whole-body PET images, 6,010 brain MR images, and 2,446 brain PET images from eight data centers. Organ-specific image encoders are first pretrained for the brain, heart, and gut individually. Then, to effectively align and integrate brain, heart, and gut features, we introduce positional prompts to act as anatomical-level attention to highlight disease-relevant spatial regions, and further develop hierarchical Transformers to model brain-heart, brain-gut, and brain-heart-gut interactions. Finally, to achieve MCI diagnosis using only brain images, we transfer the above brain-heart-gut model to a brain-only model via an introduced multi-level knowledge distillation scheme, including sample-level contrastive distillation, group-level distribution alignment, and response-level supervision. Extensive experiments on multi-center data demonstrate the superiority of our method over the state-of-the-art methods by resorting to effective integration of heart and gut interactions for MCI diagnosis.

title: Cross-dimensional Spatial-temporal Feature Integration Framework for Lung Ultrasound Video Analysis in Pneumonia
authors: Yiwen Liu, Chao He, Dongni Hou, Dean Ta, Mingbo Zhao, Wenyu Xing
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging
abstract: Pneumonia is an acute respiratory infection, posing a serious threat to health and lives. Lung ultrasound (LUS), as a non-invasive and rapid imaging technique, can monitor real-time changes in lung, providing valuable assistance in clinical diagnosis. However, most LUS studies are limited to frame-level analysis and ignore respiratory cycle changes, leading to diagnostic errors. To address these problems, we propose a cross-dimensional spatial-temporal feature integration model for LUS video analysis. Specifically, the sliding window and feature difference analysis are first utilized to preprocess the original LUS videos for eliminating invalid and highly similar frames and implementing abstract video. Subsequently, a cross-dimensional feature fusion backbone integrates an improved temporal-C3D network and a self-designed recursive inception-meet-transformer (IMT) network to extract features from different dimensions for fusion. Thereby, comprehensive features can be obtained for characterizing LUS videos. Finally, the Longformer is employed to analyze the temporal dependencies of cross-dimensional features, supplemented by a classification head for evaluating LUS videos. 3018 LUS video clips were collected from 119 patients in three hospitals for the evaluation of the proposed LUS video scoring model. By dividing at the patient level, the training and testing set consist of 2652 clips from 104 patients and 366 clips from 15 patients, respectively. Experimental results of 5-fold cross validation demonstrate that the proposed model achieves outstanding scoring performance, with an accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F1-score, and AUC of 91.78 ± 0.52%, 92.19 ± 0.76%, 91.81 ± 0.62%, 97.17 ± 0.20%, 91.94 ± 0.44%, and 97.83 ± 0.19%, respectively. The independent testing set also shows the superior generalization capability with a scoring accuracy of 87.65 ± 1.12%. Moreover, ablation studies confirm that each designed module contributes significantly to the model’s performance, and comparative experiments further confirm the superiority of the proposed model compared to previous models. These robust findings highlight the proposed LUS video scoring model’s strong potential for clinical deployment.
