title: Comparative Evaluation of Thai Speech-To-Text Models and Entity Extraction Methods for Beverage Ordering Systems
authors: Trintanut Charoenpool, Supakpong Jinarat
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Deploying robust voice-based ordering systems for tonal languages like Thai requires balancing transcription fidelity with real-time responsiveness. This paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of twelve end-to-end pipeline configurations, combining three Speech-toText (STT) architectures-OpenAI Whisper, FasterWhisper, and Biodatlab-Whisper-with four entity extraction strategies. We benchmark performance on a specialized beverage-ordering domain using an independent test set of 50 utterances (comprising both real-world recordings and AI-generated samples). Experimental results demonstrate that Faster-Whisper optimizes the latency-accuracy trade-off, achieving the lowest transcription latency via its optimized inference engine. Crucially, we address the issue of cascading errors by implementing a domain-adapted Custom NER model, fine-tuned on a synthetic corpus of 201,245 labeled samples. This proposed model significantly mitigates upstream transcription noise, achieving a peak entity-wise accuracy of 77.5 %, drastically outperforming rule-based and general-purpose baselines. These findings offer actionable architectural guidelines for building scalable, real-time Thai voice interfaces.

title: Quantitative Assessment of the Survivability of a Mission-Critical System with a Hierarchical Structure
authors: Leonid Ozirkovskyy, Bohdan Volochii, Nazar Pryymak, Yuriy Zhuk
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: A complete methodology for analyzing hierarchical structures by the logic-probabilistic trajectory modelling method is presented. The method forms a set of registration packets that traverse the structure according to the rules of first/repeated visits, downward only moves, returns, and accumulation of probabilistic information. Grouping the packets by the number of "nodes" yields R(t) and the mean time to failure T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MTTF</inf>. For software implementation, the method is formalized using an auxiliary "family tree" structure that governs packet switching. A complete methodology for analyzing complex hierarchical structures by the logic-probabilistic trajectory modelling method is presented. Validation of the results is performed using the characteristic polynomial method, the classical approach based on generating functions, and additionally the probability generating function method, which provides a compact representation of the distribution of the number of accessible lower-level nodes and of threshold survivability indicators.

title: Optical Properties of Ordered Metal-Dielectric Nanocomposites with Axially Symmetric Inclusions
authors: Andrii Korotun, Liliia Abramenko, Valery Kurbatsky
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: This paper examines the optical properties of ordered nanocomposites in various spectral ranges as potential candidates for ENG/MNG or DNG materials. Expressions are derived for the effective dielectric and magnetic permeability of a composite with metallic inclusions in the form of dimers of prolate spheroidal nanoparticles. Numerical experiments have shown that metal-dielectric composites of this type cannot be DNG materials at any inclusion sizes, but they are ENG and MNG materials in certain narrow spectral regions.

title: Retraction Notice: Survey on Automatic Temperature and Face Detection using AI
authors: Muthu V, Kavitha S
publish: 2021 Fifth International Conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)
abstract: The artificial intelligence is a computer expertise which thinks like human being and it does not need human intellect. The artificial intelligence could be categorized as: (i)Reactive machine, (ii)Machines with limited memory, (iii)Machines with a theory of mind, and (iv)Machines with self-awareness. The different applications of artificial intelligence are speech recognition, robot process automation, decision management, etc. The input given is in the form of images, videos, and sound data. The image, video is taken using high-resolution cameras like conventional thermal camera, visible IP camera, and AI-enabled thermal camera. With the advent of artificial intelligence variety of automated detection like thermal temperature detection, infrared temperature detection, mask recognition detection, computer vision was introduced and used. This survey presents a assessment of artificial intelligence methods used in the detection and recognition of face and also for testing fever. A series of algorithms like independent component analysis, local binary pattern histogram, ADA boost cascade, squirrel search, HOG, face detection and recognition in the literature. This paper highlights the automatic detection of body temperature, facial temperature and room temperature using artificial intelligence as an effective endurance. Persons with fever in public places could be identified and proper action could be taken in advance. This study is expected to provide researchers in AI a general idea of the present the current state of AI applications and inspire them in exploiting In the fight against illnesses like COVID-19, AI has a lot of promise.

title: Retraction Notice: Detection of Face Mask Using Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Ishwarya TM, K. Naveen Durai
publish: 2022 8th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: According to data acquired by the World Health Organization, the worldwide universal of COVID-19 bears harshly hit the realm and bears immediately contaminate eight heaps of human beings in general. Wearing face masks and following cautious public leave behind are two of the embellished protection from harm rules of conduct that need to take the place of honestly held places in consideration of keeping from happening or continuing the spread of the virus. To develop in mind or physically conservative surroundings that contribute to public protection from harm, we suggest an adept data processing machine located in close contact with the genuine in existence-period made or done by a human being to discover two reliable public dissociate themselves and face masks honestly placed by the model ahead of the start of the model to monitor special interests or pursuits and discover rape through photographic equipment. In addition to presenting an alarm to the public, in this proposed structure, we have designed mask detection along which indicates people to wear their mask properly before permitting in to the area which they prefer. We have used machine learning with supports the accuracy for the prediction.

title: MMRD-DETR: An RGB–Depth Multimodal Fusion Framework for Defect Detection of Transmission Line Conductors and Ground Wires
authors: Xueming Zhou, Rui Jia, Yao Yao, Jianjin Fu, Zhiqiang Feng, Xiankang Wang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Defect detection in UAV-based inspection of transmission line conductors and ground wires often suffers from limited accuracy and reduced robustness when relying solely on single-modal imagery. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes MMRD-DETR, a multimodal defect detection framework centered on a Dual-Stream Wavelet Fusion Backbone. The proposed backbone performs frequency-domain decomposition and structured cross-modal alignment between RGB and depth features at the feature extraction stage, enabling effective integration of geometric structural information and texture cues. A dual-modal adaptive interactive encoder is designed to enhance the model's capability in processing dual-modal features and enhance stability. Furthermore, an Interact-Clean-boost fusion model is developed as the fusion layer to further improve model accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that MMRD-DETR achieves a 4.8% improvement in mAP50 and a 4.5% improvement in overall mAP compared to the original single-modal model on conductor and ground wires defect datasets under challenging conditions. This algorithm provides an efficient and robust solution for multimodal defect detection in conductor and ground wires inspection.

title: Three-Dimensional Circumnavigating Control of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles Using Range-Only Measurements
authors: Xuancheng You, Baoli Ma, Lixia Yan
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: This letter investigates the three-dimensional (3D) circumnavigating control for a single unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) using only range measurements in GPS-denied environments. The core challenge lies in the unavailability of bearing information, rendering traditional control laws ineffective. To formulate the control design, we propose a hierarchical motion decomposition strategy by introducing a virtual anchor, which conceptualizes the UAV's movement as a superposition of orbital and translational motions. Based on this formulation, a global circumnavigation control law is developed by fusing a nonlinear observer with the system dynamics. A unified stability analysis is presented to guarantee that the circumnavigation errors converge to zero from any initial state, without relying on the separation principle. Numerical simulations and field experiments are performed to validate the theoretical results.

title: Linear Array Partitioning With Minimum Number of Subarrays: An Integer Programming Approach
authors: Cheng Liu, Xuejing Zhang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents an integer programming framework for partitioning a linear array into contiguous, non-overlapping subarrays while minimizing their total number. The proposed method transforms the partitioning task into a tractable integer programming problem by introducing binary selection variables for candidate subarray configurations. Unlike existing techniques, this formulation does not require predefined subarray counts or reference patterns, offering greater design flexibility. The framework supports uniform excitation for high directivity as well as window-tapered excitation for enhanced sidelobe suppression. Moreover, it can be readily extended to limited-scanning scenarios. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves superior performance in directive gain, scan range, and sidelobe control compared to state-of-the-art methods. The method is scalable to large arrays, where high-quality solutions can be efficiently obtained within acceptable time using off-the-shelf solvers.

title: MAG-KD: Mask-Guided Adaptive Gating Knowledge Distillation for Skeleton-Based Sign Language Recognition on Edge Devices
authors: Dao Tuan Dat Vo, Van Dang Nguyen, Tran Hao Minh Nguyen, Minh Huan Vo
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: While artificial intelligence has significantly advanced sign language recognition (SLR), high-performing models often entail substantial computational complexity. This creates a significant barrier to deploying real-time applications on resource-constrained edge hardware. While knowledge distillation (KD) offers a potential solution, existing multi-teacher strategies are predominantly tailored for static images and remain unoptimized for skeleton-based graph convolutional networks (GCNs), which are inherently sensitive to pose estimation noise and require modeling complex temporal-semantic dependencies. To address this, we propose Mask-Guided Adaptive Gating Knowledge Distillation (MAG-KD), a framework specifically tailored for skeleton-based GCNs. Our approach adopts a decoupled distillation strategy, integrating a Mask-Guided Filtering Mechanism based on correctness-aware principles to strictly filter noisy supervision, and an Adaptive Gating Network leveraging context-aware features to dynamically synthesize teacher knowledge based on input complexity. Extensive evaluations across multiple random seeds demonstrate the superior stability of our approach. On the AUTSL dataset, our lightweight MAG-KD student (Student-M, 0.48M parameters) achieves a highly robust Top-1 mean accuracy of 94.25% ± 0.19. Furthermore, our method proves strong cross-dataset generalization by achieving 66.33% ± 0.54 on the WLASL-100 subset, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art KD methods. Comprehensive hardware profiling on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano confirms optimal edge feasibility: the deployed model achieves a real-time inference speed of 33.13 frames per second (FPS) while maintaining a highly efficient power profile of merely 6.23W. Extended analysis indicates that the proposed algorithm operates optimally within the standard edge model regime, whereas static ensemble strategies remain more effective for ultra-compressed architectures with fewer than 100k parameters.

title: Photonic Convolutional Paradigm in Synthetic Frequency Dimensions
authors: Kangpeng Ye, Lu Zhang, Jiahao Zhang, Zhidong Lyu, Zhanhong Wang, Xiaodan Pang, Oskars Ozolins, Xianbin Yu
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Photonic convolution is a cornerstone for broadband artificial intelligence hardware, yet existing implementations often suffer from low computational density, requiring numerous wavelength channels. Here, we propose a resonator-free convolutional architecture in the synthetic frequency dimension, marking a paradigm shift from isolated spectral transmission to coupled spectral computation. By programming the driving waveforms of an IQ modulator and synchronizing symbol durations with time delays, we achieve arbitrary, high-precision, and reconfigurable convolutional kernels without iterative optimization. This approach increases the multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations per symbol per wavelength from 1 to N2 for an N×N kernel, enhancing computational density while reducing laser source requirements. In a proof-of-concept experiment, we demonstrate Gaussian kernel-based feature extraction of a barcode image with a root mean square error of 0.073, validating the feasibility and accuracy of our architecture. This work establishes a new physical paradigm for high-density photonic computing hardware.

title: A Physics-Constrained Neural Network for Electromagnetic Inverse Scattering Imaging
authors: Yunyun Hu, Wei Yang, Qing He, Rencheng Song, Yuting Zhang, Qian Huang, Meisong Tong, Qingtao Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: To accurately and robustly solve electromagnetic inverse scattering problems, a physics-constrained neural network (PCNN) method is proposed in this work. The method adopts Born iteration framework, where a neural network is embedded in each iteration to solve the optimization problem. It combines the linearization capability of traditional inverse methods with the powerful capability of neural networks in solving complex optimization tasks. In the neural network, the L2 norm of data equation is adopted as the loss function. Since the data equation directly corresponds to the target inverse problem, it provides an effective physical constraint that guides the network toward the correct solution. Furthermore, a constraint layer is incorporated in the network to restrict the solution within predefined physical bounds, thereby narrowing the feasible solution space and improving inversion stability. The synthetic and experimental results show that the proposed PCNN method consistently achieves lower RMSE and data error and higher SSIM than conventional BIM, PINN-based methods and U-Net-based supervised learning approaches, demonstrating its superior accuracy in reconstruction. These results confirm that the PCNN offers an effective and robust unsupervised learningbased approach for nonlinear electromagnetic inverse scattering problems.

title: UpDown: A Supercomputer Co-designed for Scalable Graph Processing
authors: Andrew A. Chien, Charles Colley, Jianru Ding, Alexander Fell, David F. Gleich, Henry Hoffmann, Moubarak Jeje, Rajat Khandelwal, Yanjing Li, Jose M Monsalve-Diaz, Marziyeh Nourian, Andronicus Rajasukumar, Jiya Su, Tianshuo Su, Yuqing Wang, Wenyi Wang, Ruiqi Xu, Tianchi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: Traditional supercomputers have focused on dense computation performance as exemplified by HPL. Graph processing applications differ with extreme irregularity (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{9}$</tex-math></inline-formula> imbalance in skewed, real-world graphs) that produces unpredictable work, parallelism, memory access, and communication. Together, these make scalable performance and programming difficult. We describe the UpDown system architecture, co-designed for irregular graph computations. UpDown provides efficient fine-grained thread invocations (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula>10 instructions), direct messaging (no network interface card) for scalable local and global messaging, and split-transaction memory operations that enable extremely high memory bandwidth. Combined with architectural support for global addressing and an aggressive network design, these UpDown features enable direct exploitation of edge and vertex parallelism, using it to deliver breakthrough graph processing performance and programmability. We evaluate the performance of the UpDown system using a challenging suite of graph applications (Pagerank, Breadth-first Search, Triangle Counting, Partial Match, etc). For a single-node, results show 100-fold performance advantage over multicore CPUs. Compared to today's fastest scalable parallel computers UpDown achieves 1000-fold performance increases. UpDown delivers these levels of performance with high-level programmability, these programs directly express vertex-edge parallelism which UpDown exploits directly in hardware.

title: Traffic Signal-Vehicle Cooperative Control via Multimodal Heterogeneous Subgraph Learning
authors: Bao-Lin Ye, Peng Wu, Lingxi Li, Weimin Wu, Bo Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Existing traffic signal control methods primarily rely on data from a single sensor modality, limiting their ability to comprehensively capture the spatiotemporal features of the traffic network. This leads to a mismatch between traffic signal timing plans and dynamic traffic demands, resulting in underutilization of road capacity. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a traffic signal-vehicle cooperative control method (SVCM) based on multimodal heterogeneous subgraph learning. Specifically, we first design a hybrid-strategy action space with dual-state transitions, enabling the agent to dynamically switch between traffic signal control and cooperative traffic signal-vehicle control schemes according to real-time traffic conditions. Then, by analyzing vehicle steering requirements, driving efficiency factors, and vehicle heterogeneity, we construct a multi-factor weighted decision-making model for vehicle lane-changing. It adjusts the lane-changing probability to balance traffic density among lanes. In addition, to enhance the perception and utilization of the spatiotemporal features of the road network, we designed a multimodal heterogeneous subgraph attention network to integrate multi-source traffic data. This approach more accurately captures key regional features and provides a foundation for state information perception in the aforementioned collaborative control strategies. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method, SVCM, outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both simulated and real-world traffic scenarios. Compared with the best baseline method, SVCM reduces the average queue length by 24.38% to 47.14% and decreases average lane occupancy by 21.09% to 39.72%. This work provides new insights into traffic signal-vehicle cooperative control by integrating multimodal feature information.

title: Calculating the Grid: A Late 20th Century Story of Innovation, Transition, and Reciprocity in Electrification and Computing
authors: Julie Cohn, Daniel Molzahn, Sairaj Dhople
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing
abstract: The National Academy of Engineering classified widespread electrification as the greatest engineering accomplishment of the 20th century. Innovation in computing technologies proved essential to this achievement. Additionally, as engineers adapted computer hardware and software for grid control, they contributed to advances in computation. The traditional story of electrification follows steady expansion from small central station power systems to continental-scale interconnected grids. Yet, transitions triggered by blackouts, evolving energy resource mixes and patterns of use, regulatory restructuring, and technological innovation punctuated this history. System operators faced a consistent challenge—keeping the lights on—as transitions threatened to undermine grid stability and efficiency. Today we are in the midst of another, perhaps more profound, and certainly more rapid transition. Additionally, computation itself poses both challenges and solutions for grid stability. This paper analyzes how 20th-century engineers and system operators developed the algorithms that provide optimization and control of the electric power grid.
