title: Development of a Hexad-Driven Motivational E-Learning System with AI-Based Personalization: Enhancing Engagement and Digital Literacy Learning Outcomes
authors: Phimphakan Thongthip, Pakinee Ariya, Kitti Puritat
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Digital literacy, a complex 21st-century skill, often suffers from low engagement in standard e-learning. To address this gap, we designed and evaluated a Hexad-informed motivational e-learning system incorporating a rule-based AI personalization engine. In contrast to generic gamification, the inference engine classifies learners into specific Hexad user types and delivers tailored motivational scaffolds in real time. We report a study at Yupparaj Wittayalai School (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${n}={1 0 0}$</tex>) showing that Hexad-personalized e-learning outperformed a generic gamified control in digital literacy scores (29.20 vs. 25.98; <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p}<0.05$</tex>) and behavioral engagement. In this paper, we detail an architecture that integrates expert-system logic and the Hexad model to coordinate knowledge delivery. The findings demonstrate that rule-based AI personalization offers a scalable, effective means of improving digital literacy education.

title: Tapered Transitions from Double-Sided to Balanced Microstrip Line
authors: Yulia Rassokhina, Vladimir Krizhanovski, Dmitrii Chernov
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: The paper presents a study of the dependence of the dispersion characteristics (wave deceleration coefficient and characteristic impedance) of tapered exponential transitions from a double-sided microstrip to a balanced transmission line on the longitudinal coordinate. For this purpose, an algorithm for calculating the dispersion characteristics of a double-sided microstrip transmission line was developed and implemented in software, considering the singularity of the field at the edges of the strip. The characteristic of the reflection coefficient of the fundamental wave of the microstrip line was calculated using an approximate formula by numerical integration. According to the results of the transition analysis, for the exponent indices depending on the strip width from the longitudinal coordinate n = 0.5 − 1.5, the reflection coefficient value does not exceed the attenuation level of −20 dB. The results of numerical simulation of the tapered transition with different exponent n = 0.5; 1; 1.5 showed good agreement with the results of the calculation of the scattering characteristics, and the transition is well matched in the frequency band 1 − 6 GHz.

title: SmartGuard: A Machine Learning Based Classification Framework for Automated DoS/DDoS Defense in Next-Generation Firewall Systems
authors: Santosha Pullagura, Nitin Pandita, Aman Kakwani, Santhosh Appaji Gowda, Kundhavai Kumaripalayam Rajamani
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: This paper proposes SmartGuard, a machine learning-based classification framework for DDoS detection and mitigation in next-generation firewalls. The system addresses the challenge of detecting evolving distributed denial-of-service attacks in real-time network environments. We present a practical implementation using Isolation Forest that achieves 83.2% overall detection accuracy with a 4.2% false positive rate. The framework incorporates a dynamic concept drift handling mechanism that enables continuous model adaptation as network traffic patterns evolve. Additionally, an auto-learn mode allows SmartGuard to identify and mitigate novel attack patterns without manual intervention. Evaluation using a custom attack simulation framework demonstrates protocol-specific detection rates of 85.1% for ICMP floods, 82.8% for UDP floods, and 81.9% for SYN floods. Results validate SmartGuard's effectiveness for real-world network deployments.

title: An Additive Affine Log-Distance Signal Model with Split-Conformal Uncertainty for Cellular Network Measurements
authors: Keerin Nopanitaya
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: An Additive Affine Log-Distance Signal (AALDS) is presented for predicting received signal strength (dBm) from distance-to-tower (km), SNR, and attenuation (dB) while quantifying uncertainty through split-conformal prediction. The method is evaluated on a public cellular performance dataset. The training set contains 347 observations and the test set contains 116 observations (N=463). The linear predictor achieves MAE = 11.815 dB, RMSE =14.905 dB, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=0.055 on the test set, with Pearson r=0.0804 between predictions and observations. A 90% split-conformal interval delivers empirical coverage =87.07% (101 / 116) with mean width =43.927dB, obtained from the calibration quantile q<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.9</inf>=21.963 dB. Residuals on the test set have mean 0.236 dB, standard deviation 15.033 dB, minimum -29.076 dB, and maximum 35.666 dB. The fitted coefficients indicate positive association with SNR and negative association with attenuation, while log-distance alone is weakly associated in this dataset. Results are reproducible with closed-form estimators and fully specified parameters.

title: A Method for Dynamic Graph Weight Adaptation in IoV Networks Using Fuzzy-Based Exponential Smoothing
authors: Andrii Liashenko, Larysa Globa
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: Accurate and stable edge weights are critical for real-time routing and traffic optimization in Internet-of-Vehicles (IoV) systems. However, vehicle telemetry—speed, density and acceleration—naturally contains high-frequency fluctuations and noise, causing instability in graph-based traffic models and degrading routing performance. Existing solutions typically rely on fixed-parameter exponential smoothing, Kalman filtering, or offline traffic models. These approaches assume predefined noise characteristics or stationary dynamics, making them insufficient for rapidly changing IoV environments where traffic regimes evolve unpredictably. To address this gap, this paper introduces a data-driven method for real-time edge-weight adaptation based on phase-space clustering and fuzzy exponential smoothing. Incoming traffic samples are mapped into a three-dimensional phase vector (efficiency, load factor, trend) and grouped using an adaptive micro/macro-clustering mechanism running in streaming mode. The key contribution is a hybrid Takagi–Sugeno fuzzy inference model, where each statistically learned macro-cluster forms a fuzzy rule whose consequent defines a context-dependent smoothing coefficient α. This enables strong noise suppression in stable traffic regimes and fast reaction when conditions become unstable or congested. The proposed approach is evaluated on a realistic IoV simulation built on the road network of Irpin, Ukraine, using 25,000-sample Kafka batches.

title: Cattle Detection and Pose Localization Using Computer Vision for Northern Thai Farms
authors: Myint Myat, Ant Maw Khon, Pradorn Sureephong, Santichai Wicha
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: Precision livestock farming in Thailand improves reproductive efficiency and animal welfare while managing labor and environmental variability. In smallholder beef and dairy operations, behaviors related to estrus and welfare (e.g., mounting, restlessness, feeding/drinking changes) are difficult to observe consistently under backlight, dust, and crowded pens, motivating a reliable detection-first vision pipeline. Fixed CCTV is common and affordable; translating continuous video into actionable metrics requires a robust computer-vision pipeline. We develop a deployable detection-first base-line based on DeepLabCut (DLC) with an HRNet-W32 backbone, trained on approximately 300 labeled frames from real barn footage in Northern Thailand using DeepLabCut. The pipeline consists of frame acquisition, preprocessing, DLC detection, and non-maximum suppression with confidence filtering; no temporal tracking was applied in this phase. Under an 80/20 split (shuffle =2) and tuned p<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">cutoff</inf>=0.6, the detector attains 71.02% test mean Average Precision (mAP) and 76.19% mean Average Recall (mAR). Qualitative outputs in feeder, alley, and trough zones demonstrate stable localization on collected barn footage in daylight with partial occlusion. The detection layer supports zone-level counts and dwell, establishes a curated Thai barn dataset and training recipe, and creates a direct path to pose estimation and temporal action recognition for mounting, feeding, and drinking events. We discuss optimization, deployment considerations, and limitations, providing a step-by-step template for extension to estrus analytics.

title: Eigenmodes of Photonic Crystal Waveguides Based on the Kagome Lattice
authors: Eugene Odarenko, Yuriy Averkov, Liubov Ivzhenko, Sergiy Yuhno, Yevhen Sulima, Elena Linnyk
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: This work considers eigenmode numerical analysis of two-dimensional photonic crystal waveguides based on the kagome lattice. Guided modes are investigated using the plane wave expansion method. It is shown that kagome photonic crystal waveguides support not only bandgap guided modes, but also modes due to inhibited coupling between guided core modes and cladding ones. If the waveguide cladding does not support photonic band gaps, then the guiding modes of the structure are limited to inhibited coupling modes.

title: Developing Functional Competencies for Human Resource Officers Using the Knowledge Engineering Framework
authors: Maytiya Siroros, Pitipong Yodmongkol, Suepphong Chernbumroong
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: The shift toward digital economy has intensified the need for workforce re-skilling and up-skilling. Although Chiang Mai University aims to strengthen its human capital in response to these changes, it has lacked a clear and systematic competency framework to guide staff development by job position. This study addresses the gap by applying the CommonKADS framework, which provides a structured approach for capturing and organizing tacit and explicit expert knowledge into practical competency models. Using the CommonKADS methodology across the Context, Concept, and Artifact Levels, the study extracted expert knowledge through iterative interviews, constructed the Knowledge Model, and validated the resulting functional competencies for the Human Resource Officer position. The process generated a competency structure grounded in real work practices, and the validation stages showed strong acceptance from practitioners and institutional stakeholders. These findings highlight the value of Knowledge Engineering for developing accurate, contextspecific competencies that support organizational capability and strategic workforce development.

title: Fashion E-Commerce Data Science for MSMEs: A Seller Performance Analysis in the Women's Apparel Sector
authors: Susi Hartanto
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: This study investigates e-commerce and seller performance insights in women apparel category using a case study, utilizing seller's data collected from March to September 2024. The research employs a descriptive analysis approach from actual seller data. There are five analyses discussed in this article, including traffic insights, GMV (Gross Merchandise Value) insights, customer demographics, product demographics, ratings and reviews. Details like average GMV per order, click-through rate (CTR), conversion rate (CR), customer repurchase rate, basket size, order return rate, return reasons, top price bands, top age groups, repeat customers, customer operating systems, ratings and reviews, and many others are all revealed by the analysis. Based on data analysis, some recommendations are made to help seller improve their performance. This article aims to provide useful knowledge to help stakeholders especially MSMEs (Micro Small and Medium Enterprises) who may find difficulties understanding key indicators of e-commerce performance, as a guide to create better e-business strategies.

title: Technique for Quantitative Assessment of Minimal Cut Sets in Mission-Critical Systems with Repair
authors: Leonid Ozirkovsky, Bohdan Volochiy, Bohdan Husyak, Ihor Kulyk
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: This paper is devoted to the analysis of the safety of mission-critical systems (MCS) with recovery. The article proposes a methodology for determining the quantitative values of failure functions, which are analogous to minimal cut sets. Unlike minimal cut sets, which can only be quantitatively assessed at a specific point in the time interval of system operation, failure functions allow quantitative values to be obtained over the entire operating interval. The developed methodology is based on an improved state space method, which, unlike static fault tree analysis, allows for accurate modelling of system dynamics, taking into account both failure rates (λ) and recovery rates (μ). The methodology is illustrated by the example of evaluating the quantitative values of MCS safety indicators with a "2 out of 3" majority structure with constant recovery. The results of the research were validated using the fault tree analysis method, which demonstrated the reliability of the results obtained.

title: Graph Neural Network Approaches to LDPC Decoding in 5G/6G Communications
authors: Juliy Boiko, Ilya Pyatin, Volodymir Druzhynin
publish: 2026 IEEE 18th International Conference on Advanced Trends in Radioelectronics, Telecommunications and Computer Engineering (TCSET)
abstract: This paper investigates Graph Neural Network (GNN)–based decoding of Low-Density Parity-Check (LDPC) codes for fifth- and sixth-generation (5G/6G) communication systems. Classical decoding algorithms, such as Belief Propagation (BP) and Min-Sum (MS), perform well under ideal conditions but degrade in the presence of channel fading, noise, and hardware nonlinearities. GNN-based decoders exploit the Tanner graph structure of LDPC codes, replacing fixed message-passing rules with trainable neural layers, including Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs), Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. Simulation results obtained using benchmark and 5G New Radio (NR) LDPC codes over Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) and Rayleigh fading channels demonstrate that GNN decoders achieve improved Bit Error Rate (BER) and Block Error Rate (BLER) performance, mitigate error floor effects, and reduce decoding latency and computational complexity compared with BP, MS, and Normalized Min-Sum (NMS) decoders. Consequently, GNN-based LDPC decoding emerges as an adaptive and efficient solution for ultra-reliable low-latency communication in next-generation wireless networks.

title: Envicity Board Game: Tool for Environmental Awareness
authors: Kittipan Seejanphong, Banphot Nobaew, Thanpahtt Chaichombhoo, Pruet Putjorn
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: The Envicity Board Game is an educational and interactive board game designed to promote environmental awareness and understanding of various environmental issues facing modern society. The game incorporates key themes such as deforestation, wildlife trafficking, marine pollution, air pollution, and climate change, with players taking on the roles of both protectors of nature and environmental threats. Through role-playing, strategic decision-making, and teamwork, players are encouraged to learn about environmental conservation, the consequences of human actions on ecosystems, and methods of sustainable living. The game mechanics emphasise cause-and-effect relationships, encouraging players to think critically and reflect on realworld environmental challenges. Designed for players aged 13 and above, Envicity fosters a cooperative and engaging learning experience that blends entertainment with education. This quasi-experimental study with 78 participants demonstrates significant improvements in environmental knowledge and attitudes following gameplay (knowledge: Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d=0.61$</tex>; attitudes: mean Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d=1.15$</tex>). The project not only aims to provide a recreational tool but also serves as a supplementary medium for environmental education in schools, communities, and organisations.

title: Development of an AI-Based System for Transcribing Northeastern Thai Speech into Standard Thai Text
authors: Apichut Sirigaya, Aurawan Imsombut
publish: 2026 Joint International Conference on Digital Arts, Media and Technology with ECTI Northern Section Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Computer and Telecommunication Engineering (ECTI DAMT &amp; NCON)
abstract: The Isan dialect, spoken widely across Northeastern Thailand, differs considerably from Standard Thai in both sound patterns and vocabulary. These differences often hinder the development of speech-processing tools that rely on large, standardized datasets. This study develops a system capable of converting spoken Isan into Standard Thai text, aiming to address the shortage of paired speech and text data for this dialect. Two methodological paths were examined. The first involved fine-tuning three existing Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) models: Whisper-Large-V3, XLSR-Large, and Thonburian, using 19 hours of aligned Isan audio with Standard Thai transcriptions. The second approach adopted a two-step pipeline: Isan speech was first transcribed into written Isan using a fine-tuned Thonburian ASR model, and the resulting text was then translated into Standard Thai using an mT5 model. The experimental comparison indicates that transfer learning helps the models handle dialectal variation effectively. Among all models tested, the adapted fine-tuning Thonburian ASR achieved the strongest overall performance, with lower WER and CER than the pipeline method and a semantic similarity score of 4.60 out of 5, compared to the answer. These results suggest that targeted model adaptation can meaningfully enhance transcription quality for low-resource dialects and may support future work on Thai speech technologies involving regional language varieties.

title: Retraction Notice: A CBRD Technique for Energy Efficient Bandwidth Image Sharing
authors: P.Kavitha Rani, K.S. Madhuaravindh, S. Praneet, M. Jagadeep
publish: 2021 7th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
abstract: Conventional methods for picture recovery are not upheld for the consistently far reaching picture information base. These drawbacks can be eliminated by using substance of the picture for picture retrieval. Such a picture recovery is called as Cross Batch Redundancy Detection (CBRD). Honey bees is works with CBRD is engaged around the visual highlights like shape, shading and surface. The Density-Bandwidth Energy Efficient Sharing(BEES) is a stand apart among the most locally highlight indicator and descriptors which is used as a piece of most of the vision programming. We center texture, color, shape, size, string based picture coordinating with better precision. These highlights incorporate Texture, Color, Shape and Region. It is a hot exploration zone and specialists have created numerous methods to utilize these element for precise recovery of required pictures from the information bases. In this paper we present a writing study of the Cross Batch Redundancy Detection (CBRD) procedures dependent on Texture, Color, Shape and Region. We likewise survey a portion of the cutting edge apparatuses created for CBRD.

title: Retraction Notice: Machine Learning in IoT Security Performance Analysis of Outage Probability of link selection for Cognitive Networks
authors: R.Ganesh Babu, A. Nedumaran, Asefa Sisay
publish: 2019 Third International conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC)
abstract: The future web of things architecture will have an inexpensive, industrial and social impact on our exist. The IoT network unit nodes usually resource in an unnatural way by making them more prone to cyber attacks. Throughout this regard, network standards were advanced to augment all outturn of 2 coinciding underlay optional systems that utilize the range of a dispatched essential system. Optimal systems are required either to transmit simultaneously by distributing the Interference Temperature Limit (ITL), or in separation. The in-depth efforts are created to switch the protection and privacy issues in IoT networks through the ancient science approach. This research paper has consistently reviewed the protection needs, attack vectors, and also the current security solutions incorporated in the IoT networks. This research paper will additionally discuss the prevailing cc and metric capacity unit solutions for addressing the totally different security problems in IoT networks. Finally, it derives a method to maintain the elaborated examination of the established solutions within the literature and additionally it has a facility to discuss the longer term analysis instructions used for ML- and DL-based IoT security.

title: An Ankle-Driven Proprioceptive Rehabilitation Robot During Gait: Design, Control, and Evaluation
authors: Liziyi Hao, Zhaocheng Zhou, Honghao Zheng, Xiangyu Wang, Jianda Han, Ningbo Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: Perturbation-based training is essential for improving proprioception and reducing fall risk, yet many robotic platforms emphasize sagittal-plane assistance or apply three-dimensional perturbations via trunk, and configurable multidirectional perturbations directly at the foot-ankle interface during walking are still lacking. In this study, we present a robotic system for proprioceptive training that programmatically delivers plantar perturbations in multiple directions during gait. The system integrates a 10-DoF series-parallel hybrid mechanism, an immersive visual interface, and a suite of task modes to reproduce unstable terrains and generate controlled perturbations. To ensure stable operation, a composite control method combining iterative learning with neural-network disturbance estimation was designed, and reduced the trajectory-tracking errors by over 60% in comparison with PID and sliding mode control. Experiments involving ten healthy participants were conducted under mediolateral perturbations and sEMG-based muscle synergy analysis revealed training-induced neuromuscular adaptations, marked by the progressive activation of key frontal-plane balance muscles - gluteus medius, tensor fasciae latae, and peroneus longus - and a reorganization of task-specific synergies under higher perturbation levels. These findings demonstrate that the elaborated proprioceptive training with controlled perturbations drives neuromuscular adaptation, highlighting the potential of the proposed robotic system as a viable tool for balance rehabilitation.

title: Experimental Assessment of the Dielectric and Physicochemical Performance of Liquid Insulation in Retrofilled Transformers
authors: Andrés Montero, Rashed Al-Tarawneh, Taskin Aman, Belén García, Néstor García-Hernando
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The retrofilling of transformers with alternative oils is becoming an industrial practice to enhance the system reliability. However, the technical implications of residual mineral oil, particularly when either oil is aged, remain insufficiently explored. This paper presents a comprehensive study on the physicochemical and dielectric characteristics of oil mixtures in proportions typical of retrofilled units. A distinctive feature of this research is the evaluation of mixtures incorporating oils with different levels of degradation, providing a realistic representation of in-service transformers. AC breakdown voltage, partial discharge inception voltage, permittivity, tangent delta, acidity, density and viscosity, were characterized under both fresh and aging conditions. The experimental results reveal that most physicochemical properties change linearly with the MO mixing ratio. Dielectrically, small amounts of MO do not compromise the system’s integrity in fresh conditions; however, aging induces a critical reduction in PDIV, highlighting a potential risk for long-term operation.

title: SAMamba: Stream Alignment Mamba for Motion Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Yi Sui, Xiyang Zhi, Yuanxin Huang, Tianjun Shi, Shikai Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: The key to infrared small target detection lies in dis-tinguishing targets from backgrounds for accurate extraction. However, long-distance imaging with low angular resolution causes morphology loss and weak saliency, making targets easily submerged in complex backgrounds. As a result, achieving high-probability, low-false-alarm detection solely in the spatial domain is challenging. To address this, we extend to the spatiotemporal domain and propose Stream Alignment Mamba for Motion In-frared Small Target Detection (SAMamba). Specifically, we first design a Stream Mamba Block based on a dynamic state space model, which introduces bidirectional spatiotemporal scanning to explore inter-frame relationships in sequential images, not only preserving spatial correlations but also leveraging the causality of temporal cues for comprehensive global background representa-tion. Second, we develop a Cross-Frame Feature Enhancement Block to implicitly align feature streams by computing local re-sponses between neighboring and reference frames, thereby strengthening weak target features. In addition, we devise a Hi-erarchical Feature Alignment Block with adaptive adjustment strategies and position-aware mechanisms to address spatial mis-alignment during upsampling, thus enhancing the effectiveness of multi-scale target fusion and ultimately improving detection ac-curacy. Extensive experiments on IRDST, IRSatVideo-LEO, and TSIRMT datasets demonstrate that our method achieves signifi-cant advantages over state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches.

title: Time-Division SQUID Multiplexing With Nanohenry Scale Input Mutual Inductance Using Transformers With Intra-Coil Damping
authors: Malcolm Durkin, Douglas Bennett, William B. Doriese, Johnathon D. Gard, Johannes Hubmayr, Richard Lew, Erin Maloney, Carl D. Reintsema, Christopher Rooney, Robinjeet Singh, Daniel R. Schmidt, Joel N. Ullom, Leila R. Vale, Michael R. Vissers
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Time division multiplexing (TDM) developed for the Athena X-ray integral field unit (X-IFU) has achieved readout performance that can be scaled to multiplexing factors of several hundred for TES bolometer missions. The lower slew rate requirements of bolometer and soft X-ray missions favor greater first stage SQUID amplifier input mutual inductance (Min) to reduce readout noise and compensate for aliasing as multiplexing factor is increased. We find that, as Min increases, TDM performance becomes sensitive to the design of the TES bias circuit, with degraded noise performance and the emergence of resonant features. In TDM chips with nanohenry scale Min, we demonstrate that this sensitivity can be eliminated by adding intra-coil damping resistors to the superconducting transformers, enabling the robust use of these higher Min multiplexers.

title: Corrections to “Distributional Treatment of Real2Sim2Real for Object-Centric Agent Adaptation in Vision-Driven DLO Manipulation”
authors: Georgios Kamaras, Subramanian Ramamoorthy
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: In the present article, we wish to correct three minor typographic issues of our recent publication [1]. These issues affect only the referenced algorithm lines and figures, and have no influence on the methodology, discussions, and conclusions of our paper. The corrected elements are given here along with an explanation of the corresponding amendments.

title: Masthead
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Antennas and Propagation Magazine

title: Design of a Low-Cost Power Quality Analyzer with LoRa-WAN Telemetry for Rural Smart Grid Applications
authors: Kélton Da Rosa Severo, Natalia Braun Chagas, José Wagner M. Kaehler, Crístian Müller, Alessandro G. Girardi, Paulo César C. De Aguirre
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a low-cost, high-performance power quality analyzer (PQA) tailored for rural electrical installations in Brazil. The system integrates real-time measurement capabilities with telemetry via LoRa-WAN, enabling long-range data acquisition in remote areas. Developed using an ESP32 microcontroller, the device performs non-invasive monitoring of key power quality parameters — including voltage, current, frequency, harmonics, and power factor —while complying with the Brazilian PRODIST Module 8 and IEC 61000-4-30 standards. A LUT-based calibration method was implemented to overcome ADC non-linearity, significantly improving measurement accuracy. The system features a graphical user interface (GUI), onboard data storage, and a web-based dashboard powered by Firebase for remote access and analysis. Experimental validation demonstrates the device accuracy, reliability, and suitability for smart grid applications in cost-sensitive and infrastructure-limited environments. The proposed solution offers a scalable and affordable alternative to commercial analyzers, supporting national campaigns for power quality assessment and rural electrification.

title: Movable-Antenna-Assisted Dual-Hop FSO/RF Space-Air-Ground Networks With Underlay Spectrum Sharing
authors: Zain Ali, Saud Althunibat, Rula Ammuri, Mazen Omar Hasna, Khalid Qaraqe
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper investigates a movable-antenna-assisted dual-hop space-air-ground non-terrestrial network (NTN) operating under an underlay spectrum sharing paradigm. A satellite communicates with multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) over free-space optical (FSO) links, while each UAV simultaneously serves a cluster of ground users over radio-frequency (RF) channels subject to interference constraints imposed by a primary receiver. To efficiently exploit the complementary advantages of FSO and RF transmission and the additional spatial degrees of freedom offered by movable antennas, a joint optimization framework is developed to maximize the system sum rate. The proposed framework jointly optimizes satellite and UAV power allocation, multi-antenna precoding at the UAVs, and the positions of multiple movable antennas mounted on each UAV. An alternating-optimization algorithm is employed, where minimum mean square error (MMSE) based precoding accounts for both communication and interference channels, power allocation is convexified using auxiliary rate variables and successive convex approximation, and antenna locations are optimized via Taylor-based convex surrogates. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly outperforms benchmark schemes with fixed antenna locations, heuristic optimization and reinforcement learning methods, while providing robust performance across different system parameters.

title: Privacy-Preserving Revocable Certificateless Cloud Data Auditing Scheme Based on IoT Data Collection for Secure Medical Data Sharing
authors: Xu An Wang, Zhongqiang Liu, Wenhao Liu, Weiwei Jiang, Wenjun Jiang, Yangyu Li
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: With the advent of the 5G era, online healthcare has rapidly developed, and medical efficiency has been greatly enhanced through medical data sharing. However, as cloud services are not fully trusted, users who have lost physical control of their data urgently require a provable data possession scheme to regularly verify the integrity of their outsourced data. Moreover, the scheme must protect the user’s real identity and the security of the data content. To meet these needs while overcoming issues such as complex certificate management, high operational costs, and the key escrow problem, scholars have proposed many certificateless provable data possession schemes. Among them, we analyze the ReCIP scheme which supports privacy preservation and efficient revocation, identify its limitations, and propose an enhanced ReCIP+ scheme tailored for medical data sharing. Under the assumption that the discrete logarithm problem and the computational Diffie–Hellman problem are hard, we formally prove the correctness and security of the ReCIP+ scheme. Furthermore, comparative evaluation demonstrates that the ReCIP+ scheme offers improved security and relatively lower computational costs compared to existing schemes.

title: A Quantum-Enhanced Key Agreement and Signature Protocol for Securing Transportation Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Sahaya Beni Prathiba, Saikiran Sankaranarayanan, Rampriya Rajendran Shanthi, Dhanalakshmi Ranganayakulu, Arikumar K. Selvaraj, Joel J. P. C. Rodrigues
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Transportation Cyber-Physical Systems (T-CPS) integrate transportation information with physical elements, enabling advanced features such as real-time vehicle tracking, collision avoidance, and intelligent traffic management. However, this also increases the need for improved security protocols to safeguard the critical identity-based data they transmit through vulnerable wireless networks, such as Vehicle Identification Numbers (VINs) and live Global Positioning System (GPS) coordinates. These T-CPS, traditionally protected by classical encryption algorithms, are now vulnerable due to the rise in quantum computing. Existing solutions are hindered by their complex security features and high computational overhead. To address these challenges, we propose Quantum Key Agreement and Signature Verification (QKASV), a post-quantum protocol that integrates Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and Quantum Signatures for T-CPS. QKASV uses identity-tied QKD-based keys for session creation, followed by either individual or group quantum signatures. Signatures are created by applying basic quantum gates sequentially rather than lattice-based or certificate-based schemes. Hence, this speeds up the overall key generation, distribution, and management process without compromising the enhanced security. Formal security analysis using standard security models and the Scyther cryptographic protocol verification tool proves that QKASV meets standard security requirements. Further, comparison with similar schemes shows that QKASV operates on the least communication rounds required per signature, and reduces the computational overhead by at least 12%. Therefore, QKASV offers a better security solution compared to existing schemes.

title: Adaptive Visual-Tactile Fusion for Contact-Rich Dexterous Manipulation
authors: Zedong Cai, Chuan Liu, Siyuan Li, Peng Liu
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Effective dexterous manipulation hinges on the dynamic integration of visual context with fine-grained tactile feedback. This remains a significant challenge, as existing methods often rely on static fusion strategies and struggle to learn from isolated tactile features. To address this, we propose a hardware-decoupled tactile representation that learns cross-finger spatial features by training a sparse Transformer on unified tactile images, enabling cross-device generalization. Furthermore, we introduce a tactile-activity-guided adaptive visual-tactile fusion mechanism that dynamically adjusts the influence of vision and touch, showing the contribution of touch feedback upon physical contact. We evaluate our method on a series of contact-rich manipulation tasks requiring fine force control. Experimental results show that our method has an average success rate of over 90%, demonstrating effectiveness compared to other methods. Further analysis shows that adaptive multimodal fusion is essential to complete the dexterous manipulation tasks. The videos can be viewed at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://avtf-dex.github.io</uri>.

title: A Systematic Review of Soft Respiratory Exoskeletons: Assistance Mechanisms, System Architectures and Key Technologies
authors: Yan Zhang, Dinghao Xue, Bin Zhao, Mai Shi, Xiaolu Nan, Yingheng Zhao, Qinggang Ge, Yuru Zhang, Zongyu Wang, Yanggang Feng, Dangxiao Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Biometric exoskeletons have drawn extensive research interest for augmenting human motor capabilities, particularly in upper- and lower-limb assistance. However, their potential for respiratory support remains significantly underexplored. This review investigates the emerging concept of respiratory robots by examining the similarities and differences between assistive robots designed for motor augmentation and those intended for respiratory assistance. Building on respiratory physiology, the paper outlines the assistive mechanisms of existing respiratory robots and identifies key enabling technologies—including soft actuation methods, respiratory intention recognition and human-in-the-loop control—as well as clinical evaluation. Finally, the review provides a systematic overview of the current research landscape and highlights promising directions for future development in respiratory assistive robot.

title: Improved Quantum Cryptanalysis of MD5 and SHA-1
authors: Kyungbae Jang, Hwajeong Seo, Anupam Chattopadhyay
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: MD5 and SHA-1 are widely studied hash functions in cryptanalysis, owing to their well-structured round functions and historical significance in cryptography. This paper presents improved quantum circuit implementations of MD5 and SHA-1 specifically optimized for quantum cryptanalysis. We reduce the quantum implementation cost of auxiliary functions and eliminate the use of ancilla qubits, while reducing circuit depth (the number of sequential gate layers) by parallelizing multiple additions in the round operations of MD5 and SHA-1. Additionally, the quantum implementations of MD5 and SHA-1 are optimized by considering a fixed input size under the attack scenario. The proposed implementations achieve a 63% improvement in the depth - qubit count metric for MD5 and a 27% improvement for SHA-1 compared to previous works. By providing reduced quantum resources, this work establishes an improved baseline for the quantum cryptanalysis of MD5 and SHA-1. We analyze quantum attacks on MD5 and SHA-1, showing that the preimage attacks require a full depth of 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">79</sup> and 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">96</sup> for MD5 and SHA-1, respectively, while the collision attacks require a full depth of 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">54</sup> and 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">64</sup>. For a realistic evaluation, we also estimate the quantum resources for fault-tolerant quantum computation, including the number of physical qubits and the runtime, to break MD5 and SHA-1.

title: Miniature Quadrotor Altitude Controller Design Methods: Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System and Explicit Model Predictive Control
authors: Min-hyuk Jeong, Hyuk-jun Chang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Advances in micro electro mechanical systems technology have facilitated the ongoing miniaturization of unmanned aerial vehicles, thereby enabling their use as platforms for repetitive and precise tasks, e.g., inspection, monitoring, and sensing in confined indoor environments. However, indoor environments impose physical and spatial constraints, making the design of altitude controllers with high accuracy and reliability essential for stable flight. Model predictive control (MPC) provides systematic constraint-handling capability; however, the conventional MPC method requires online optimization at each sampling instant, which can impose a significant computational burden for miniature quadrotor platforms. Explicit MPC (eMPC) addresses this issue by precomputing the optimal control law offline and storing it as piecewise affine functions defined over critical regions, thereby eliminating online optimization processes. However, eMPC can still incur computational and memory overhead due to region-search operations and the growth of critical regions. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference system (ANFIS)-based altitude control scheme that learns the offline eMPC laws. First, an eMPC controller is designed, and the piecewise affine control laws defined over its critical regions are employed to construct training data for the ANFIS controller. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed ANFIS controller reduces computational complexity and achieves tracking performance that is comparable to that of both MPC and eMPC. The proposed controller is evaluated on a real-world Parrot Mambo platform through indoor altitude flight experiments. For step, varying-altitude, and sinusoidal reference tracking scenarios, the proposed ANFIS controller is compared with conventional proportional-integral-derivative and linear quadratic regulator controllers. Experimental results indicate that the proposed ANFIS controller generates smoother control signals and achieves stable tracking performance under sensor noise conditions.

title: Towards a systematic review of artificial intelligence-based models for predicting academic success in higher education
authors: Meryem Ziz, Sophia Faris, Khalifa Mansouri
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In the context of the rapid expansion of learning analytics, data mining and artificial intelligence in education, predicting academic performance has become a major field of research, mobilizing approaches derived from machine learning. This systematic review of the literature examines the evolution of prediction models for academic performance in higher education over the period 2016-2025. In accordance with PRISMA guidelines, 165 empirical studies were analyzed to identify the dominant prediction models, the most decisive predictive variables, and the degree of rigor of the validation protocols. The results reveal a marked shift towards machine learning and deep learning models, with a predominance of ensemble models and deep architecture. Academic and behavioral variables remain central, whereas sociodemographic and psychological factors make an explanatory contribution. However, methodological limitations persist, particularly in terms of sample representativeness, model validation, and transparency. These shortcomings limit the generalizability and reproducibility of the results. Finally, the review highlights the need for more rigorous and explainable approaches in the design of predictive models, paving the way for a more efficient use of artificial intelligence in higher education.

title: HiFAKES: Synthetic Data for NILM Model Selection and Generalization Testing
authors: Ilia Kamyshev, Sahar Moghimian Hoosh, Henni Ouerdane
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM) breaks down household consumption to appliance-level, providing detailed insights into end-user electricity behavior using a single meter. NILM algorithms are typically trained on a household’s total power consumption paired with submetered appliance labels. When sampled at high frequencies (≥1 kHz), these datasets capture the full waveform characteristics, which significantly improves disaggregation accuracy and model generalization. Nevertheless, such datasets are scarce, collected from a limited number of households, and rarely include labels for power estimation, which complicates their use for model evaluation or diagnosis. To overcome these limitations, we propose HiFAKES, a pre-trained synthetic data generator that can instantly generate unlimited amounts of fully labeled high-frequency NILM data, including aggregate and submetered current signatures. Unlike prior works that use synthetic data for training, our focus is on using it for NILM model selection and generalization testing. Our approach allows simulating both seen and completely unseen scenarios of appliances’ behavior with full control over parameters such as the number of appliance classes, operational modes, class similarity, brand diversity, and the number of concurrently running devices. The complete training of HiFAKES on 10,000 real cycles takes 0.01 seconds on a single CPU core (Intel Core i7-14700K), and 100,000 synthetic cycles are generated in 0.18 seconds. A physics-informed active power and power factor constraint guarantees physical validity of all generated signatures by construction. Using HiFAKES, we propose a structured methodology to test the generalization of four NILM models (two classical and two deep learning) on simulated households with novel appliances and brands. The reliability of the synthetic data is assessed using 13 metrics that cover fidelity, diversity, and novelty, and is compared against three reference generators (PIASG, Edframe, WGAN-GP). HiFAKES achieves the best novelty-fidelity trade-off among all evaluated generators: geometric novelty of 37% at integrated α-precision of 95%, with complete coverage of the real data distribution, and the novelty can be further increased to 42% while keeping fidelity above 66%.

title: A High-Angular Misalignment Tolerance Wireless Power Transfer System Based on Primary-Side Control
authors: Meng Wang, Cheng Peng, Yanyan Shi, Jing Feng, Lin Yang, Qi Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: In this paper, a high-angular misalignment tolerance wireless power transfer (WPT) system based on primary-side control is proposed. Two orthogonal DD coils are adopted as the transmitting coils for the cross-coupling suppression, and each of the coils is driven by one inverter independently. To achieve a stable output against misalignments, the pre-trained echo state neural network (ESN) is proposed to dynamically adjust the phase-shift angle of the two inverters based on the identified coupling states of each transmitting coil and the receiving coil. The control strategy is carried out at the primary side, and the communication is omitted. The transfer characteristics of the system are analysed, and the judgment criterion of coupling states is provided. It is proven that the current ratio of the inverter outputs can be used to judge the coupling state. With the proposed system, both the constant voltage (CV) and the efficient power transfer are maintained in various angular misalignment modes and cases. An 810 W WPT prototype is developed. Experimental results verify that the proposed system can not only transfer stable power efficiently over a wide range of angular misalignment, with a maximum efficiency of 90.9% and efficiency maintained around 90% over the full angular range in Mode 1, but also maintain constant output voltage under extreme misalignment (when the receiving coil couples with only a transmitting coil).

title: A Wide-Output-Range High-Efficiency Wireless Power Transfer System Using Novel Cuk-Based Single-Stage Partial Power Conversion Architecture
authors: Muxing Wu, Io-Wa Iam, Zhicong Huang, Chi-Seng Lam
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a wireless power transfer system employing a novel Cuk-based single-stage partial power conversion architecture that simultaneously ensures wide-range output regulation and high power transfer efficiency. In the proposed topology, the Cuk circuit's input terminals are cascaded between the rectifier bridge arms. During positive halfcycles of the secondary current is, power flows directly to the load, while during negative half-cycles, a precisely controlled switch determines the fraction of power subjected to regulation. Herein, this work categorizes the operation of the proposed system into three distinct PPC operations based on the extent to which the secondary current participates in power regulation. Notably, the proposed system achieves multiple times the voltage output range while offering superior efficiency. Moreover, the proposed singlestage system enables both PPC and galvanic isolation without requiring additional devices for power separation, thus reducing component count and overall cost. Finally, a 1 kW experimental prototype was developed with an output voltage range of 100 V to 400 V. The system achieved a maximum efficiency of 94.88%, and comprehensive transient and misalignment experiments were conducted to verify the feasibility and advantages of the proposed topology.

title: Enhancing the Capability and Accuracy of Motor Imagery Classification: A Deep Neural Network-Powered Multifaceted Strategy Model
authors: Weijie Chen, Ian Daly, Yixin Chen, Xiao Wu, Wei Liang, Xinjie He, Xingyu Wang, Andrzej Cichocki, Jing Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: Motor imagery (MI) is a popular noninvasive brain computer interface (BCI) paradigm, yet its decoding accuracy remains hindered by the inherent nonstationarity and low signal-to-noise ratio of electroencephalogram (EEG) signals. Current decoding frameworks often fail to fully exploit the intricate spatial-temporal dependencies, leading to suboptimal feature representation and the omission of latent discriminative cues. To address these challenges, we introduce a deep neural network-powered multifaceted strategy (DPMS-Net) model, a novel approach that employs dynamic convolution to unearth effective discriminative cues across multiple dimensions, including the temporal, spatial, and frequency domains. This model synergizes channel and temporal attention mechanisms to adeptly capture the salient features of EEG signals across diverse spatial-temporal dimensions, thereby mitigating the risk of omitting critical information. Furthermore, we introduce a spectral-domain analysis component that unearths subtle oscillatory signatures hidden within the EEG spectrum, providing enriched evidence for classification. We evaluated the performance of DPMS-Net on two publicly available datasets and a self-collected dataset from stroke patients. On the BCI Competition IV 2a and BCI Competition IV 2b datasets, DPMS-Net achieved subject-dependent classification accuracies of 83.93% and 88.38%, respectively, alongside subject-independent classification accuracies of 65.88% and 76.01%. In the stroke patient dataset, DPMS-Net attained a subject-dependent classification accuracy of 67.67% and a subject-independent classification accuracy of 57.58%. Experimental results indicate that DPMS-Net possesses efficient decoding capabilities and robust stability, reflecting its potential for deployment in neurorehabilitation BCI systems.

title: AdaNoise: Cycle-Consistent Image Translation with Domain-Adaptive Noise Perturbation
authors: Xi Yang, Haoyuan Shi, Fei Gao, Nannan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Image-to-image (I2I) translation aims to transform an input image into a target domain while preserving its structural details. Recent advances in diffusion-based generative models have significantly improved the perceptual quality of generated images; however, these approaches still face challenges in controllability and consistency, largely due to the inherent randomness introduced by stochastic noise during the generation process. Specifically, directly manipulating noise distributions without semantic alignment can lead to mode collapse, texture distortion, or loss of domain-specific features. To overcome these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Noise Framework (AdaNoise), a novel and cycle-consistent I2I translation approach guided by domain-adaptive noise modulation. AdaNoise introduces a Domain-Adaptive Noise Perturbation (DANP) module, which adaptively learns structured noise patterns aligned with the target domain distribution, enhancing both the expressiveness and reliability of the translation process. Through integration with a Cycle-Consistent Dual Diffusion (CDD) architecture, AdaNoise ensures faithful content reconstruction while allowing semantically meaningful domain shifts. The framework is designed to maintain a balance between generation quality and controllability, enabling more faithful and flexible image translation. Extensive experiments on two tasks, including SAR-to-optical image translation and low-light image enhancement, validate that AdaNoise not only surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of image fidelity and semantic preservation, but also achieves more controllable and diverse outputs across varying conditions, thus offering a robust and scalable solution for cross-domain visual generation.

title: Burst Mode DSP and Preamble Design for Low Complexity TDMA CPON Upstream Transmission
authors: M. Ahmed Leghari, Ognjen Jovanovic, Gabriele Di Rosa, Martin Kuipers, Jim Zou, Jörg-Peter Elbers, Norbert Hanik
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: We experimentally and numerically demonstrate a 100 Gbit/s burst-mode coherent upstream transmission for Passive Optical Networks (PON), achieving a link budget of 38 dB without optical amplification. The system utilizes a novel 17.1 ns dual-polarization quadrature phase shift keying (DP-QPSK) preamble to enable reliable burst-mode digital signal processing (DSP) convergence. We pose particular emphasis on the robustness of the burst-mode DSP in correcting the frequency offset within the preamble duration. Considering different pulse shapes reveals that rectangular (RECT) pulses outperform root-raised cosine (RRC) shaping in terms of link budget and enable the use of low-complexity transmitter architectures, including 1-bit digital-to-analog converters (DACs). Receiver bandwidth requirements are also investigated, showing that reduced bandwidth can be employed without compromising system performance. Long-term stability of the proposed system is validated through continuous operation over 12 hours and more than 300 burst transmissions, confirming the practical viability of the proposed system for next-generation coherent PON transmission systems.

title: HLIP-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Agile Multigait Biped Locomotion
authors: Yan Liu, Xizhe Zang, Xuehe Zhang, Chao Song, Boyang Chen, Yue Zhang, Jie Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Humans can naturally switch between low-speed walking and high-speed running. For biped robots, this demands both multigait switching capability and high-speed motion performance. Imitation learning can readily generate diverse gaits using human motion data, but it is constrained by dataset quality and dynamic discrepancies between robots and humans, hindering the achievement of robust high-speed running. To address this limitation, we forgo human data and instead adopt a hybrid linear inverted pendulum (HLIP) as a dynamics prior: the HLIP generates nominal footholds and swing-leg trajectories online and in parallel, and these references are combined with reinforcement learning to guide exploration toward high-speed, multigait behaviors that better match the robot’s own dynamics. Specifically, we abstract the robot as a linear inverted pendulum and derive unified walking and running dynamics, yielding an HLIP that captures the key features of both gaits. Based on this model, a foothold planner is designed to generate nominal footholds. Then, a foothold tracking reward, in conjunction with other task-specific reward functions, is employed to guide the robot in exploring more stable high-speed multigait behaviors. We validate our approach on the 0.7-m-tall biped BRUCE, which seamlessly switches between walking and running on varied terrains, reaching 2.5 (simulation) and 1.8 m/s (hardware) top speeds. This hardware speed substantially exceeds the reported 0.6 m/s top speed of comparable sub-1 m bipeds, demonstrating that the learned policy concurrently acquires high-speed locomotion and multigait capabilities.

title: Robust Adaptation of Foundation Models with Black-Box Visual Prompting
authors: Changdae Oh, Gyeongdeok Seo, Geunyoung Jung, Zhi-Qi Cheng, Hosik Choi, Jiyoung Jung, Kyungwoo Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: With a surge of large-scale pre-trained models, parameter-efficient transfer learning (PETL) of large models has garnered significant attention. While promising, they commonly rely on two optimistic assumptions: 1) full access to the parameters of a PTM, and 2) sufficient memory capacity to cache all intermediate activations for gradient computation. However, in most real-world applications, PTMs serve as black-box APIs or proprietary software without full parameter accessibility. Besides, it is hard to meet a large memory requirement for modern PTMs. This work proposes black-box visual prompting (BlackVIP), which efficiently adapts the PTMs without knowledge of their architectures or parameters. BlackVIP has two components: 1) Coordinator and 2) simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation with gradient correction (SPSA-GC). The Coordinator designs input-dependent visual prompts, which allow the target PTM to adapt in the wild. SPSA-GC efficiently estimates the gradient of PTM to update Coordinator. Besides, we introduce a variant, BlackVIP-SE, which significantly reduces the runtime and computational cost of BlackVIP. Extensive experiments on 19 datasets demonstrate that BlackVIPs enable robust adaptation to diverse domains and tasks with minimal memory requirements. We further provide a theoretical analysis on the generalization of visual prompting methods by presenting their connection to the certified robustness of randomized smoothing, and presenting an empirical support for improved robustness.
